EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool pool aloft associated with a large upper level low pressure system located off of the New England coast combined with strong mid-June sunshine to create a favorable environment for thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours on June 12, 2007. A compact upper air disturbance aided in providing large scale ascent as it rotated around the periphery of the upper level vortex . The storm motion was from the northeast to the southwest, which is unusual for summertime convection in Central PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lebanon county Emergency Manager reported trees down in Harper Tavern.

Thunderstorms developed rapidly and produced large hail ranging from nickel to baseball size and damaging winds. Large hail covered the ground in several locations. In addition, hail ripped siding off of homes, put holes in slate roofs, dented hundreds of cars and broke numerous windshields.  Lycoming county emergency management estimated between $300,000 to $400,000 dollars in hail damage. Although some storms exhibited strong rotational signatures and funnel clouds were sighted, no tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported nickel sized hail in Millville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused isolated Flash Flooding in Cambria County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain from thunderstorms triggered a rock slide in Ferndale Borough along Plainfield Avenue. Flash Flooding was also noted along Berkley Road in Upper Yoder Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary across southwest Pennsylvania provided the focus for heavy rain and thunderstorms which produced isolated flash flooding in Cambria County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding which resulted in the closure of Route 22 one mile west of Portage exit. Several basements were also flooded in Conemaugh Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorm complexes moved over Cambria County, causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of slow moving thunderstorms caused flash flooding in the Nanty Glo area. Numerous roads were flooded between Nanty Glo and Twin Rocks, with additional road flooding reported in Blacklick Township. The north branch of Blacklick Creek also came out of it's banks, flooding Route 422, Route 271 and numerous other local roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused flooding and closure of numerous roads near Wilmore and Portage. Numerous reports of basement flooding were also received near Wilmore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary thunderstorm caused isolated flash flooding in Tioga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential thunderstorm rains of up to 2 inches in an hour caused the flooding the northbound lanes of Route 15 about 2 miles south of Blossburg in Tioga County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across upstate New York and northeast Pennsylvania ahead of a slow moving cold front that extended from southern Ontario to lower Michigan. The airmass ahead of this front was warm and unstable, and with the aid of an upper level disturbance, severe thunderstorms developed over the area. Some of the storms produced very large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near route 6 and route 84. Other roads were closed due to trees blocking roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southwesterly flow developed ahead of an approaching cold front. Showers and thunderstorms developed in this warm and unstable environment, and some of the storms reached severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were blown down in Butler Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the central Great Lakes on November 6th.  Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie behind this low caused lake effect snow showers to develop in Northwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers began during the early morning hours of November 6th.  By daybreak, up to 6 inches of snow was reported at Waterford.  The snow continued through the day and tapered off during the evening hours.  Snowfall totals across inland portions of Erie County ranged from 6 to 10 inches.  A maximum of 16.9 inches was reported at Waterford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper brought an early season heavy snowfall event to far eastern portions of the State College CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow from an Alberta Clipper system deposited between 8 and 10 inches of snow over Sullivan County. Snow began during the early morning hours of November 18th, and ended before noon on the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across upstate New York and northeast Pennsylvania ahead of a slow moving cold front that extended from southern Ontario to lower Michigan. The airmass ahead of this front was warm and unstable, and with the aid of an upper level disturbance, severe thunderstorms developed over the area. Some of the storms produced very large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Clifford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure pushed a cold front across the region with heavy rain. Combined with some snow melt this produced flooding across portions of Northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and Greene and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported minor flooding of small streams and roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure pushed a cold front across the region with heavy rain. Combined with some snow melt this produced flooding across portions of Northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and Greene and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported Pursley Creek flooding State Route 301 north of Waynesburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure pushed a cold front across the region with heavy rain. Combined with some snow melt this produced flooding across portions of Northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and Greene and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River flooding along State Route 201 with reports of 2 feet of water  on roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front approaching the Poconos helped generate precipitation on the 20th. While it was warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain, some surface temperatures were still below freezing over the higher terrain. Freezing rain fell from about Midnight EST through 7 a.m. EST on the 20th before temperatures rose above freezing. Ice accretions were less than one-tenth of an inch and untreated roadways were slippery.

A warm front approaching the Poconos helped generate precipitation on the 20th. While it was warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain, some surface temperatures were still below freezing over the higher terrain. Freezing rain fell from about Midnight EST through 7 a.m. EST on the 20th before temperatures rose above freezing. An area of low pressure brought freezing rain with one quarter inch of ice accumulation to parts of Northwest Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland, as low level cold air remained entrenched across these regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported one quarter inch ice accumulations in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure brought freezing rain with one quarter inch of ice accumulation to parts of Northwest Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland, as low level cold air remained entrenched across these regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported one quarter inch of ice accumulations in the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported one quarter inch ice accumulations in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure produced snow and freezing rain across Northern Pennsylvania with heavy snow in Venango county and significant ice accumulation in Clarion county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 6 inches of snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure produced snow and freezing rain across Northern Pennsylvania with heavy snow in Venango county and significant ice accumulation in Clarion county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported one quarter inch of ice accumulation across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 5th that caused lightning damage in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and badly damaged the chimney of a South Whitehall Township home on 34th Street. There was no ensuing fire and no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 5th that caused lightning damage in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a two-and-a-half story barn on the Christman Farm on Balthaser Road in Windsor Township. The ensuing fire gutted the structure. It took volunteers about half an hour to control the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 5th that caused lightning damage in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning rod attached to the steeple of Saint John's Lutheran Church in Boyertown absorbed most of the blast from a lightning strike during the late afternoon on the 5th. Nevertheless, the organ and air conditioning controls, one computer and pulpit microphone were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance in the general northwest flow coupled with daytime heating behind the surface cold front to produce severe weather in Northampton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm ripped the siding from one home and snapped a tree in half in Tatamy Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance in the general northwest flow coupled with daytime heating behind the surface cold front to produce severe weather in Northampton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm peeled away shingles from a 20 foot by 30 foot section of the roof from the Our Lady of Lebanon Maronite Catholic Church in Easton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching warm front helped produce severe thunderstorms and also caused some lightning damage across Eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a stereo to explode in a Tilden Township home. About 1,500 homes and businesses lost power throughout the county due to the combination of damaging winds and lightning strikes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary acted as a focusing mechanism for thunderstorms throughout the day on the 29th in eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike at about 930 a.m. EDT and the ensuing fire badly damaged the Whitehall Township municipal building. Heavy rain that also fell exacerbated the damage. More than 150 firefighters assisted in extinguishing the blaze. The roof of the building (a 20 foot by 30 foot section) partially collapsed and damaged several administrative offices, computers and phones. Some records burned. A state of emergency was declared. Damage to the building was estimated as much as one million dollars. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary acted as a focus for a severe thunderstorm and heavy rain across southeast Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees in Bristol Township. The combination of the wind and lightning caused about 11,500 homes and businesses to lose power in Lower Bucks County. About 700 of those PECO customers did not have power restored until the morning of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes in Barrett Township started separate house and shed fires. No serious injuries were reported. Lightning also struck an unoccupied hot tub in Brodheadsville (Chestnut Hill Township).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the severe weather, heavy rain also accompanied the thunderstorm in and around Bristol Township. A pick-up truck became stuck in flood waters under a railroad bridge in the township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary acted as a focus for a severe thunderstorm and heavy rain across southeastern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in Radnor Township during the afternoon of the 18th. A parking lot in the Wayne section of the township had waist high water. Doppler Radar storm total estimates were around one inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved east across northwestern Pennsylvania on January 30th.  Cold westerly winds behind the front caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the morning hours of the 30th and persist into the 31st.  Some of the heaviest snow fell during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 30th.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed during the morning of the 30th and persisted through the early morning hours of the 31st.  Heavy snow fell during the evening hours of the 30th with snowfall rates in excess of an inch per hour at times.  Storm total accumulations of 8 to 12 inches were reported across inland sections of Erie County.  A maximum of 16.8 inches was reported in McKean with 14.8 inches at Franklin Center.  Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours into the 13th, an area of low pressure developed over Texas. This area of low pressure tracked northeast through the Tennesse Valley during the 13th, and then over the central Appalachians during the 14th. Strong winds and heavy snow accompanied this area of low pressure that affected the entire region. The snow finally ended across the region during the evening and overnight hours of the 14th into the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the morning hours of the 13th, and continued through the day. By the evening, snowfall amounts ranged between 4.0 and 8.0 inches across the area. The snow and gusty winds continued overnight and through the day on the 14th. By the end of the event, snowfall totals include 20.7 inches west of Meadville, 10.5 inches in Titusville, and 9.0 inches in Linesville. A peak wind of 29 mph was measured during the overnight hours on the 14th at the Meadville Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the morning hours of the 13th, and continued through the day. Snow showers that occurred preceding a cold front helped produce slippery conditions and numerous accidents during the morning commute across eastern Pennsylvania from Bucks County northward through the Poconos. The first tractor-trailer jack-knifed. A second tractor-trailer could not stop in time and struck the first tractor-trailer and caught on fire. A third tractor-trailer veered off the interstate to avoid the collision and went down an embankment. All three drivers were injured and hospitalized. Traffic was detoured onto Interstate 380 and Pennsylvania State Route 940. Commutes took three times longer than normal. Schools had delayed openings.|There were also several accidents on Pennsylvania State Route 33 in both Monroe and Northampton Counties. There were delayed school openings in Northampton County also. In Northampton County, accidents were reported in Palmer and Upper Nazareth Townships. In Bucks County, many accidents occurred along U.S. Route 13. |Actual accumulations included 0.7 inches in Warminster (Bucks County), 0.4 inches in Feasterville (Bucks County), 0.3 inches in Macungie (Lehigh County) and the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 0.2 inches in Tannersville (Monroe County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some light precipitation preceding the passage of a warm front fell across the Poconos during the morning of the 15th. While it was warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain, temperatures were around the freezing mark over the higher terrain in the county. The ground was warm from the unseasonably mild first half of the month. So most roadways were just wet. But, a light coating of ice accrued on trees, decks and vehicles. The precipitation ended by 8 a.m. EST and by then all temperatures were above freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours that repeated over the same areas produced poor drainage flooding and flooding of smaller creeks in east central Chester County. Doppler radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches in Newlin, East Bradford, West Bradford and Pocopson Townships, all of which fell within two hours. Actual storm totals included 2.67 inches in Northbrook and 1.87 inches in Downingtown.

Lightning struck the electrical ballasts in the cafeteria area of the Pennridge Central Middle School in Silverdale. The ballasts which control the lights and have tar in them started smoking after the strike. The smoke filled part of the school building, but no flames occurred. The lightning strike did cause some electrical damage, but a school concert that was scheduled for that evening went on without any problems.

Lightning ignited a fire that caused damage to a Wyomissing home on the 27th. One firefighter battling the blaze was treated for smoke inhalation. The fire caused extensive damage to the second floor and attic of the house. More than 80 firefighters from six municipalities helped fight the blaze. The fire was under control at 1045 p.m. EDT.

More than a half dozen fire companies and police departments from Northampton, Bucks and Lehigh Counties helped contain a wildfire on the south side of South Mountain in Lower Saucon Township during the afternoon of the 14th. About three acres were burned. Fire trucks forced the closure of the northbound lanes of Pennsylvania Route 378 for more than an hour. The blaze started about 2 p.m. EDT and the fire was contained by 4 p.m. EDT. Firefighters struggled with the steep terrain and a layer of dried leaves that made footing treacherous.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a couple large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire consumed 70 acres in Washington Township (Lehigh County) in the Blue Mountain area. It was the biggest brush fire in Pennsylvania so far this year to date. Four fire companies were needed to extinguish the blaze (on the 22nd). The rocky ground where the fire occurred and the recent windy, dry weather made it difficult to battle the blaze.

A wildfire that started in the brush from discarded cigarettes, damaged several homes in Bethlehem and forced two dozen people from their home. Gusty southwest winds and warm weather made it difficult for firefighters to battle the blaze. The high temperature at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was 84 degrees and the peak wind gust was 32 mph from the southwest.

In the wake of the departing nor���easter, the combination of strong winds, snow on tree limbs and heavy rain loosening the ground caused many tree limbs, trees and wires to be knocked down on the 16th. Over 160,000 homes and businesses across Eastern Pennsylvania lost power. Most power was restored by the night of the 17th. Many school districts cancelled classes because of the power outages and/or blocked roadways.|Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 77,100 of its eastern Pennsylvania customers lost power on the 16th.  Carbon and Monroe Counties were among the hardest hit counties. In Carbon County, the downed trees caused most of the east side of Jim Thorpe to not have power for most of the daylight hours on the 16th. The docket for the county courthouse was cancelled for the day. In Mahoning Township, part of the metal flashing on the roof of the Times News newspaper was torn away. A fifteen foot piece of the metal was tossed to the ground. In Monroe County, an additional 3,000 Metropolitan Edison customers also lost power in Monroe County. |About 6,000 Metropolitan Edison customers lost power mainly in the eastern half of Berks County as well as Montgomery County. One home was badly damaged by a downed tree in Mount Penn Borough (Berks County). The family was displaced. In the Lehigh Valley, about 65,000 homes and businesses lost power. In Northampton County, the most concentrated outages were in the northern part of the county in Upper Mount Bethel, Bushkill and Moore Townships. Downed tree limbs closed a few roads in Plainfield Township.   |Across southeastern Pennsylvania including Philadelphia, about 80,000 PECO Energy customers lost power. In Chester County, a 100-foot tree crushed a King Road home in East Whiteland Township rendering it uninhabitable. Twelve roads across the county were closed because of downed trees, utility poles and wires. PECO Energy reported that 30,000 of its 80,000 outages in southeastern Pennsylvania occurred in Chester County. The R5 SEPTA Commuter Rail Line suspended its Paoli service because of lost power. The worst utility damage occurred in the northwest part of the county as many Pennsylvania Power and Light utility poles were knocked down along Pennsylvania State Route 10 in Honey Brook. The road was closed for several hours. In Delaware County, about 24,000 homes and businesses lost power, with the most outages in Radnor Township. One home was damaged by downed trees in Radnor Township. Downed trees closed roads and caused power outages in Chadds Ford, Colwyn, Concord, Folcroft, Haverford, Lansdowne, Marple, Newtown Square, Radnor, Sharon Hill and Springfield.  The Pennsylvania Route 101 trolley was replaced by bus service as a downed tree blocked the route. Power interruptions also affected the R3 SEPTA Regional Rail Media Line. In Montgomery County, the worst reported tree damage was in Lower Merion Township where about two dozen trees were knocked down. One house was damaged by the fallen trees. Power interruptions suspended service on the Cynwyd R6 SEPTA Regional Rail Line on the 16th. In Philadelphia, the strong winds caused delays that averaged 100 minutes at Philadelphia International Airport. U.S. Air cancelled about 35 flights outright.  About 50 trees were knocked down within Philadelphia. In Bucks County, about 6,000 homes and businesses lost power. |Peak wind gusts included 55 mph in Slatington (Lehigh County), 52 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 51 mph in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 49 mph in Doylestown (Bucks County) and London Grove (Chester County), 47 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 46 mph in Reading (Berks County) and the Lehigh Valley International Airport and the 45 mph at the Willow Grove NAS was 45 mph.

A low pressure system that passed just south of Lakes Michigan and Erie helped produce the first measurable snow of the winter season for the higher terrain of the Poconos. Rain began during the afternoon of the 9th and precipitation changed to snow over the higher terrain during the late evening of the 9th. Light snow continued through the night and ended during the morning of the 10th. Accumulations over the higher terrain averaged around one inch.  The low pressure system moved from Ohio on the morning of the 9th into western Pennsylvania on the evening of the 9th. A second low pressure system formed well east of the Delmarva Peninsula the same evening. The primary low pressure system stalled and dissipated in western Pennsylvania overnight as the secondary low slowly intensified and moved farther offshore. By the morning of the 10th, this low pressure system was well southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, late season Nor'easter brought rain and heavy, wet snow to portions of Central Pennsylvania during April 16 through 17. Rain initially over the region changed to wet snow during the 16th, continuing into the early morning hours of the 17th. Heavy snowfall was confined to higher elevations, with warning criteria snow only observed along ridges. Valley locations received much lesser snow amounts. The heavy snow, combined with strong northwest winds of 20 to 40 mph caused numerous power outages across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the county Sunday evening. Significant accumulations of snow occurred over most of the county, with Dushore reporting twelve inches, Laporte reporting ten inches and Eldredsville reporting five and one half inches. Greatest snow totals occurred over the higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November 2007 brought the return of below normal precipitation to the local Philadelphia area and Berks County. Precipitation was about normal in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. On a county weighted average, November rainfall ranged from 1.5 inches in Philadelphia County to 4.2 inches in Carbon and Monroe Counties. The northwest Philadelphia counties averaged around 2.5 inches. Normal is around 3.9 inches.||The drought watch remained in effect for all of Eastern Pennsylvania. A drought watch calls for a voluntary reduction in water consumption of five percent. The Department of Environmental Protection recommended residents to check for leaks, install low flow plumbing, take showers instead of baths, replace older appliances with more efficient new ones, run the dishwasher and washing machine at full loads and keep cold water in the refrigerator.  ||Actual monthly precipitation totals included 1.45 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport (1.71 inches below normal), 3.36 inches in Reading in Berks County (0.18 inches below normal), 4.23 inches in Mount Pocono in Monroe County (0.01 inches above normal) and 4.44 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (0.74 inches above normal).

Periods of freezing rain resulted in minor to moderate ice accretion from the afternoon into the early evening across the northern tier of Central Pennsylvania. Slick roads resulted in numerous reports of vehicles sliding into telephone polls, causing sporadic power outages. Despite widespread quarter-inch ice build-up on trees and wires, local electric companies did not have any problems with downed lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Wellsboro reported over a quarter-inch of ice from freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of freezing rain resulted in minor to moderate ice accretion over the North Central Mountains of Central Pennsylvania. The freezing rain began late on the 2nd and continued into the early morning hours on the 3rd. The overall impact from the ice accretion was minimal, given the time of occurrence (very late at night). In addition, the ice that did build-up on lines and trees quickly melted as temperatures increased overnight and precipitation changed to plain rain. While there were a few reports of power outages, none of them could be directly attributed to the freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bradford Regional Airport ASOS (KBFD) reported just over a quarter inch of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the northern Plains on November 17th toward the Mid Atlantic States on the 18th, with a warm front draped from Ohio to southern Delaware. Another area of low pressure however developed off the coast of Virginia during the 18th and then moved out to sea on the 19th and into the 20th. A surface trough however bridged the two areas of low pressure. Meanwhile, an upper-level low tracked across the upper Ohio Valley and right across Pennsylvania late on the 19th, however it was weakening with time. This brought the first widespread accumulating snowfall of the season to northeastern Pennsylvania and even to some areas a little farther south. This storm was elevation dependent to some extent, as temperatures in the lower elevations were just above freezing during most of the event. The snow that did accumulate was heavy and wet and caused nearly 13,000 homes and businesses to lose power in the Lehigh Valley (where some trees still had leaves) and the Poconos. The storm system gradually moved off to the east on the 19th, bringing an end to the widespread precipitation.||Snow began falling in the Poconos just after Midnight EST on the 18th and fell heavy at times during the evening and night. The snow ended during the early afternoon on the 19th. In the Lehigh Valley, snow began falling around 7 a.m. EST on the 18th, but did not accumulate as efficiently as the Poconos as temperatures were at or above freezing. Most of the accumulating snow was over by sunrise on the 19th, but the snow did not end until around Noon EST on the 19th. In Berks County and upper Bucks County, precipitation started as rain during the morning of the 18th. The rain changed to snow during the afternoon of the 18th in Berks County and the evening of the 18th in upper Bucks County. It ended during the morning of the 19th. Accumulations were very elevation dependent and ranged from only an inch or two in the valleys to around ten inches in the higher terrain of the Poconos.||In The Poconos, fallen trees caused about 6,000 homes and businesses to lose power. Power was not completely restored until the afternoon of the 20th. In Smithfield Township (Monroe County), two sixty-five foot red oak trees were downed by the snow onto the deck of one home. A large limb also damaged the chimney of that home. Fallen trees blocked several roads. In Monroe County, reported power outages caused by downed trees included Middle Smithfield, Smithfield, Hamilton and Ross Townships.  Throughout the Poconos, most schools had delayed openings on the 19th (a Monday); a few were closed for the day. Many accidents were caused by the snow. In the Lehigh Valley, about 7,000 homes and businesses lost ower, mainly in Allentown and Bethlehem. Half of the outages were restored on the morning of the 19th, but power was not completely restored until the afternoon of the 20th.||Specific accumulations included (and were very elevation dependent): 11.0 inches in Blakeslee (Monroe County), 10.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County) and Albrightsville (Carbon County), 7.0 inches in Summit Hill (Carbon County), East Stroudsburg (Monroe County),  Fredericksville and Stony Run (Berks County), 6.5 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 6.0 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 4.0 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.2 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 2.5 inches in Elverson (Chester County), 2.0 inches in Boyertown (Berks County) and East Nantmeal (Chester County), 1.0 inch in Morgantown (Berks County), 0.6 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County) and only a trace in Doylestown (Bucks County) and Royersford (Montgomery County).

An area of low pressure moved from the northern Plains on November 17th toward the Mid Atlantic States on the 18th, with a warm front draped from Ohio to southern Delaware. Another area of low pressure however developed off the coast of Virginia during the 18th and then moved out to sea on the 19th and into the 20th. A surface trough however bridged the two areas of low pressure. Meanwhile, an upper-level low tracked across the upper Ohio Valley and right across Pennsylvania late on the 19th, however it was weakening with time. This brought the first widespread accumulating snowfall of the season to northeastern Pennsylvania and even to some areas a little farther south. This storm was elevation dependent to some extent, as temperatures in the lower elevations were just above freezing during most of the event. The snow that did accumulate was heavy and wet and caused nearly 13,000 homes and businesses to lose power in the Lehigh Valley (where some trees still had leaves) and the Poconos. The storm system gradually moved off to the east on the 19th, bringing an end to the widespread precipitation.||Snow began falling in the Poconos just after Midnight EST on the 18th and fell heavy at times during the evening and night. Most of the accumulating snow was over by sunrise on the 19th, but the snow did not end until around Noon EST on the 19th. In Berks County and upper Bucks County, precipitation started as rain during the morning of the 18th. The rain changed to snow during the afternoon of the 18th in Berks County and the evening of the 18th in upper Bucks County. It ended during the morning of the 19th. November 2007 brought the return of below normal precipitation to the local Philadelphia area and Berks County. Precipitation was about normal in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. On a county weighted average, November rainfall ranged from 1.5 inches in Philadelphia County to 4.2 inches in Carbon and Monroe Counties. The northwest Philadelphia counties averaged around 2.5 inches. Normal is around 3.9 inches.||The drought watch remained in effect for all of Eastern Pennsylvania. A drought watch calls for a voluntary reduction in water consumption of five percent. The Department of Environmental Protection recommended residents to check for leaks, install low flow plumbing, take showers instead of baths, replace older appliances with more efficient new ones, run the dishwasher and washing machine at full loads and keep cold water in the refrigerator.  ||Actual monthly precipitation totals included 1.45 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport (1.71 inches below normal), 3.36 inches in Reading in Berks County (0.18 inches below normal), 4.23 inches in Mount Pocono in Monroe County (0.01 inches above normal) and 4.44 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (0.74 inches above normal).

Dense fog formed around the local Philadelphia area from the late morning into the evening of the 20th. Widespread visibilities were one quarter of a mile or less. The fog formed in advance of a warm front and was assisted by the rain that fell during the day. The fog started to dissipate in the evening as the warm front moved through. The dense fog caused delays at Philadelphia International Airport to reach up to two hours on departures and up to three hours on arrivals. Only a few flights overall were canceled. Truckers along Interstate 95 pulled over at rest stops in Bucks County because the fog was too thick for them to drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm affected much of Central Pennsylvania from the evening of the 15th through the 16th. Heavy snow fell over the northern tier Counties, with a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain over the South Central Mountains and Middle/Lower Susquehanna Valley.||Numerous reports of hazardous road conditions and downed utility lines and trees were received as the storm moved north across the Commonwealth. County 911 centers reported numerous vehicle accidents and power outages.  The Public Utility Commission reported more than one-hundred thousand customers were without power. Utility crews from Pennsylvania and Ohio were dispatched to make repairs and restore service. The affected counties coordinated with local emergency units and the American Red Cross to establish shelters for residents who were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moderate to heavy coating of ice was common across the entire Lower Susquehanna Valley. Lancaster County emergency management along with trained spotters reported numerous trees and wires down, which resulted in over 11,000 reported power outages. MetEd electric company reported approximately 64,000 customers without power across the Lower Susquehanna Valley.||A brief period of sleet was observed at the onset of precipitation, but the heavy ice build-up was primarily due to freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper low pressure system dropped light snow across eastern Pennyslvania from the Lehigh Valley southeast through the local Philadelphia area on December 5th. Snow began during the morning and ended during the evening. Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches with the highest amounts in Chester County. Portions of Bucks and Montgomery Counties had less snow. Less than an inch of snow fell in the Poconos. While the snow was never heavy, most of the accumulating snow across southeastern Pennsylvania occurred during two spurts, one in the early afternoon and the second during the early evening.||Chester County had the greatest problems with the snow. Untreated roads iced as evening arrived and numerous accidents occurred. The southbound lanes of Pennsylvania State Route 100 Spur in West Goshen and West Whiteland were closed for a while until the roadway could be treated. Several school districts cancelled after school activities. On the morning of the 6th, nine school districts had two hour delayed openings because of the icy conditions.||Actual accumulations included 2.8 inches in Lionville (Chester County), 2.0 inches in West Chester (Chester County), 1.9 inches in Weisenberg (Lehigh County), 1.7 inches in Morgantown (Berks County), 1.6 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 1.5 inches in Reading (Berks County) and Feasterville (Bucks County), 1.4 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 1.2 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County) and 0.7 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County). ||As the name implies, the low pressure that caused the snow moved quickly from southern Alberta at 7 a.m. EST on the 4th to the central Ohio Valley at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th. By sunrise on the 6th, it was already well east of the Virginia coast.
Persistent snow over northeast Pennsylvania accumulated 7 inches in Sullivan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of snow and snow showers produced up to 7 inches of snow accumulation across Sullivan County during the evening and overnight hours of January 28th to the early morning of the 29th.

Cold temperatures and brisk winds combined to produce bitterly cold wind chills across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures within a few degrees of zero, combined with west winds of 10 to 20 mph, produced persistent wind chills of 15 to 20 degrees below zero, and occasionally dropping to between 25 and 30 degrees below zero during the overnight and early morning hours of the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure moved east from the Great Lakes to the New Jersey coast. This system combined with an upper level disturbance to bring accumulating snow to much of Northeast Pennsylvania. The event was elevation driven, with the valley areas receiving about 3 inches of snow, while the higher elevations received as much as one foot. The heavy, wet nature of the snow caused some tree and power line damage, with scattered power outages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county ranged from 4 to 11 inches, with the higher totals measured at the higher elevations.

High winds brought down trees and wires during the afternoon and evening hours. The high winds were the result of a very tight pressure gradient following the departure of a significant winter storm. The winds were non-convective in nature. Regional ASOS observations recorded peak gusts as high as 51 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blair County emergency management reported a tree down on a house in Martinsburg due to high winds. The Altoona ASOS at the Blair County Airport reported a peak gust of 48 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a storm system on December 13th, a moist air mass was left in place. As a weak area of high pressure settled in early on the 14th, this allowed for light winds which combined with the moist air mass. As a result, areas of dense fog formed across southeastern Pennsylvania. The dense fog occurred from about 4 AM EST and lasted until about 11 AM EST. The dense fog caused flight delays at Philadelphia International Airport.

Cold west to northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold west to northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the morning hours of December 3rd.  The snow showers diminished to flurries during the afternoon of the 4th.  Snowfall totals across much of Crawford County ranged from 6 to 10 inches during this event.  A maximum of 13.5 inches was measured near Meadville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west winds and low temperatures combined to produce very low wind chills across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the single digits, combined with west winds of 10 to 20 mph, produced wind chills of 10 to 15 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Colebrookdale and Hereford Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Tredyffrin Township. One fell down on King of Prussia Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm also knocked down trees and wires in Chestnuthill and Ross Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense area of low pressure on the 1st moved into the Upper Midwest with a cold front stretched southward across the Ohio Valley all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. Meanwhile, another cold front moved through the Northeast and settled into the Mid Atlantic region. This boundary remained stationary for a time during the overnight hours of the 1st, as the strong low pressure system moved into the western Great Lakes. Gradually an occluded front moved through the area during the morning hours of the 2nd, as a weak area of low pressure developed over eastern Pennsylvania. Along and ahead of this boundary and weak area of low pressure, an abundance of moisture was brought northward across our area. This lead to 1 to 3 inches of rain late on the 1st into the 2nd, and when combined with melting piles of snow in some areas, mainly minor flooding of streams, creeks and some roads resulted across eastern Pennsylvania. Some of the flooding closed several roads in the region.|The rain began between 7:45 PM and 9:00 PM EST on the 1st and became heavy at times, then tapered off generally between 9:00 AM and 10:00 AM EST on the 2nd. Some rainfall totals include, 3.65 inches in New Garden Township (Chester County); 2.50 inches in Fricks (Bucks County); 2.25 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County); 2.21 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County); 2.20 inches in Exton, Franklin and Honey Brook (Chester County); 2.13 inches in Pine Valley (Philadelphia County); 2.10 inches in Southampton (Bucks County); 2.00 inches on Martins Creek (Northampton County); 1.89 inches in Chadds Ford (Delaware County); 1.82 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport (Philadelphia County); 1.73 inches at Blue Marsh Lake (Berks County); 1.70 inches in Springfield (Delaware County); 1.67 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County); 1.62 inches in Palm (Montgomery County); 1.52 inches in Hamburg and at the Reading Airport (Berks County); 1.50 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport near Allenton (Lehigh County); 1.46 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County); 1.45 inches in Blakeslee (Monroe County); 1.43 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport (Philadelphia County); 1.40 inches at the Mount Pocono Airport (Monroe County); 1.40 inches in Beltzville Lake (Carbon County); 1.38 inches in Green Lane (Montgomery County); and 0.93 inches in Stoddartsville (Monroe County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snowmelt caused flooding on Bake Oven Road in Heidelberg Township, closing the road early on the 3rd after a local stream left its banks. The flooding occurred between Routes 309 and 100. Hokendauqua Park and a nearby playground were flooded on the 3rd after the Hokendauqua Creek overflowed its banks. Shortly after 10:30 PM EST on the 2nd, high water from heavy rain flooded Wild Cherry Lane between Lower Macungie Road and Sauerkraut Road in Macungie Township, which trapped a motorist. The person climbed out onto the trunk of their car and was rescued by firefighters. The Catasauqua Creek overflowed its banks on the 2nd at the intersection of Walnut and St. John streets in Catasauqua.|Allentown fire officials reported that minor flooding occurred in parts of the city early on the 2nd. The main trouble spots were confined mostly to Upper Macungie and Lower Macungie. The Pennsylvania Department of Transportation closed roads in Heidelberg, Lynn and South Whitehall townships due to flooding. Schantz Road, between Hamilton Boulevard and Cetronia Road, and Spring Creek Road, between Route 100 and Lower Macungie Road, were closed for much of the 2nd.  Flooding also occurred along the Jordan Creek in Whitehall early on the 2nd.|Some local gauged waterways that experienced flooding are as follows: The Little Lehigh River at Allentown went above the flood stage of 6.0 feet at 9:35 AM EST, crested at 6.16 feet at 6:00 PM EST and dropped below flood stage at 7:45 PM EST. The Monocacy Creek at Bethlehem went above the flood of 4.5 feet at 6:43 AM EST, crested at 5.69 feet at 2:45 PM EST and dropped below flood stage at 6:54 PM EST. (all times on the 2nd).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold temperatures and brisk winds combined to produce bitterly cold wind chills across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures within a few degrees of zero, combined with west winds of 10 to 20 mph, produced persistent wind chills of 15 to 20 degrees below zero, and occasionally dropping to between 25 and 30 degrees below zero during the overnight and early morning hours of the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A drought watch remained in effect for all of eastern Pennsylvania. A drought watch calls for a voluntary reduction in water consumption of five percent. Conditions though improved as December was wetter than normal throughout the area. On a county weighted average, December precipitation ranged from 4.3 inches in Philadelphia County to 5.7 inches in Monroe County. Normal is around 3.7 inches. The December monthly precipitation total in Philadelphia was 4.41 inches, 1.10 inches wetter than normal, at the Lehigh Valley International Airport 5.02 inches, 1.63 inches wetter than normal, in Reading (Berks County) 4.99 inches, 1.68 inches wetter than normal and in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) 6.33 inches, 2.73 inches wetter than normal.

A wintry mix of precipitation fell across eastern Pennsylvania from the early morning of the 2nd through the early morning of the 3rd. Precipitation slowly changed to rain from Philadelphia northwest during the evening of the 2nd, but it never changed to plain rain in the Poconos. Snow and sleet accumulations reached up to four inches in the Poconos and ice accretions reached around one quarter of an inch. Many accidents were caused by the wintry mix of precipitation including a traffic related fatality in Monroe County.||Precipitation started as light snow from Philadelphia north during the early morning of the 2nd. The snow changed to sleet from around 8 a.m. EST in the local Philadelphia area to Noon EST in the Poconos. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged around an inch or less in the local Philadelphia area and two to four inches in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. In the local Philadelphia area, precipitation was intermittent during the afternoon, but fell primarily as freezing rain. Because of the recent warm weather and indirect insolation from the sun, roads remained wet and the freezing rain was limited to exposed surfaces. Farther to the north precipitation mixed with and then changed to freezing rain from the late morning into the afternoon.  As warmer air moved inland, the freezing rain changed to rain during the early evening around the local Philadelphia area and took to about Midnight EST on the 3rd for the change to plain rain to occur in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley. Precipitation remained freezing rain over the Poconos until the event ended around 3 a.m. EST on the 3rd. Ice accretions averaged around one-quarter of an inch with the highest amounts in the Poconos and Berks County.||In Monroe County, a 61-year-old man was killed on Pennsylvania State Route 447 after another vehicle crossed the center line and hit him head-on. His passenger, his 61-year old wife was injured.  The other vehicle's driver and passenger were also hospitalized. Pennsylvania State Route 447 was closed for over an hour. Schools in the Poconos and the Lehigh Valley had two hour delayed openings the following morning (the 3rd). In the Lehigh Valley, an Allentown couple was uninjured in Wilson (Northampton County) after their vehicle skidded off the road and flipped into Springbrook Creek. Another vehicle skidded off the same road. In Lower Mount Bethel Township (Northampton County), a woman was injured after skidding into a pole. In Whitehall Township (Lehigh County), two separate vehicles failed to negotiate a curve on Fullerton Avenue. One skidded into a police vehicle. The worst reported accident in Philadelphia was a multi-vehicular one in Chestnut Hill.||Specific snow and sleet accumulations included 4.0 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 3.4 inches in Middle Smithfield Township (Monroe County), 2.0 inches in Tatamy (Northampton County), 1.8 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County) and Lehighton (Carbon County), 1.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.5 inches in Fredericksville (Berks County), 1.4 inches in Springtown (Bucks County) and 1.0 inch in Palm (Montgomery County).||The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Great Lakes on the morning of the 2nd into the Saint Lawrence Valley on the morning of the 3rd. Even though the low pressure system moved north of the region, the cold air near the ground was difficult to dislodge. While it became warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain, it still froze on contact especially over the higher terrain.

Strong winds buffeted eastern Pennsylvania during the second half of the morning and into the evening on the 3rd. Highest wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and downed trees, tree limbs, scattered garbage pails and caused scattered power outages. Two people were injured by the wind.  ||In Bucks County, a 26-year-old man was injured while hanging rafters at a Richland Township store that was under construction. He was literally blown over by the wind and fell twenty feet. In Doylestown, a downed tree damaged a pole and the corner of a roof of one home. Evening classes were cancelled at the Delaware Valley College because of power outages. In Solebury Township, downed trees blocked Bridgeton Road. In Montgomery County, a woman was injured by a downed tree on the Pennsylvania Turnpike in Upper Moreland Township near the Willow Grove Interchange. Downed trees forced road closures in Horsham Township. In Delaware County, a downed tree limb damaged a corner of a home in Haverford Township. PECO Energy reported about 33,000 homes and businesses lost power in the local Philadelphia area. All power was restored by the 4th.||Specific wind gusts included 52 mph in Forks Township (Northampton County), 49 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Willow Grove (Montgomery County), 48 mph in London Grove (Chester County) and 46 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County). The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system that was intensifying as it was moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building toward the northeast from the Southern Plains.

A lake effect snow band caused moderate to heavy snow showers to occur across about a ten mile wide band from the Pocono Plateau eastsoutheast into northern New Jersey during the morning commute on the 5th. Prior to the band, accumulating snow showers occurred earlier that same morning. Within the band, accumulations average around two inches, but localized amounts reached six inches. This occurred in Pocono Summit (Monroe County).  Very strong winds (gusts averaged around 40 mph) accompanied the snow showers and caused very poor visibilities and hazardous driving conditions.

A weak low pressure system combined with cold air near the surface to cause freezing rain across parts of Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos from the afternoon of the 9th into the early morning on the 10th. Ice accretions averaged around one tenth of an inch in the Lehigh Valley and Berks County (where ice occurred) and up to one-quarter of an inch in the Poconos.||As precipitation started during the afternoon of the 9th, it began as freezing rain. In Berks County, the freezing rain was mainly confined to the higher terrain in the northeastern part of the county. As warmer air near the surface moved north, the freezing rain changed to rain by the middle of the evening in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley. But, warmer air did not make it entirely through the Poconos where precipitation remained as freezing rain until it ended a little bit after midnight EST on the 10th. Even after the precipitation ended in the Poconos, some dense fog with subfreezing temperatures persisted through the night.||Ice accretions reached up to one-quarter of an inch on the Pocono Plateau. The ice forced many school districts to have delayed openings on Monday morning the 10th in the Poconos. A few never opened at all. In the Lehigh Valley and higher terrain of eastern Berks County, only a light glazing was reported on trees, decks and other exposed surfaces.||A large high pressure system that was over New York State and New England on the 9th supplied the dense, cold air near the surface. That morning a low pressure system started to develop along the stationary front in the Tennessee Valley. It supplied the overrunning moisture. While it was warm enough above the surface for precipitation to fall as rain, the cold dense air near the ground was tough to displace and the rain froze on contact. The low pressure system itself moved northeast and during the early evening on the 9th was near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. A secondary low pressure system formed along its warm frontal boundary east of Delaware overnight. By sunrise on the 10th, the low pressure system was already well east (about 150 miles) of the New Jersey coast.

A cold front moved through the region on December 12th. Behind this front, high pressure settled into northern New England providing colder air into the region. An area of low pressure then developed along the aforementioned front across the Tennessee Valley. This storm then tracked northeastward on December 13th, spreading moisture across the entire region. This storm produced one to as much as five inches of snow and sleet across eastern Pennsylvania, however warmer air arrived well above the surface allowing freezing rain to fall for a time. Some places picked up about one-quarter of an inch of ice. The storm then moved out of the area during the first half of the night on the 13th.||In the southern Poconos, all the schools in Carbon County were closed on the 13th. Due to the slippery conditions on many of the roadways, there were some accidents, which included an overturned SUV on the eastbound lanes of Interstate 80 near the Water Gap. ||For the Lehigh Valley and Berks County, the combination of snow and ice caused roadways to become slippery. As a result of the inclement weather, open schools and businesses let out early on the 13th. At 2:05 PM EST, there was an indirect fatal accident on the westbound lanes of Interstate 78 in Greenwich Township (Berks County) involving two tractor-trailers and two vehicles. One of the vehicles failed to yield and caused another vehicle to strike a tractor-trailer and then spun when it was hit by another vehicle. An 83-year old female passenger in one of the vehicles was killed, and the 50-year old female driver was injured. The slippery conditions lead to several fender benders and sliding cars. In anticipation of the hazardous winter weather, many schools closed before the precipitation started. There were a number of businesses that either shut down or dismissed early, and mass transit had between 10 and 15 minute delays. Travel by air was also impacted as cancellations and delays were reported at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. There were about 160 customers without power, and power was completely restored at 3:30 AM EST on the 14th. ||For the northern and western suburbs of Philadelphia, ice built-up on trees in North Coventry (Chester County) due to freezing rain. The icing created hazardous road conditions, particularly on untreated surfaces, with traffic even stalled in some areas. The ice build-up downed some trees and trees limbs in some areas including the Phoenixville area (Chester County), and trees were completely covered in ice in the Horsham area (Montgomery County). Three school districts in Montgomery County and six in Bucks County closed early, with after school activities cancelled at several districts. In total however, some 60 schools closed early or sent students home early. An Outlet Center in Limerick (Montgomery County) lost power because of the icing. There were a total of 1,900 customers without power in Montgomery County alone, and when those outage numbers were combined with Bucks County, the number was greater than 10,000 customers. Some brief road closures were reported in parts of Bucks County because of minor accidents, including the Doylestown area and on the by-pass in Richland Township, with vehicles off the roads in Plumstead Township. Branches sagging from the weight of the ice touched power lines, which contributed to about 180 PPL customers losing power. ||Accumulations of snow, sleet along with some ice across eastern Pennsylvania included, 5.2 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County); 4.0 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County); 2.0 inches in Lehighton (Monroe County); 1.2 inches at Trexlertown (Lehigh County); 1.0 inch in Hamburg (Berks County), the Lehigh Valley International Airport, New Tripoli (Lehigh County), Martins Creek (Northampton County), Tatamy (Northampton County) and Souderton (Bucks County); 0.8 of an inch in Weisenberg (Lehigh County) and Easton (Northampton County); 0.7 of an inch in Glenmoore (Chester County), Honey Brook (Chester County) and Fricks (Bucks County); 0.5 of an inch in Perkasie (Bucks County); 0.4 of an inch in Palm (Montgomery County) and 0.3 of an inch in Hatboro (Montgomery County) and Doylestown (Bucks County).||Freezing rain amounts ranged from, 0.30 of an inch in Morgantown (Berks County), West Lawn (Berks County) and Phoenixville (Chester County); 0.25 of an inch in East Nantmeal (Chester County); 0.20 of an inch in Easton (Northampton County), Coatesville (Chester County), West Brandywine (Chester County), West Grove (Chester County) and Westtown (Chester County) and 0.1 of an inch in Fricks (Bucks County), Exton (Chester County) and Royersford (Montgomery County). Some ice was reported in Warrington (Bucks County).

A low pressure system brought mixed precipitation across Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. The combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain fell and accumulations averaged one to four inches with the highest amounts in the Poconos. ||Precipitation started as rain in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and as snow in the Poconos during the afternoon on the 30th. As warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain in the Poconos during the evening of the 30th. As the low pressure pulled away, precipitation changed back to snow in the Poconos late that evening and then later that night in the Lehigh Valley and Berks County. The snow ended by 6 a.m. EST on the 31st. Most of the traffic related accidents occurred in the Poconos, as the plateau region reported the most. There were two serious crashes in the area of Interstate 80 and Interstate 380 in Monroe County. ||Specific accumulations included 4.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 3.7 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 2.0 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 1.6 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 0.7 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 0.4 inches in Hamburg (Berks County).||The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that formed over the southeastern states early on December 30th. It moved northeast and at 7 p.m. EST on the 30th it was just east of Elizabeth City, North Carolina. The low moved northeast and at 1 a.m. EST on the 31st, it was just east of Delaware. It accelerated eastnortheastward and by 7 a.m. EST on the 31st was already about 125 miles southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. The fast movement prevented precipitation from becoming heavy.

A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region, which is known as cold air damming. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. As a result, moisture was lifted up and over colder air trapped at the surface. This produced about 1 to 4 inches of snow and sleet across portions of eastern Pennsylvania, with up to one-half of an inch of ice (from freezing rain). The storm system moved away from the area during the overnight hours of the 16th. A total of 160,000 customers in eastern Pennsylvania lost power during the storm.||Across the southern Poconos, snow and sleet accumulated about 2 to 5 inches before changing to freezing rain. Ice accumulations up to one-half inch were reported across the area. The mixed bag of precipitation created hazardous conditions on the roads, and the weight from the ice brought down some trees and power lines. Interstate 80 was closed in Carbon County between exits 274 and 277 because of downed power lines during the afternoon of the 16th which lasted until about 11 PM EST. Many schools were closed in Carbon County due to the inclement weather. Power was knocked out to about 7 PPL customers in Monroe County.||In the Lehigh Valley and Berks County, a total of 35,000 Met Ed customers lost power and around 2,500 PPL customers were without power. In Northampton County, Met Ed reported outages in Wind Gap and Easton. Of the total Met Ed customers who lost power, 13,500 were still without late on the 16th, 5,100 without during the evening of the 17th, with the last 3,250 customers on the 19th. Due to the widespread power outages in Berks County, shelters were opened in Oley, Sinking Spring and Bernville. The ice made roadways very hazardous. In Lehigh County, a 28-year old man was killed in Upper Macungie Township at 4:00 AM EST on the 16th after his car struck a tree and pole on Newtown Road. In addition, there was a report of a vehicle rollover on Interstate 78 in Williams Township (Northampton County). Businesses were closed on the 16th and some of them opened late. Increasing winds added to the number of fallen trees and resultant power outages. Dozens of downed trees along with power lines were reported across Berks County.||For the northern and western suburbs of Philadelphia, 10,000 PECO customers were without power, with 7,000 of those in Bucks County alone. PPL reported 7,300 customers were without power in Bucks and Montgomery Counties. The power was completely restored to all customers by the early evening on the 18th. Trees and power lines were downed across Chester, Montgomery and Bucks Counties with the most damage having occurred in the western/northwestern areas.||While accumulations across eastern Pennsylvania were mostly of sleet, there was some snow mixed in. These amounts include, 4.0 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County) and Nesquehoning (Carbon County); 3.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), Pocono Summit (Monroe County)and Snydersville (Monroe County); 2.1 inches in Marshalls Creek (Northampton County); 1.8 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and in Williams Township (Northampton County); 1.5 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County) and Nazareth (Northampton County); 1.4 inches in Springtown (Bucks County); 1.2 inches in Martins Creek Northampton County); and 1.0 inch in Hamburg (Berks County) and Whitehall (Lehigh County). Some ice accumulations from freezing rain include, 0.50 of an inch in parts of Carbon County; 0.30 of an inch in Warwick (Bucks County), Slatington (Lehigh County) and Souderton (Montgomery County); 0.25 of an inch in Saylorsburg (Monroe County); 0.20 of an inch in Williams Township (Northampton County) and 0.10 of an inch in Whitehall (Lehigh County) and Berlinsville (Northampton County).||Following this winter storm, ice flying off of moving vehicles was a problem across the region. In the Lehigh Valley, a 47-year old female was injured when ice dislodged from the top of a tractor-trailer and struck her jeep on Route 22 near Schoenersville Road in Bethlehem. At 6 PM EST on the 17th, a female was traveling westbound on Interstate 78 when ice flew off a tractor-trailer and smashed her vehicles windshield. At 6 AM EST on the 17th, a male was traveling eastbound on Route 22 near Route 378 in Bethlehem when ice from a westbound truck flew off and smashed the windshield. In Upper Bern Township (Berks County) at 10:15 PM EST, a 55-year old male was traveling westbound on Interstate 78 and passed a tractor-trailer when ice flew off and broke his windshield. There were several other non-detailed incidents related to flying ice on Interstate 78. At about 12:40 AM on the 17th, a male was traveling eastbound on Route 22 near Allentown when ice flew off a westbound tractor-trailer, damaging his cars hood, grille and windshield. At about that same time, a 19-year old female was driving westbound on Route 22 in South Whitehall Township when ice from a westbound SUV hit her car. At 11:15 AM on the 19th, a 22-year old female and a 25-year old female in East Allen Township were on there way in an ambulance on Route 329 near Airport Road to pick up a sick patient when ice flew off a tractor-trailer striking the ambulance and hitting the driver in the head. The 22-year old driver suffered a concussion and back and neck pain, while the 25-year old passenger suffered cuts.

Interstate 80 was closed in Carbon County between exits 274 and 277 because of downed power lines during the afternoon of the 16th which lasted until about 11 PM EST. Some ice accumulations from freezing rain include, 0.50 of an inch in parts of Carbon County; 0.30 of an inch in Warwick (Bucks County), Slatington (Lehigh County) and Souderton (Montgomery County); 0.25 of an inch in Saylorsburg (Monroe County); 0.20 of an inch in Williams Township (Northampton County) and 0.10 of an inch in Whitehall (Lehigh County) and Berlinsville (Northampton County).||Following this winter storm, ice flying off of moving vehicles was a problem across the region. In the Lehigh Valley, a 47-year old female was injured when ice dislodged from the top of a tractor-trailer and struck her jeep on Route 22 near Schoenersville Road in Bethlehem. At 6 PM EST on the 17th, a female was traveling westbound on Interstate 78 when ice flew off a tractor-trailer and smashed her vehicles windshield. At 6 AM EST on the 17th, a male was traveling eastbound on Route 22 near Route 378 in Bethlehem when ice from a westbound truck flew off and smashed the windshield. In Upper Bern Township (Berks County) at 10:15 PM EST, a 55-year old male was traveling westbound on Interstate 78 and passed a tractor-trailer when ice flew off and broke his windshield. There were several other non-detailed incidents related to flying ice on Interstate 78. At about 12:40 AM on the 17th, a male was traveling eastbound on Route 22 near Allentown when ice flew off a westbound tractor-trailer, damaging his cars hood, grille and windshield. At about that same time, a 19-year old female was driving westbound on Route 22 in South Whitehall Township when ice from a westbound SUV hit her car. At 11:15 AM on the 19th, a 22-year old female and a 25-year old female in East Allen Township were on there way in an ambulance on Route 329 near Airport Road to pick up a sick patient when ice flew off a tractor-trailer striking the ambulance and hitting the driver in the head. The 22-year old driver suffered a concussion and back and neck pain, while the 25-year old passenger suffered cuts.

A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region, which is known as cold air damming. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. As a result, moisture was lifted up and over colder air trapped at the surface. This produced about 1 to 4 inches of snow and sleet across portions of eastern Pennsylvania, with up to one-half of an inch of ice (from freezing rain). The storm system moved away from the area during the overnight hours of the 16th. A total of 160,000 customers in eastern Pennsylvania lost power during the storm.||Across the southern Poconos, snow and sleet accumulated about 2 to 5 inches before changing to freezing rain. Ice accumulations up to one-half inch were reported across the area. The mixed bag of precipitation created hazardous conditions on the roads, and the weight from the ice brought down some trees and power lines. Interstate 80 was closed in Carbon County between exits 274 and 277 because of downed power lines during the afternoon of the 16th which lasted until about 11 PM EST. Many schools were closed in Carbon County due to the inclement weather. Power was knocked out to about 7 PPL customers in Monroe County.||In the Lehigh Valley and Berks County, a total of 35,000 Met Ed customers lost power and around 2,500 PPL customers were without power. In Northampton County, Met Ed reported outages in Wind Gap and Easton. Of the total Met Ed customers who lost power, 13,500 were still without late on the 16th, 5,100 without during the evening of the 17th, with the last 3,250 customers on the 19th. Due to the widespread power outages in Berks County, shelters were opened in Oley, Sinking Spring and Bernville. The ice made roadways very hazardous. In Lehigh County, a 28-year old man was killed in Upper Macungie Township at 4:00 AM EST on the 16th after his car struck a tree and pole on Newtown Road. In addition, there was a report of a vehicle rollover on Interstate 78 in Williams Township (Northampton County). Businesses were closed on the 16th and some of them opened late. Increasing winds added to the number of fallen trees and resultant power outages. Dozens of downed trees along with power lines were reported across Berks County.||For the northern and western suburbs of Philadelphia, 10,000 PECO customers were without power, with 7,000 of those in Bucks County alone. A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. Strong winds resulted as the storm intensified, mostly as the storm itself began to depart during the evening and overnight on the 16th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph.||The combination of the ice on the trees and wires and the strong winds led to widespread power outages across Eastern Pennsylvania. About 160,000 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not completely restored everywhere until the 19th. The longest outages were in Berks County where shelters were opened in Bernville, Oley and Sinking Spring.||Several homes were damaged by downed trees in the northwest Philadelphia suburbs as Bucks County reported the most damage. About 40,000 homes and businesses lost power. In Buckingham Township on Lancaster Drive, a two-year-old girl narrowly escaped injury as a tree fell into her bedroom. Her mother was injured rescuing her daughter. Three more homes were also damaged by downed trees and/or ensuing fires from downed wires in Bristol Township and Lower Makefield Township. In Towamensing Township, a garage fire was started by downed wires. Elsewhere in Bucks County, downed trees that blocked roads and power outages were reported in Hilltown, Bedminster and East and West Rockhill Townships. After the wind ceased, a 48-year-old man was killed by a tree limb in Bridgeton Township. He was struck in the head by the limb after he slipped on the ice trying to avoid it. The tree was in the process of being removed because of the wind damage it suffered.||In Montgomery County, Franconia Township was hit the hardest as the wind caused over a dozen reports of downed wires. Roadways were closed for up to six hours. Most of the township lost power and a shelter was opened at the fire station. In Hatfield Township, a large tree was knocked down. In Delaware County, in Villanova, a downed 200 foot oak tree cut a home in half. In Chester County, the strong winds overturned a tractor-trailer on Eastbound U.S. Route 422 in North Coventry Township. The roadway was closed for six hours until the tractor-trailer was removed. In Carbon County, Interstate 80 was closed for a couple of hours because of downed wires.||Measured peak wind gusts included 52 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, in London Grove (Chester County) and on Newbold Island (Bucks County), 51 mph at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 44 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

A low pressure system brought mixed precipitation across Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. The combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain fell and accumulations averaged one to four inches with the highest amounts in the Poconos. ||Precipitation started as rain in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and as snow in the Poconos during the afternoon on the 30th. As warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain in the Poconos during the evening of the 30th. As the low pressure pulled away, precipitation changed back to snow in the Poconos late that evening and then later that night in the Lehigh Valley and Berks County. The snow ended by 6 a.m. EST on the 31st. Most of the traffic related accidents occurred in the Poconos, as the plateau region reported the most. There were two serious crashes in the area of Interstate 80 and Interstate 380 in Monroe County. ||Specific accumulations included 4.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 3.7 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 2.0 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 1.6 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 0.7 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 0.4 inches in Hamburg (Berks County).||The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that formed over the southeastern states early on December 30th. It moved northeast and at 7 p.m. EST on the 30th it was just east of Elizabeth City, North Carolina. The low moved northeast and at 1 a.m. EST on the 31st, it was just east of Delaware. It accelerated eastnortheastward and by 7 a.m. EST on the 31st was already about 125 miles southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. The fast movement prevented precipitation from becoming heavy.
