In Chester County, the strong winds overturned a tractor-trailer on Eastbound U.S. Route 422 in North Coventry Township. The roadway was closed for six hours until the tractor-trailer was removed. In Carbon County, Interstate 80 was closed for a couple of hours because of downed wires.||Measured peak wind gusts included 52 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, in London Grove (Chester County) and on Newbold Island (Bucks County), 51 mph at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 44 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. Strong winds resulted as the storm intensified, mostly as the storm itself began to depart during the evening and overnight on the 16th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph.||The combination of the ice on the trees and wires and the strong winds led to widespread power outages across Eastern Pennsylvania. About 160,000 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not completely restored everywhere until the 19th. The longest outages were in Berks County where shelters were opened in Bernville, Oley and Sinking Spring.||Several homes were damaged by downed trees in the northwest Philadelphia suburbs as Bucks County reported the most damage. About 40,000 homes and businesses lost power. In Buckingham Township on Lancaster Drive, a two-year-old girl narrowly escaped injury as a tree fell into her bedroom. Her mother was injured rescuing her daughter. Three more homes were also damaged by downed trees and/or ensuing fires from downed wires in Bristol Township and Lower Makefield Township. In Towamensing Township, a garage fire was started by downed wires. Elsewhere in Bucks County, downed trees that blocked roads and power outages were reported in Hilltown, Bedminster and East and West Rockhill Townships. After the wind ceased, a 48-year-old man was killed by a tree limb in Bridgeton Township. He was struck in the head by the limb after he slipped on the ice trying to avoid it. The tree was in the process of being removed because of the wind damage it suffered.||In Montgomery County, Franconia Township was hit the hardest as the wind caused over a dozen reports of downed wires. Roadways were closed for up to six hours. Most of the township lost power and a shelter was opened at the fire station. In Hatfield Township, a large tree was knocked down. In Delaware County, in Villanova, a downed 200 foot oak tree cut a home in half. In Chester County, the strong winds overturned a tractor-trailer on Eastbound U.S. Route 422 in North Coventry Township. The roadway was closed for six hours until the tractor-trailer was removed. In Carbon County, Interstate 80 was closed for a couple of hours because of downed wires.||Measured peak wind gusts included 52 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, in London Grove (Chester County) and on Newbold Island (Bucks County), 51 mph at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 44 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

A strong cold front was traversing across the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of December 23rd. This strong front crossed eastern Pennsylvania during the evening hours. A southerly flow turned gusty ahead of this front, then winds peaked once again with the passage of the cold front later in the evening. The strongest winds generally occurred between 3 PM EST and 9 PM EST on the 23rd. Peak wind gusts included 46 mph in Reading (Berks County) and 45 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County). Peak wind gusts were lower farther southeast in the Lehigh Valley and the local Philadelphia area.

A low pressure system brought mixed precipitation across Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. The combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain fell and accumulations averaged one to four inches with the highest amounts in the Poconos. ||Precipitation started as rain in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and as snow in the Poconos during the afternoon on the 30th. As warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain in the Poconos during the evening of the 30th. As the low pressure pulled away, precipitation changed back to snow in the Poconos late that evening and then later that night in the Lehigh Valley and Berks County. The snow ended by 6 a.m. EST on the 31st. Most of the traffic related accidents occurred in the Poconos, as the plateau region reported the most. There were two serious crashes in the area of Interstate 80 and Interstate 380 in Monroe County. ||Specific accumulations included 4.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 3.7 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 2.0 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 1.6 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 0.7 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 0.4 inches in Hamburg (Berks County).||The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that formed over the southeastern states early on December 30th. It moved northeast and at 7 p.m. EST on the 30th it was just east of Elizabeth City, North Carolina. The low moved northeast and at 1 a.m. EST on the 31st, it was just east of Delaware. It accelerated eastnortheastward and by 7 a.m. EST on the 31st was already about 125 miles southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. The fast movement prevented precipitation from becoming heavy.

A drought watch remained in effect for all of eastern Pennsylvania. A drought watch calls for a voluntary reduction in water consumption of five percent. Conditions though improved as December was wetter than normal throughout the area. On a county weighted average, December precipitation ranged from 4.3 inches in Philadelphia County to 5.7 inches in Monroe County. Normal is around 3.7 inches. The December monthly precipitation total in Philadelphia was 4.41 inches, 1.10 inches wetter than normal, at the Lehigh Valley International Airport 5.02 inches, 1.63 inches wetter than normal, in Reading (Berks County) 4.99 inches, 1.68 inches wetter than normal and in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) 6.33 inches, 2.73 inches wetter than normal.

Strong winds buffeted eastern Pennsylvania during the second half of the morning and into the evening on the 3rd. Highest wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and downed trees, tree limbs, scattered garbage pails and caused scattered power outages. Two people were injured by the wind.  ||In Bucks County, a 26-year-old man was injured while hanging rafters at a Richland Township store that was under construction. He was literally blown over by the wind and fell twenty feet. In Doylestown, a downed tree damaged a pole and the corner of a roof of one home. Evening classes were cancelled at the Delaware Valley College because of power outages. In Solebury Township, downed trees blocked Bridgeton Road. In Montgomery County, a woman was injured by a downed tree on the Pennsylvania Turnpike in Upper Moreland Township near the Willow Grove Interchange. Downed trees forced road closures in Horsham Township. In Delaware County, a downed tree limb damaged a corner of a home in Haverford Township. PECO Energy reported about 33,000 homes and businesses lost power in the local Philadelphia area. All power was restored by the 4th.||Specific wind gusts included 52 mph in Forks Township (Northampton County), 49 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Willow Grove (Montgomery County), 48 mph in London Grove (Chester County) and 46 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County). The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system that was intensifying as it was moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building toward the northeast from the Southern Plains.

A strong area of low pressure moved through the central Great Lakes on the 23rd. The cold front extending from this low pressure moved through northwest Pennsylvania during the midday and afternoon hours. Winds ahead of the cold front were gusty from the south. Winds continued through the afternoon hours as the cold front moved through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of precipitation fell across eastern Pennsylvania from the early morning of the 2nd through the early morning of the 3rd. Precipitation slowly changed to rain from Philadelphia northwest during the evening of the 2nd, but it never changed to plain rain in the Poconos. Snow and sleet accumulations reached up to four inches in the Poconos and ice accretions reached around one quarter of an inch. Many accidents were caused by the wintry mix of precipitation including a traffic related fatality in Monroe County.||Precipitation started as light snow from Philadelphia north during the early morning of the 2nd. The snow changed to sleet from around 8 a.m. EST in the local Philadelphia area to Noon EST in the Poconos. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged around an inch or less in the local Philadelphia area and two to four inches in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. In the local Philadelphia area, precipitation was intermittent during the afternoon, but fell primarily as freezing rain. Because of the recent warm weather and indirect insolation from the sun, roads remained wet and the freezing rain was limited to exposed surfaces. Farther to the north precipitation mixed with and then changed to freezing rain from the late morning into the afternoon.  As warmer air moved inland, the freezing rain changed to rain during the early evening around the local Philadelphia area and took to about Midnight EST on the 3rd for the change to plain rain to occur in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley. Precipitation remained freezing rain over the Poconos until the event ended around 3 a.m. EST on the 3rd. Ice accretions averaged around one-quarter of an inch with the highest amounts in the Poconos and Berks County.||In Monroe County, a 61-year-old man was killed on Pennsylvania State Route 447 after another vehicle crossed the center line and hit him head-on. His passenger, his 61-year old wife was injured.  The other vehicle's driver and passenger were also hospitalized. Pennsylvania State Route 447 was closed for over an hour. Schools in the Poconos and the Lehigh Valley had two hour delayed openings the following morning (the 3rd). In the Lehigh Valley, an Allentown couple was uninjured in Wilson (Northampton County) after their vehicle skidded off the road and flipped into Springbrook Creek. Another vehicle skidded off the same road. In Lower Mount Bethel Township (Northampton County), a woman was injured after skidding into a pole. In Whitehall Township (Lehigh County), two separate vehicles failed to negotiate a curve on Fullerton Avenue. One skidded into a police vehicle. The worst reported accident in Philadelphia was a multi-vehicular one in Chestnut Hill.||Specific snow and sleet accumulations included 4.0 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 3.4 inches in Middle Smithfield Township (Monroe County), 2.0 inches in Tatamy (Northampton County), 1.8 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County) and Lehighton (Carbon County), 1.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.5 inches in Fredericksville (Berks County), 1.4 inches in Springtown (Bucks County) and 1.0 inch in Palm (Montgomery County).||The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Great Lakes on the morning of the 2nd into the Saint Lawrence Valley on the morning of the 3rd. Even though the low pressure system moved north of the region, the cold air near the ground was difficult to dislodge. While it became warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain, it still froze on contact especially over the higher terrain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds brought down trees and wires during the afternoon and evening hours. The high winds were the result of a very tight pressure gradient following the departure of a significant winter storm. The winds were non-convective in nature. Regional ASOS observations recorded peak gusts as high as 51 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cambria County emergency management reported trees and wires down due to high winds. The Johnstown Airport ASOS reported a peak gust of 51 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant Lake Effect Snowfall occurred over the Northwest Mountains of Central Pennsylvania. Storm total snowfall amounts of over a foot were reported across the Snowbelt region of northwest Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from six to eleven inches across portions of McKean County. Notable snowfall totals included eleven inches at Kane and eight inches at Cyclone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure moved east from the Great Lakes to the New Jersey coast. This system combined with an upper level disturbance to bring accumulating snow to much of Northeast Pennsylvania. The event was elevation driven, with the valley areas receiving about 3 inches of snow, while the higher elevations received as much as one foot. The heavy, wet nature of the snow caused some tree and power line damage, with scattered power outages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county ranged from 5 to 11 inches, with the higher amounts occurring at higher elevations. The storm caused power outages and closed some roads as the weight of the snow brought down scattered trees and utility poles. About 1,000 customers were without power in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the Southeast United States to off the Middle Atlantic coast. Light snow overspread northeast Pennsylvania and became heavy at times before tapering off by early evening. The snow also mixed with sleet across some areas, especially the Wyoming Valley, which held snow accumulations down. Snowfall amounts ranged generally between 5 and 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a mix of snow and sleet to the area. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 6 to 8 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from the Carolina coast to east of Cape Cod from the afternoon of the 29th through the morning of the 30th. Snow, mixed with sleet at times, spread into northeast Pennsylvania during the evening on Sunday and continued overnight before tapering off Monday morning. Snowfall amounts ranged around 7 inches in Susquehanna County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the county ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest to west winds occurred during the daytime hours on January 20th across Eastern Pennsylvania. It was caused by an intense pressure gradient (large difference in surface pressure over a relatively small area) between a powerful low pressure system (960 millibars at 7 a.m. EST on the 20th) moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building east from the Midwest. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 47 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, London Grove (Chester County) and Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 44 mph at the Willow Grove NAS (Montgomery County), 43 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 40 mph in Reading (Berks County).

A major winter storm affected central Pennsylvania from the early morning hours of February 13th through the late afternoon of the 14th. In addition to at least 6 inches of snow, significant accumulations of sleet and ice were also noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lake effect snow band from Lake Erie that remained intact brought snow showers and snow squalls to the Poconos, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley during the morning commute. Accumulations were light, all reported amounts were less than one inch. But the combination of the snow melting and then water freezing on road surfaces plus the first snow event of the season (for some) led to many traffic accidents, especially in the Lehigh Valley and particularly in Northampton County. |Westbound Interstate 78 had just one lane opened because of several crashes between the New Jersey and Lehigh County lines. A van spun out of control on the icy roadway and struck a concrete barrier in Lower Saucon Township (Northampton County), closing the right lane for about an hour. One person in the van suffered a minor injury. All lanes were reopened by 11 a.m. EST. One vehicle rolled over on U.S. Route 22 in Palmer Township (Northampton County). Westbound traffic was disrupted until nearly Noon EST. A 40-year-old male suffered minor injuries. A woman was seriously injured when she lost control of her sport utility vehicle on northbound Pennsylvania State Route 33 in Bethlehem Township (Northampton County) and rolled the vehicle on its roof. In Lehigh County, officials reported two accidents involving buses around 9 a.m. EST, both in Washington Township. One, at Old Mill and Main Streets, involved a school bus and another vehicle. The children on board were taken to the hospital as a precaution. The second accident, at Pennsylvania State Route 873 and Oakland Road, involved a LANTA bus carrying 15 passengers. No injuries were reported in either accident. |In the Poconos, several accidents occurred on the Northeast Extension of the Pennsylvania Turnpike. Accidents were also reported in Carbon County in Lehighton Borough and Franklin Township. In Monroe County, a school bus accident occurred at the intersection of Pennsylvania State Routes 423 and 196 in Coolbaugh Township. A 52-year-old woman could not stop her vehicle in time to avoid the stopped school bus at the intersection. No injuries occurred.  |In Berks County, a four vehicle accident that included a school bus caused minor injuries to seven children in South Heidelberg Township. A school bus, tractor-trailer, pick-up truck and minivan all collided. Accidents snarled traffic on Interstate 78. Numerous accidents were reported in both directions of the interstate between mile marker eight in Bethel Township and mile marker forty-three in Greenwich Township.| |Specific accumulations included 0.9 in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 0.6 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County) and 0.1 inches in Allentown/Lehigh Valley International Airport. This was the latest in the winter season for Allentown to record its first measurable snow.

Strong northwest to west winds occurred during the daytime hours on January 20th across Eastern Pennsylvania. It was caused by an intense pressure gradient (large difference in surface pressure over a relatively small area) between a powerful low pressure system (960 millibars at 7 a.m. EST on the 20th) moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building east from the Midwest. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 47 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, London Grove (Chester County) and Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 44 mph at the Willow Grove NAS (Montgomery County), 43 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 40 mph in Reading (Berks County).

An inverted trough developed along the Middle Atlantic coastal waters during the day on the 18th. The convergent flow near this trough helped some light snow to fall across the local Philadelphia during the afternoon of the 18th. It was the first widespread measurable snow of the winter season, even though all places accumulated less than half an inch. Temperatures were right around the freezing mark and the snow caused untreated bridges and overpasses to become slippery. In Bucks County, in Falls Township, twenty-one vehicles were involved in accidents on an icy railroad overpass on Lincoln Highway. Drivers had trouble stopping on the overpass as they started their downhill descent. Several people were injured and one was hospitalized because of a leg injury. There were other minor accidents during the evening commute on bridges and overpasses in lower Bucks County. Accumulations included 0.1 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

Snow showers that occurred preceding a cold front helped produce slippery conditions and numerous accidents during the morning commute across eastern Pennsylvania from Bucks County northward through the Poconos. Reported accumulations were all less than one inch, but the lack of snow so far this winter, the change in road conditions (since the snow showers did not affect all areas) and the confluence with the morning commute contributed to an abnormal increase in the number of accidents.|In Monroe County, eastbound Interstate 80 was closed from 530 a.m. EST through 300 p.m. EST after a multiple tractor-trailer accident. The first tractor-trailer jack-knifed. A second tractor-trailer could not stop in time and struck the first tractor-trailer and caught on fire. A third tractor-trailer veered off the interstate to avoid the collision and went down an embankment. All three drivers were injured and hospitalized. Traffic was detoured onto Interstate 380 and Pennsylvania State Route 940. Commutes took three times longer than normal. Schools had delayed openings.|There were also several accidents on Pennsylvania State Route 33 in both Monroe and Northampton Counties. There were delayed school openings in Northampton County also. In Northampton County, accidents were reported in Palmer and Upper Nazareth Townships. In Bucks County, many accidents occurred along U.S. Route 13. |Actual accumulations included 0.7 inches in Warminster (Bucks County), 0.4 inches in Feasterville (Bucks County), 0.3 inches in Macungie (Lehigh County) and the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 0.2 inches in Tannersville (Monroe County).

Snow squalls and heavy snow showers developed along a southward moving arctic cold front in the Poconos during the morning of the 25th. Within this band of heavier snow, accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches. Untreated roadways were hazardous. Specific accumulations included 2.8 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County) and 1.2 inches at the F.E. Walter Dam along the Carbon and Luzerne County border. More snow showers fell during the evening of the 25th, but were of lesser intensity than the morning snow showers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong northwest flow around an arctic high pressure system and because this high originated near the North Pole produced the coldest morning in two years across eastern Pennsylvania on the 26th. Most low temperatures that morning were in the single numbers, except they were below zero in the Poconos. Wind chill factors were below zero. The hourly wind chill factor at Mount Pocono bottomed at -21F. The extremely cold weather caused many municipalities in and around Philadelphia to declare a code blue. In Philadelphia, for example the cold blue declaration by the city's Office of Shelter Services sets into motion methods to assist the homeless population. Actual low temperatures included -7F at the F.E. Walter Dam on the Carbon/Luzerne County border, -3F in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 3F in Springtown (Bucks County), 4F in West Chester (Chester County), 6F at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 7F in Reading (Berks County) and Doylestown (Bucks County), 8F in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 10F at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast over Lake Erie during the early morning hours of the 19th. Strong northwest flow behind the cold front allowed for lake effect snow to develop across northwest Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of the 19th, and continue through the day. The snow tapered off during the overnight hours into the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow began during the early morning hours of the 19th, and continued through the day. The snow tapered off during the overnight hours into the 20th. The snow was heaviest during the daytime hours, with Cambridge Springs picking up 7.4 inches during the daylight hours on the 19th. A report of 11.3 inches of snow fell in Meadville during this event, and about 8 inches fell in Linesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved southeast across Northwest Pennsylvania on January 24th.  Very cold northwest winds behind the front caused lake effect snow showers to develop on the 24th and persist through most of the 25th.  The most significant accumulations occurred along and north of Interstate 90 in Erie County.  Dozens of accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed on the morning of January 24th and continued through the afternoon hours of the 25th.  Some of the heaviest snow fell during the morning hours of the 25th when snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour at times.  Storm total accumulations of 8 to 12 inches were reported in northern Erie County.  A peak of 14.0 inches was reported in the City of Erie.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with significant snowmelt caused numerous small streams to exceed bank-full. A number of roads were closed due to flooding. Areas that were most significantly impacted were Roulette, Shinglehouse and Genesee, all across northern and central portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with rapid snowmelt lead to flooding across portions of the northern tier counties of central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with significant snowmelt caused numerous small streams to exceed bank-full. A number of roads in the county were closed due to flooding. Areas that were most significantly impacted were around Sheffield, where Tionesta Creek and Twomile Creek merge. Flooding was also noted along Brokenstraw Creek near Youngsville. A number of roads and bridges were closed, including Route 666 near Sheffield. 4 homes sustained major damage in the Sheffield area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong northwest flow around an arctic high pressure system and because this high originated near the North Pole produced the coldest morning in two years across eastern Pennsylvania on the 26th. Most low temperatures that morning were in the single numbers, except they were below zero in the Poconos. Wind chill factors were below zero. The hourly wind chill factor at Mount Pocono bottomed at -21F. The extremely cold weather caused many municipalities in and around Philadelphia to declare a code blue. In Philadelphia, for example the cold blue declaration by the city's Office of Shelter Services sets into motion methods to assist the homeless population. Actual low temperatures included -7F at the F.E. Walter Dam on the Carbon/Luzerne County border, -3F in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 3F in Springtown (Bucks County), 4F in West Chester (Chester County), 6F at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 7F in Reading (Berks County) and Doylestown (Bucks County), 8F in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 10F at the Philadelphia International Airport.

The New Year began on a very wet note across Eastern Pennsylvania as a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico helped drop heavy rain across the state. Rain began falling around the start of the New Year and ended during by the early afternoon. The heaviest rain fell in and around the local Philadelphia area. This caused poor drainage flooding, as well as flooding of some of the creeks and streams in southeastern Pennsylvania. Storm totals averaged 1 to 2 inches with the lightest amounts in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley.|The slow moving frontal system that wrung the heavy rain across the region moved from the Tennessee Valley on the morning of December 31st, east to the Ohio Valley and southern Appalachians at 7 p.m. EST on the 31st, to the central Appalachians at 1 a.m. EST on January 1st. As the front started to cross the Pennsylvania, Maryland and West Virginia Appalachians during the morning of January 1st, a low pressure system formed on the triple point of the occluded front in northeastern Virginia around 7 a.m. EST on January 1st. The low pressure system then moved northeast through the Delmarva Peninsula and into New Jersey by the early afternoon of January 1st. At 7 p.m. EST on January 1st, the low pressure system reached western Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused sections of both the East and West Branch of the Brandywine Creek to flood. The West Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Honey Brook was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1008 a.m. EST through 1 p.m. EST on the 1st. It crested at 7.34 feet at 1115 a.m. EST. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 959 a.m. EST through 237 p.m. EST on the 1st. It crested at 8.59 feet at Noon EST. Storm totals within Chester County included 1.94 inches in Honey Brook, 1.78 inches in Glenmoore, 1.75 inches in Coatesville, 1.49 inches in Valley Forge and 1.46 inches in Exton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls and heavy snow showers developed along a southward moving arctic cold front in Berks, Chester and Delaware Counties during the evening of the 25th. Within this band of heavier snow, accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches, with lesser amounts elsewhere across southeastern Pennsylvania. Untreated roadways were hazardous. It was fortuitous that the heavy snow fell after the evening commute was done. Specific accumulations included 3.3 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County), 2.2 inches in Wallingford (Delaware County), 2.0 inches in Bernville, Lincoln Park and Morgantown (all Berks County) and Downingtown (Chester County), 1.6 inches in Coatesville (Chester County), 1.1 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County) and 0.3 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

In Philadelphia, for example the cold blue declaration by the city's Office of Shelter Services sets into motion methods to assist the homeless population. Actual low temperatures included -7F at the F.E. Walter Dam on the Carbon/Luzerne County border, -3F in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 3F in Springtown (Bucks County), 4F in West Chester (Chester County), 6F at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 7F in Reading (Berks County) and Doylestown (Bucks County), 8F in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 10F at the Philadelphia International Airport.

Snow squalls and heavy snow showers developed along a southward moving arctic cold front in the Poconos during the morning of the 25th. Within this band of heavier snow, accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches. Untreated roadways were hazardous. Specific accumulations included 2.8 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County) and 1.2 inches at the F.E. Walter Dam along the Carbon and Luzerne County border. More snow showers fell during the evening of the 25th, but were of lesser intensity than the morning snow showers.

The New Year began on a very wet note across Eastern Pennsylvania as a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico helped drop heavy rain across the state. Rain began falling around the start of the New Year and ended during by the early afternoon. The heaviest rain fell in and around the local Philadelphia area. This caused poor drainage flooding, as well as flooding of some of the creeks and streams in southeastern Pennsylvania. Storm totals averaged 1 to 2 inches with the lightest amounts in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley.|The slow moving frontal system that wrung the heavy rain across the region moved from the Tennessee Valley on the morning of December 31st, east to the Ohio Valley and southern Appalachians at 7 p.m. EST on the 31st, to the central Appalachians at 1 a.m. EST on January 1st. As the front started to cross the Pennsylvania, Maryland and West Virginia Appalachians during the morning of January 1st, a low pressure system formed on the triple point of the occluded front in northeastern Virginia around 7 a.m. EST on January 1st. The low pressure system then moved northeast through the Delmarva Peninsula and into New Jersey by the early afternoon of January 1st. At 7 p.m. EST on January 1st, the low pressure system reached western Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused flooding along the East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek in upper Bucks County. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Dublin was above its 5 foot flood stage from 715 a.m. EST through 929 a.m. EST on the 1st. It crested at 5.7 feet at 815 a.m. EST. Storm totals from Bucks County included 2.32 inches in Sellersville, 2.01 inches in Perkasie, 1.94 inches in Neshaminy Falls and 1.69 inches in Doylestown.

A lake effect snow band from Lake Erie that remained intact brought snow showers and snow squalls to the Poconos, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley during the morning commute. Accumulations were light, all reported amounts were less than one inch. But the combination of the snow melting and then water freezing on road surfaces plus the first snow event of the season (for some) led to many traffic accidents, especially in the Lehigh Valley and particularly in Northampton County. |Westbound Interstate 78 had just one lane opened because of several crashes between the New Jersey and Lehigh County lines. A van spun out of control on the icy roadway and struck a concrete barrier in Lower Saucon Township (Northampton County), closing the right lane for about an hour. One person in the van suffered a minor injury. All lanes were reopened by 11 a.m. EST. One vehicle rolled over on U.S. Route 22 in Palmer Township (Northampton County). Westbound traffic was disrupted until nearly Noon EST. A 40-year-old male suffered minor injuries. A woman was seriously injured when she lost control of her sport utility vehicle on northbound Pennsylvania State Route 33 in Bethlehem Township (Northampton County) and rolled the vehicle on its roof. In Lehigh County, officials reported two accidents involving buses around 9 a.m. EST, both in Washington Township. One, at Old Mill and Main Streets, involved a school bus and another vehicle. The children on board were taken to the hospital as a precaution. The second accident, at Pennsylvania State Route 873 and Oakland Road, involved a LANTA bus carrying 15 passengers. No injuries were reported in either accident. |In the Poconos, several accidents occurred on the Northeast Extension of the Pennsylvania Turnpike. Accidents were also reported in Carbon County in Lehighton Borough and Franklin Township. In Monroe County, a school bus accident occurred at the intersection of Pennsylvania State Routes 423 and 196 in Coolbaugh Township. A 52-year-old woman could not stop her vehicle in time to avoid the stopped school bus at the intersection. No injuries occurred.  |In Berks County, a four vehicle accident that included a school bus caused minor injuries to seven children in South Heidelberg Township. A school bus, tractor-trailer, pick-up truck and minivan all collided. Accidents snarled traffic on Interstate 78. Numerous accidents were reported in both directions of the interstate between mile marker eight in Bethel Township and mile marker forty-three in Greenwich Township.| |Specific accumulations included 0.9 in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 0.6 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County) and 0.1 inches in Allentown/Lehigh Valley International Airport. This was the latest in the winter season for Allentown to record its first measurable snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeast winds blowing down Lake Erie caused a reverse lake effect snow event along northern Erie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold northeast winds blowing down Lake Erie caused reverse lake effect snow bands to develop and move across northern Erie County.   This activity began during the early morning hours of the 5th and intensified after daybreak.  Visibilities during the mid morning hours were near zero with snowfall rates of up to 2 inches per hour reported.  The snow bands shifted west out of the area during the afternoon.  Snowfall totals across northern Erie County ranged from 6 to 10 inches north of Interstate 90 to around 6 inches to the south of the interstate.  Dozens of accidents and significant travel delays were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure moved northeast across Ohio and into Northwest Pennsylvania during the early afternoon hours of December 16th.  Precipitation associated with this low spread into northwestern Pennsylvania late in the afternoon of the 15th and continued till late on the 16th.  The precipitation started as mostly snow then transitioned to a mixture of rain and freezing rain late in the evening on the 15th.  This mixture changed back to all snow during the morning hours of the 16th.  Winds gusted to as much as 40 mph during the afternoon of the 16th causing near blizzard conditions.  Snow measurements were hampered by the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in Erie County during the late afternoon hours of December 15th. The snow changed to sleet and freezing rain during the evening hours. The precipitation eventually turned to rain for a few hours on the morning of the 16th. By mid morning on the 16th, the precipitation changed back to all snow with moderate to heavy snow then falling through the afternoon and into the evening. Winds gusted to 40 mph during the afternoon and evening of the 16th causing near blizzard conditions for several hours. Considerable blowing and drifting occurred with drifts over three feet deep reported. Snowfall totals during this event were around six inches over much of the county. Some ice accumulation was also reported. Travel was severely impacted by this storm with travel during the afternoon and evening of the 16th very difficult. Numerous accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, late season Nor'easter brought rain and heavy, wet snow to portions of Central Pennsylvania during April 16 through 17. Rain initially over the region changed to wet snow during the 16th, continuing into the early morning hours of the 17th. Heavy snowfall was confined to higher elevations, with warning criteria snow only observed along ridges. Valley locations received much lesser snow amounts. The heavy snow, combined with strong northwest winds of 20 to 40 mph caused numerous power outages across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the county Monday morning. Accumulations of snow ranged greatly across the county, with highest amounts of around 6 inches over the ridges. Johnstown reported 6 inches of snow with the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the nor'easter intensified as it headed into the New England coastal waters and the Gulf of Maine, strong northwest winds enveloped Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and the evening of the 14th. Peak wind gusts averaged between 40 and 55 mph and hampered snow and sleet removal efforts. Peak wind gusts included 52 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and London Grove (Chester County), 43 mph in Reading (Berks County), 39 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 33 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport.

Another strong northwest flow between a high pressure system building in from Canada and a low pressure system deepening over the Canadian Maritimes brought another unseasonably cold shot of air into the Poconos, Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and upper Bucks County.  Morning wind chills on the 19th were between zero and 10 degrees below zero in the valleys and as cold as around 20 degrees below zero over the higher terrain, in particular the Pocono Plateau. Actual low temperatures were in the positive single numbers. Since this occurred on President's Day, it did not have the same effect as a typical weekday day. Low temperatures included 1 degree in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 6 degrees in Springtown and Perkasie (Bucks County), 9 degrees in Easton (Northampton County), 10 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 11 degrees in Reading (Berks County).

A persistent northwest to west flow following the winter storm on February 13th and 14th culminated in blow-out tides around the times of low tide during the evening of the 16th and the morning of the 17th. The lowest tides occurred on the morning of the 17th. The new moon assisted, because spring tides cause the biggest differences in water levels between high and low tides. Low tide in Philadelphia reached 2.3 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water. The west flow subsided later in the day on the 17th.

In the wake of the February 13th and 14th nor'easter, unseasonably cold air was drawn southward into Pennsylvania on gusty west to northwest winds on the 15th and 16th. From upper Bucks County northward through the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, morning low temperature were mainly in the positive single numbers both days. Wind chill factors reached as low as 20 degrees below zero on the Pocono Plateau and between zero and 10 degrees below zero elsewhere. The unseasonably cold weather led to pipes being burst at the Snowshoe Condominium in Mount Pocono. One family was displaced. Actual low temperatures included 3 degrees below zero at the F.E. Walter Dam (Carbon County), 1 degree in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 4 degrees in Perkasie (Bucks County), 6 degrees in Springtown (Bucks County), 7 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 8 degrees in Hamburg (Berks County), 10 degrees in Easton (Northampton County) and 11 degrees in Reading (Berks County).

A strong, late season Nor'easter brought rain and heavy, wet snow to portions of Central Pennsylvania during April 16 through 17. Rain initially over the region changed to wet snow during the 16th, continuing into the early morning hours of the 17th. Heavy snowfall was confined to higher elevations, with warning criteria snow only observed along ridges. Valley locations received much lesser snow amounts. The heavy snow, combined with strong northwest winds of 20 to 40 mph caused numerous power outages across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the county Sunday evening. Significant accumulations of snow were confined to the higher elevations of the county, while valley locations saw significantly less snow. Reports ranged from 6.2 inches in Sabinsville and 5.0 inches in Covington, to between 2 and 3 inches in Wellsboro and at Cowanesque Dam.

Significant accumulations of snow were confined to the higher elevations of the county, while valley locations saw much less snow. While 13.9 inches of snow was reported on Laurel Summit, only 1.0 inch of snow was reported in Meyersdale. Somerset recorded 4.6 inches of snow, and Glencoe recorded 2.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, late season Nor'easter brought rain and heavy, wet snow to portions of Central Pennsylvania during April 16 through 17. Rain initially over the region changed to wet snow during the 16th, continuing into the early morning hours of the 17th. Heavy snowfall was confined to higher elevations, with warning criteria snow only observed along ridges. Valley locations received much lesser snow amounts. The heavy snow, combined with strong northwest winds of 20 to 40 mph caused numerous power outages across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the county Sunday evening. Significant accumulations of snow were confined to the higher elevations of the northwest portions of the county, with much lesser amounts in valley locations. Frackville and Ashland both recorded 11.0 inches of snow from the storm, while Mahanoy City recorded 9.8 inches. In the valleys, snow totals included 1 inch at Pine Grove and Pottsville.|Over 10,000 customers lost power in the Frackville area from the snow and wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass that originated near the North Pole invaded eastern Pennsylvania. The combination of the unseasonably cold air and gusty northwest winds produced wind chill factors as low as zero to 10 degrees below zero during the mornings of the 5th and 6th around Philadelphia, around 10 degrees below zero in the Lehigh Valley and Berks County and around 20 degrees below zero in the Poconos. The extreme cold caused the death of two males. A homeless man was found dead near the Falls Bridge in Philadelphia and a 25-year-old man was found dead in a driveway in Plymouth Township (Montgomery County). The lowest temperatures were in the single numbers across much of eastern Pennsylvania except for some below zero readings in the Poconos.  |The extreme cold caused many municipalities to declare Code Blue winter weather alerts. This sets into motion efforts by churches, local charitable organizations and outreach programs to open shelters and help assist getting the homeless indoors. Many schools in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos and had delayed openings both mornings. Many pipes froze across the region and many vehicles had dead batteries. Firefighters had a particular difficult time battling blazes in these conditions as in some instances water froze in tanker trucks and fire hydrants froze. The cold silenced the emergency alarm system in Nazareth Borough (Northampton County). The extreme cold helped Pennsylvania Power and Light set a new hourly wintertime usage record. between 8 a.m. and 9 a.m. EST on the 5th, 7,318 megawatts of electricity were used, breaking the previous record of 7,274 megawatts. Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop on February 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers intensified during the evening hours of February 22nd and continued through the early morning hours of the 23rd.  Storm total accumulations across northern Crawford County ranged from 6 to 10 inches.  A maximum of 12 inches was reported near Cambridge Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours into the 13th, an area of low pressure developed over Texas. This area of low pressure tracked northeast through the Tennesse Valley during the 13th, and then over the central Appalachians during the 14th. Strong winds and heavy snow accompanied this area of low pressure that affected the entire region. The snow finally ended across the region during the evening and overnight hours of the 14th into the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the midday hours of the 13th. The snow and gusty winds persisted through the overnight hours into the 14th, and finally diminished during the early evening on the 14th. By the end of the event, 13.1 inches fell in Fairview, 8.7 inches fell in Millcreek Township, and 16.0 inches was measured in Franklin Center. Over the two day period of the 13th and 14th, 11.6 inches of snow was measured at the Erie Airport. Winds were also gusty during the event with a peak wind gust measured at the Erie Airport of 37 mph during the early morning hours of the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the nor'easter intensified as it headed into the New England coastal waters and the Gulf of Maine, strong northwest winds enveloped Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and the evening of the 14th. Peak wind gusts averaged between 40 and 55 mph and hampered snow and sleet removal efforts. Peak wind gusts included 52 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and London Grove (Chester County), 43 mph in Reading (Berks County), 39 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 33 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport.

In the wake of the February 13th and 14th nor'easter, unseasonably cold air was drawn southward into Pennsylvania on gusty west to northwest winds on the 15th and 16th. From upper Bucks County northward through the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, morning low temperature were mainly in the positive single numbers both days. Wind chill factors reached as low as 20 degrees below zero on the Pocono Plateau and between zero and 10 degrees below zero elsewhere. The unseasonably cold weather led to pipes being burst at the Snowshoe Condominium in Mount Pocono. One family was displaced. Actual low temperatures included 3 degrees below zero at the F.E. A renewed strong northwest flow coupled with the astronomical spring (low) tides around the new moon caused blow-out tides around the times of the morning low tide on the 19th. The low tide In Philadelphia reached 2.53 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water. Unlike previous blow-out tides this month, the northwest flow diminished quickly and was only a one low tide cycle event.

A strong cold front moved through eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 22nd. The northwest flow between a deepening low pressure at the head of the front and a high pressure system building southeast from Canada intensified during the night of the 22nd. The strongest wind gusts occurred in Chester County. Lower winds occurred elsewhere across the eastern part of state and higher winds occurred farther south in Delmarva. Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in London Grove (Chester County), 45 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 43 mph in Reading (Berks County) and 39 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport.

In the wake of the February 13th and 14th nor'easter, unseasonably cold air was drawn southward into Pennsylvania on gusty west to northwest winds on the 15th and 16th. From upper Bucks County northward through the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, morning low temperature were mainly in the positive single numbers both days. Wind chill factors reached as low as 20 degrees below zero on the Pocono Plateau and between zero and 10 degrees below zero elsewhere. The unseasonably cold weather led to pipes being burst at the Snowshoe Condominium in Mount Pocono. One family was displaced. Actual low temperatures included 3 degrees below zero at the F.E. Walter Dam (Carbon County), 1 degree in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 4 degrees in Perkasie (Bucks County), 6 degrees in Springtown (Bucks County), 7 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 8 degrees in Hamburg (Berks County), 10 degrees in Easton (Northampton County) and 11 degrees in Reading (Berks County).

A low pressure system developed over the southern plains on February 12th, and intensified rapidly as it neared the East Coast on the night of the 13th. The storm then continued to strengthen as it moved up the Atlantic Seaboard during the day of February 14th. The storm spread snow into northeast Pennsylvania beginning in the afternoon on Tuesday, the 13th. Snow mixed with, and then changed to sleet across all of northeast Pennsylvania, except in Bradford, Wyoming and western Susquehanna counties, during the morning hours of the 14th. The sleet changed back to snow which became very heavy by the afternoon of the 14th. Snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour were reported. The high snowfall intensity wreaked havoc with snow plowing operations and closed several major interstates in northeast Pennsylvania, stranding hundreds of motorists for hours creating a state of emergency in Lackawanna and Luzerne counties. The interstates that were closed were 80, 81, 380 and the northeast extension of the Pennsylvania Turnpike. At one point Interstate 81 was closed from the Scranton area north to the New York border. The snow gradually tapered off by the 15th as the storm pulled northeast into the Gulf of Maine. Storm total snowfall amounts across much of northeast Pennsylvania ranged generally between 1 and 2 feet. The exception was Pike county where much more sleet occurred holding snow amounts down to between 6 and 12 inches. The heaviest snowfall in northeast Pennsylvania occurred over the higher terrain of northern Lackawanna, western Wayne and southeast Susquehanna counties where between 24 and 30 inches of snow fell. The weight of the snow and sleet caused a few roofs to collapse as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and sleet caused Interstate 81 and the northeast extension of the Pennsylvania Turnpike to close. The county declared a state of emergency.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the southern plains on February 12th, and intensified rapidly as it neared the East Coast on the night of the 13th. The storm then continued to strengthen as it moved up the Atlantic Seaboard during the day of February 14th. The storm spread snow into northeast Pennsylvania beginning in the afternoon on Tuesday, the 13th. Snow mixed with, and then changed to sleet across all of northeast Pennsylvania, except in Bradford, Wyoming and western Susquehanna counties, during the morning hours of the 14th. The sleet changed back to snow which became very heavy by the afternoon of the 14th. Snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour were reported. The high snowfall intensity wreaked havoc with snow plowing operations and closed several major interstates in northeast Pennsylvania, stranding hundreds of motorists for hours creating a state of emergency in Lackawanna and Luzerne counties. The interstates that were closed were 80, 81, 380 and the northeast extension of the Pennsylvania Turnpike. At one point Interstate 81 was closed from the Scranton area north to the New York border. The snow gradually tapered off by the 15th as the storm pulled northeast into the Gulf of Maine. Storm total snowfall amounts across much of northeast Pennsylvania ranged generally between 1 and 2 feet. The exception was Pike county where much more sleet occurred holding snow amounts down to between 6 and 12 inches. The heaviest snowfall in northeast Pennsylvania occurred over the higher terrain of northern Lackawanna, western Wayne and southeast Susquehanna counties where between 24 and 30 inches of snow fell. The weight of the snow and sleet caused a few roofs to collapse as well.

A winter storm that featured a potpourri of precipitation types affected Eastern Pennsylvania from the afternoon of the 25th into the early morning of the 26th. Snowfall accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches. Ice accretions occurred from the Lehigh Valley southeastward and averaged one-tenth to one-quarter of an inch with the highest accretions in Philadelphia and its Pennsylvania suburbs. Precipitation started as snow during the afternoon of the 25th. In the local Philadelphia area, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain during the early evening. Precipitation fell as freezing rain from the middle of the evening onward until it changed to plain rain around 2 a.m. EST on the 26th. In Berks County and the Philadelphia Pennsylvania suburbs, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain during the middle of the evening and then to just freezing rain in the late evening. Temperatures never rose above freezing and precipitation ended as freezing rain. Some locations in Berks County changed back to snow before precipitation ended. In the Lehigh Valley, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain around Midnight EST on the 26th and then changed back to snow before it ended. In the Poconos, precipitation fell as mainly snow. All precipitation ended across the eastern part of the state between 3 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST.  |Untreated roads and walkways were hazardous. Several suburban Philadelphia municipalities declared snow emergencies. The snow did accumulate on road surfaces as the weather had remained unseasonably cold and many accidents occurred. In Delaware County, Interstate 95 was closed near the Delaware state border for 30 minutes after a vehicle struck a guard rail and spun across the roadway. Many accidents also occurred on Interstate 95 in Bucks County. Some vehicles flipped over across the county. In Montgomery County, several accidents shut down Westbound Route 422 at the Armand-Hammer Boulevard Interchange. In Chester County, portions of Pennsylvania State Route 100 (in Exton) and northbound and southbound lanes of U.S. Route 202 were closed because of multiple accidents. Multiple accidents also occurred on the Pennsylvania Turnpike between Morgantown (Berks County) and Norristown (Montgomery County). In Lehigh County, accidents on Interstate 78 caused a closure and detours. Two serious crashes in Lynn Township on Pennsylvania State Route 143 caused the road to be closed until the afternoon of the 26th.  Schools in the Poconos had two hour delayed openings on the 26th, a couple in Carbon County were closed. Two hour delayed openings occurred as far south as Montgomery County.  | |Snowfall accumulations included 3.2 inches in Middle Smithfield (Monroe County), 3.0 inches in Phoenixville (Chester County) and Lehighton (Carbon County), 2.5 inches in Havertown (Delaware County) and Downingtown (Chester County), 2.2 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 2.1 inches in Palm (Montgomery County), 2.0 inches in Riegelsville (Bucks County) and Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 1.8 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 1.7 inches in Sinking Spring (Berks County) and 1.5 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and Neshaminy Falls (Bucks County).    |The low pressure system that caused the winter storm moved northeast from the foothills of the central Rockies on the morning of the 24th. At 7 p.m. EST on the 24th it reached Kansas City, Kansas City and then northeastern Iowa at 7 a.m. EST on the 25th. A secondary low pressure system started forming on the primary low's warm front in South Carolina during the early afternoon of the 25th. At 7 p.m. EST the secondary low was near Raleigh, North Carolina, while the primary low pressure system was weakening near Chicago, Illinois. At 1 a.m. EST on the 26th, the secondary low pressure system was just east of the Delaware coast and at 7 a.m. EST that morning was already well southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. While warm air continued to move in aloft because of the circulation associated with the primary low pressure system, the formation and deepening of the secondary low pressure system slowed the advancement of warmer air near the surface and prolonged the duration of sleet and freezing rain inland, especially in southeastern Pennsylvania. The secondary low pressure system moved offshore too quickly to bring heavy snow into the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos.

At 7 p.m. EST the secondary low was near Raleigh, North Carolina, while the primary low pressure system was weakening near Chicago, Illinois. At 1 a.m. EST on the 26th, the secondary low pressure system was just east of the Delaware coast and at 7 a.m. EST that morning was already well southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. While warm air continued to move in aloft because of the circulation associated with the primary low pressure system, the formation and deepening of the secondary low pressure system slowed the advancement of warmer air near the surface and prolonged the duration of sleet and freezing rain inland, especially in southeastern Pennsylvania. The secondary low pressure system moved offshore too quickly to bring heavy snow into the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos.

A winter storm that featured a potpourri of precipitation types affected Eastern Pennsylvania from the afternoon of the 25th into the early morning of the 26th. Snowfall accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches. Ice accretions occurred from the Lehigh Valley southeastward and averaged one-tenth to one-quarter of an inch with the highest accretions in Philadelphia and its Pennsylvania suburbs. Precipitation started as snow during the afternoon of the 25th. In the local Philadelphia area, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain during the early evening. Precipitation fell as freezing rain from the middle of the evening onward until it changed to plain rain around 2 a.m. EST on the 26th. In Berks County and the Philadelphia Pennsylvania suburbs, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain during the middle of the evening and then to just freezing rain in the late evening. Temperatures never rose above freezing and precipitation ended as freezing rain. Some locations in Berks County changed back to snow before precipitation ended. In the Lehigh Valley, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain around Midnight EST on the 26th and then changed back to snow before it ended. In the Poconos, precipitation fell as mainly snow. All precipitation ended across the eastern part of the state between 3 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST.  |Untreated roads and walkways were hazardous. Several suburban Philadelphia municipalities declared snow emergencies. The snow did accumulate on road surfaces as the weather had remained unseasonably cold and many accidents occurred. In Delaware County, Interstate 95 was closed near the Delaware state border for 30 minutes after a vehicle struck a guard rail and spun across the roadway. Many accidents also occurred on Interstate 95 in Bucks County. Some vehicles flipped over across the county. In Montgomery County, several accidents shut down Westbound Route 422 at the Armand-Hammer Boulevard Interchange. In Chester County, portions of Pennsylvania State Route 100 (in Exton) and northbound and southbound lanes of U.S. Route 202 were closed because of multiple accidents. Multiple accidents also occurred on the Pennsylvania Turnpike between Morgantown (Berks County) and Norristown (Montgomery County). In Lehigh County, accidents on Interstate 78 caused a closure and detours. Two serious crashes in Lynn Township on Pennsylvania State Route 143 caused the road to be closed until the afternoon of the 26th. An area of low pressure tracked across southeastern Canada during the 12th, which brought an arctic front across the Mid Atlantic region late on the 12th. As this occurred, a strong area of high pressure located across central Canada built down into the Northeast, allowing very cold air to spill southward ultimately leading to what is known as cold air damming. This occurs east of the mountains down through the Mid Atlantic states. Meanwhile, a Pacific storm slammed onto the California coast during the 11th and as it moved eastward, a new area of low pressure developed across southeastern Colorado and the Texas Panhandle. This storm became impressive as it moved into the Ohio Valley during the 13th. However as additional energy moved into the eastern part of the country, a new area of low pressure developed near the North Carolina coast late on the 13th. This allowed mainly light snow to overspread the region from south to north during the early morning hours of the 13th. As the secondary area of low pressure became more organized to our south, moisture began to fill in across North Carolina and Virginia and this moved northward. The arctic front that slipped through the state on the 12th was positioned across the southern Delmarva and this began a northward jog during the night of the 13th and the morning of the 14th. The intensifying area of low pressure tracked very near southern Delaware and then slid close to the New Jersey Shore during the morning of the 14th. This system continued to intensify as it moved into New England during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. The track of the storm kept winds at the surface from the northeast, which pretty much locked in the cold air at the surface while some warmer air worked in above the surface for a period of time. The upper-level system then passed overhead during the evening of the 14th, with the entire system pretty much a memory by late in the evening of the 14th. Governor Ed Rendell declared a disaster emergency across the entire state on the 15th.  PPL Electric Utilities Corporation reported 1,542 customers were without power for a time during the storm in the Lancaster, Harrisburg and Allentown areas.|Snow began across between 6 AM and 7 AM EST on the 13th in Philadelphia, Delaware, Chester, Montgomery and Berks Counties. There was a lull in the precipitation during the evening hours of the 13th especially across southeastern Pennsylvania, however the precipitation became widespread overnight and during the early morning hours of the 14th. As some warmer air began to move in above the surface, the snow mixed with and changed to sleet and freezing rain between 6 PM and 7 PM EST on the 13th in Philadelphia and Delaware Counties, then between 7 PM and 11 PM across most other areas in eastern Pennsylvania. The southern Poconos saw less mixed precipitation, however there was a period of mixed precipitation between about 7:30 PM on the 13th and 11:30 AM on the 14th. An area of low pressure tracked across southeastern Canada during the 12th, which brought an arctic front across the Mid Atlantic region late on the 12th. As this occurred, a strong area of high pressure located across central Canada built down into the Northeast, allowing very cold air to spill southward ultimately leading to what is known as cold air damming. This occurs east of the mountains down through the Mid Atlantic states. Meanwhile, a Pacific storm slammed onto the California coast during the 11th and as it moved eastward, a new area of low pressure developed across southeastern Colorado and the Texas Panhandle. This storm became impressive as it moved into the Ohio Valley during the 13th. However as additional energy moved into the eastern part of the country, a new area of low pressure developed near the North Carolina coast late on the 13th. This allowed mainly light snow to overspread the region from south to north during the early morning hours of the 13th. As the secondary area of low pressure became more organized to our south, moisture began to fill in across North Carolina and Virginia and this moved northward. The arctic front that slipped through the state on the 12th was positioned across the southern Delmarva and this began a northward jog during the night of the 13th and the morning of the 14th. The intensifying area of low pressure tracked very near southern Delaware and then slid close to the New Jersey Shore during the morning of the 14th. This system continued to intensify as it moved into New England during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. The track of the storm kept winds at the surface from the northeast, which pretty much locked in the cold air at the surface while some warmer air worked in above the surface for a period of time. The upper-level system then passed overhead during the evening of the 14th, with the entire system pretty much a memory by late in the evening of the 14th. Snow fell during the evening commute on the 2nd across most of eastern Pennsylvania. Precipitation actually started as rain across the Philadelphia northwester suburbs, but changed to snow by 5 p.m. EST. Elsewhere precipitation started as snow around 3 p.m. EST. The steady snow ended by 8 or 9 p.m. EST that evening.   Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches and caused slippery traveling conditions on untreated roads. This was compounded by it occurring during the evening commute. Actual accumulations included 3.5 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 3.2 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 3.0 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 2.6 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.5 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County) and Springtown (Bucks County), 2.3 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 2.2 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 1.7 inches in Coatesville (Chester County), 1.5 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 1.0 inch in Skippack (Montgomery County). While snow did also fall closer to Philadelphia, accumulations were less than an inch. The snow was caused by the combination of a cold front and a vigorous upper air disturbance that moved through the state.

From early morning of the 13th to early morning on the 14th a large and powerful storm system moved east from the western Ohio Valley to the Mid Atlantic states. Across western Pennsylvania, much of southeast Ohio, parts of northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, the storm system produced heavy snow of 6 to 12 inches, sleet accumulations of a half inch to 3 inches, and freezing rain accumulations from one quarter to one half inch.

A persistent northwest flow started on February 4th and continued through February 6th. This caused blowout tides around the times of low tide from the mid day low tide on February 5th through the two subsequent low tides on February 6th along the Delaware River and tidal sections of its tributaries. The lowest tides occurred on February 5th. Coming off of the full moon assisted, because spring tides cause the biggest differences in water levels between high and low tides. Low tide in Philadelphia reached 2.8 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water. The northwest flow subsided later in the day on February 6th and the tide cycle was also further removed from spring tides.

Precipitation actually started as rain across the Philadelphia northwester suburbs, but changed to snow by 5 p.m. EST. Elsewhere precipitation started as snow around 3 p.m. EST. The steady snow ended by 8 or 9 p.m. EST that evening.   Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches and caused slippery traveling conditions on untreated roads. This was compounded by it occurring during the evening commute. Actual accumulations included 3.5 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 3.2 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 3.0 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 2.6 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.5 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County) and Springtown (Bucks County), 2.3 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 2.2 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 1.7 inches in Coatesville (Chester County), 1.5 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 1.0 inch in Skippack (Montgomery County). While snow did also fall closer to Philadelphia, accumulations were less than an inch. The snow was caused by the combination of a cold front and a vigorous upper air disturbance that moved through the state.

Across western Pennsylvania, much of southeast Ohio, parts of northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, the storm system produced heavy snow of 6 to 12 inches, sleet accumulations of a half inch to 3 inches, and freezing rain accumulations from one quarter to one half inch.

The rain changed to snow across the higher terrain of the Poconos shortly after Midnight EDT on the 16th. Precipitation fell as snow during heavier bursts during the day on the 16th in the valleys also, but much of the accumulation (around 3 to 6 inches) occurred across the Pocono Plateau. The snow tapered off by the evening of the 16th. The snow caused a few accidents and closed or caused delayed openings for schools on the 16th, particularly on the plateau. A jack-knifed tractor-trailer caused traffic to back-up during the afternoon of the 16th on Interstate 380 north of Mount Pocono (Monroe County). A few other vehicles slid off the interstate in that area. In Penn Forest Township (Carbon County), a vehicle slid off the road after driving over a snow drift and crashed into a disabled vehicle. No serious injuries occurred. Accumulations included 7.4 inches in Blakeslee (Monroe County), 6.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 5.0 inches in Penn Forest Township (Carbon County), 4.0 inches in Summit Hill (Carbon County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County).

An intense area of low pressure on the 1st moved into the Upper Midwest with a cold front stretched southward across the Ohio Valley all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. Meanwhile, another cold front moved through the Northeast and settled into the Mid Atlantic region. This boundary remained stationary for a time during the overnight hours of the 1st, as the strong low pressure system moved into the western Great Lakes. Gradually an occluded front moved through the area during the morning hours of the 2nd, as a weak area of low pressure developed over eastern Pennsylvania. Along and ahead of this boundary and weak area of low pressure, an abundance of moisture was brought northward across our area. This lead to 1 to 3 inches of rain late on the 1st into the 2nd, and when combined with melting piles of snow in some areas, mainly minor flooding of streams, creeks and some roads resulted across eastern Pennsylvania. Some of the flooding closed several roads in the region.|The rain began between 7:45 PM and 9:00 PM EST on the 1st and became heavy at times, then tapered off generally between 9:00 AM and 10:00 AM EST on the 2nd. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm affected much of Central Pennsylvania on December 13th, producing a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain. Moderate to heavy snow fell across the North Central Mountains north and east of Interstate 80 and Route 219. Further south across the Central Mountains, snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain. Freezing rain was the dominant precipitation type from the Laurel Highlands eastward into the Susquehanna Valley with over a quarter inch of ice build up on trees and wires.||The storm resulted in a wide range of of emergencies including several vehicle accidents, road closures, and downed utility lines. The downed lines lead to sporadic power outages statewide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of sleet and freezing rain lead to significant ice accretion across Cumberland County. The ice build-up lead to very slick roads which contributed to several vehicle accidents. One injury was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snowmelt on the 2nd in West Penn Township caused flooding of many roads and basements. A power outage lasted more than three hours, which affected the West Penn Elementary School, a mini mart on Route 309 and 895, along with a day care and other businesses and residences. PPL Electric reported the outage was a result of a water soaked tree limb that took down a phase wire. The outage occurred at 6:04 AM EST on the 2nd, with the power restored at 9:23 AM EST. With the water covering many roads, travel was hampered as motorists had to avoid hydroplaning. In Mahoning Township, heavy erosion due to rainfall runoff occurred on the shoulder of Twin Crest Drive at Keiser Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The rain changed to snow across the higher terrain of the Poconos shortly after Midnight EDT on the 16th. Precipitation fell as snow during heavier bursts during the day on the 16th in the valleys also, but much of the accumulation (around 3 to 6 inches) occurred across the Pocono Plateau. The snow tapered off by the evening of the 16th. The snow caused a few accidents and closed or caused delayed openings for schools on the 16th, particularly on the plateau. A jack-knifed tractor-trailer caused traffic to back-up during the afternoon of the 16th on Interstate 380 north of Mount Pocono (Monroe County). A few other vehicles slid off the interstate in that area. In Penn Forest Township (Carbon County), a vehicle slid off the road after driving over a snow drift and crashed into a disabled vehicle. No serious injuries occurred. Accumulations included 7.4 inches in Blakeslee (Monroe County), 6.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 5.0 inches in Penn Forest Township (Carbon County), 4.0 inches in Summit Hill (Carbon County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County).

A wildfire consumed about 11 acres of woodland on the south side of South Mountain in Lower Saucon Township (Northampton County). The fire occurred at the corner of the Lehigh University Mountaintop Campus. About 100 emergency responders from four counties helped extinguish the blaze. The fire was reported about 915 a.m. EDT, under control at Noon EDT and was extinguished at 345 p.m. EDT on the 25th. There were apparently two fires that occurred between North Mountain Drive and Seidersville Road that merged into one larger blaze. The recent dry weather, the late start for the vegetation and the rocky terrain made it difficult to battle the fire.

Widespread thunderstorms affected central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of June 1, 2007. Some of these thunderstorms developed into pulse severe storms, causing large hail and isolated reports of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down several large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continued strong west to northwest winds from the 5th into the 6th produced blow out tides during the morning and evening low tides on the 6th along the Delaware River. The lowest tides occurred during the morning low tide. The morning low tide reached 3.35 feet below mean lower low water in Philadelphia. Blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water. The winds subsided the evening of the 6th and there were no further blow out tides the next day.

Strong west to northwest winds occurred across Eastern Pennsylvania from the afternoon of the 5th into the morning of the 6th. The strongest winds occurred in two surges: when the front first moved through during the afternoon of the 5th and then overnight when the cold air advection was at its peak. The air mass was very unseasonably cold for early March. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph. The strong winds knocked out glass from an upper story window of a vacant downtown Allentown (Lehigh County) building and narrowly missed a man and woman that were walking on the sidewalk. The strong winds also knocked down a few weak trees. Peak wind gusts included 53 mph in London Grove (Chester County), 51 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 47 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and Easton (Northampton County), 46 mph at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 45 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Reading (Berks County)and 41 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

A widespread rainfall occurred from March 15th to 16th as a slow-moving cold front dropped south from Canada and crossed northern Pennsylvania from the evening of the 14th to the morning of the 15th. The rain fell on a melting snowpack which caused many rivers, and creeks to exceed flood stage.  Rainfall of 0.75 to 1.50 inches was observed in the Susquehanna and Chemung River basins. Rainfall of 0.50 to 1.00 inches was observed in the Delaware River basin. Rivers generally crested 0.5 to 2.0 feet above flood stage. This amounted to minor flooding at most locations.  |The Susquehanna River at Conklin, New York was the only location to report significant flood damage. The river at Conklin, NY crested just above the moderate flood stage of 13.0 feet the evening of the 15th. 20 homes (6 occupied), 1 business, and 5 roads were flooded. An ice jam was associated with this flooding in New York State just north of the Pennsylvania border. There was some flooding from this ice jam just south of the New York border in the Great Bend-Hallstead area on the Susquehanna River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Susquehanna River at Towanda from the afternoon hours of March 15th to the morning of the 16th. The river at Towanda crested at 17.5 feet which is 1.5 feet over the flood stage of 16 feet. Over an inch of rain combining with snowmelt caused the minor flooding.

Unseasonably cold west-northwest flow over Lake Erie produced 2 to 5 inches of lake-effect snow across portions of the Northwest Mountains in Central Pennsylvania with higher amounts observed over the Snowbelt region of northwest Warren County. In addition, an intense snow band developed over portions of the Central Mountains producing a narrow swath of 2 to as much as 8 inches of snow in a 4-5 hour period from around midnight on the 8th through 5am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of lake-effect snow showers accumulated to 2 to 4 inches across much of Warren county. Higher amounts were observed over the Snowbelt region with 6 inches of snow measured in Youngsville.
