Low pressure gathered strength and moisture while tracking northeast over the Carolinas and eastern Virginia during the morning and afternoon of the 16th. This low reached the Delmarva peninsula by the evening of the 16th and intensified further becoming a major late season winter storm. This storm spread snow, heavy at times, to upstate New York during the morning and early afternoon of the 16th. The snow ended by the early morning hours of the 17th as the storm tracked out to sea well away from the region. Total snowfall accumulations range from 10 to 15 inches over the Poconos of northeast PA to between 5 and 10 inches across the rest of northeast PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 0.50 to 1.00 inches occurred on the night of the 24th as low pressure tracked across central NY. In addition, mild temperatures remained over the region through the end of the month. The mild temperatures continued melting of snowpack. This amounted to a period of prolonged minor flooding at a few river forecast points from the 25th to the 30th. For much of this time, river levels hovered close to or slightly above flood stage along the main branch of the Susquehanna from Waverly/Sayre east through the Binghamton area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Susquehanna River at Waverly/Sayre from the morning of March 27th to the evening of the 29th. The river at Waverly/Sayre crested at 11.1 feet which was .1 feet over the flood stage of 11 feet. Continued melting of the snowpack and a period of rainfall on the 24th and 25th led to the minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving pulse thunderstorms produced isolated hail and downburst wind damage across portions of Central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported penny size hail at Beaver Stadium on the University Park Campus of Penn State, at the intersection of Porter Road and Park Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving pulse thunderstorms produced isolated hail and downburst wind damage across portions of Central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail in Lewistown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving pulse thunderstorms produced isolated hail and downburst wind damage across portions of Central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: PennDot reported several trees down on Route 305.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving pulse thunderstorms produced isolated hail and downburst wind damage across portions of Central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Centre County Emergency Manager reported trees down in State College at 415 pm. Additionally, 18 utility poles were snapped and power lines were blown down on Park Avenue (Penn State University). A traffic light was also knocked down and damaged. The power lines landed on two vehicles, but the passengers were extracted successfully and sustained no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread rainfall occurred from March 15th to 16th as a slow-moving cold front dropped south from Canada and crossed northern Pennsylvania from the evening of the 14th to the morning of the 15th. The rain fell on a melting snowpack which caused many rivers, and creeks to exceed flood stage.  Rainfall of 0.75 to 1.50 inches was observed in the Susquehanna and Chemung River basins. Rainfall of 0.50 to 1.00 inches was observed in the Delaware River basin. Rivers generally crested 0.5 to 2.0 feet above flood stage. This amounted to minor flooding at most locations.  |The Susquehanna River at Conklin, New York was the only location to report significant flood damage. The river at Conklin, NY crested just above the moderate flood stage of 13.0 feet the evening of the 15th. 20 homes (6 occupied), 1 business, and 5 roads were flooded. An ice jam was associated with this flooding in New York State just north of the Pennsylvania border. There was some flooding from this ice jam just south of the New York border in the Great Bend-Hallstead area on the Susquehanna River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Susquehanna River at Wilkes-Barre from the early morning hours of March 16th to the early morning hours of the 17th. The river at Wilkes-Barre crested at 23.0 feet which is 1.0 feet over the flood stage of 22 feet. Over an inch of rain combining with snowmelt caused the minor flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported multiple trees down in Stewartstown. One tree fell onto and damaged a 2 story house, while another tree fell onto Route 83 at mile marker 6, blocking the roadway. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense squall line produced widespread wind damage across Central Pennsylvania during the evening hours on June 8, 2007. Numerous trees and power lines were downed causing several power outages and other sporadic damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph recorded on weather station located on roof of NWS office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nor'easter caused heavy sleet to fall across the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan Area, heavy snow and sleet to fall across Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and heavy snow in the Poconos on the 16th into the early morning of the 17th. The winter storm caused scores of accidents. Vehicles rolled over, slid off roads, slid into each other, slammed into guardrails and fishtailed. The afternoon and evening commute slowed to a crawl. A few schools closed. Most of the others had early dismissals. Many state offices and county courts closed early. Many after school activities were postponed. Many municipalities declared snow emergencies. A couple of Saint Patrick's Day parades scheduled for Saturday the 17th were also postponed. The winter storm wreaked havoc at Philadelphia International Airport as most flights on the 16th were cancelled and it took a couple of days for flights to return to normal. The Pennsylvania Interscholastic Athletic Association postponed several state playoff high school basketball games. Accumulations averaged 4 to 6 inches in the local Philadelphia area, 5 to 9 inches in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and 12 to 18 inches in the Poconos.|Precipitation started as rain across the region during the evening of the 15th. But as colder air moved in aloft, precipitation changed quickly to snow in the Poconos around Midnight EDT on the 16th and in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley around 7 a.m. EDT. Farther to the south, the surge of cold air was confined to a more shallower layer and precipitation changed to sleet around the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area between 6 a.m. and 9 a.m. EDT. Precipitation continued as mainly sleet across the greater Philadelphia through the evening. In Berks County and the Lehigh Valley, the snow mixed with and changed over to sleet during the later afternoon and the first half of the evening before it went back to all snow. Precipitation ended early in the day on the 17th (before 3 a.m. EDT) as mainly snow in all areas.|The only reported traffic fatality from the storm in Eastern Pennsylvania occurred in Bucks County. An 18-year-old girl from Plumstead Township was killed when her vehicle crossed the center line of an icy road Durham Road and collided with a dump truck. Elsewhere in Bucks County, portions of the Northeast Extension of the Pennsylvania Turnpike near Quakertown were stalled when two tractor-trailers collided in the southbound lanes at 2 p.m. EDT. A six mile back-up occurred. About 1,900 homes and businesses lost power in Newtown after a vehicle struck a pole.  |In the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area, about 265 schools dismissed early and more than 60 community events were either cancelled or postponed. The largest impact of the winter storm within Philadelphia was the cancellation of most of the 1,200 scheduled flights at the Philadelphia International Airport. About 1,000 people were stranded at the airport the night of the 16th. Passengers on about 15 U.S. Airways planes sat on the tarmac for over four hours before gates became available to deplane them. Operations resumed on the 17th, but U.S. Airways still had to cancel about one quarter of its flights because the weather prevented crews and planes from arriving in Philadelphia. The Philadelphia Flyers hockey team was forced to fly out of Atlantic City International. About 100 travelers slept at the airport on the night of the 18th. Normal operations resumed on the 19th. The sleet forced the closure of the eastbound lanes of the Vine Expressway within the city for about half an hour between the Schuylkill Expressway and Interstate 95 for its removal. The horse racing card at Philadelphia Park was cancelled for three days. |In Montgomery County, in Montgomery Township, an accident on Pennsylvania State Route 309 and Taylor Road badly injured one person. In Towamencin Township, two accidents resulted in two injuries. In Chester County, the state police reported 46 accidents in the central part of the county, but only one reported injury. A woman was hospitalized after a crash on Pennsylvania State Route 113 in Phoenixville. Several businesses in the county closed early. In Berks County, in Union Township one vehicle slid off a road and landed upside down in a creek. The driver was treated for non-threatening life injuries. A serious accident occurred on Old U.S. Route 22 in Lenhartsville.|The Lehigh Valley had a number of serious accidents on Interstate 78. In Lehigh County, a tractor-trailer jack-knifed near Pennsylvania State Route 100 at Fogelsville and closed the interstate from 330 p.m. EDT through 515 p.m. EDT. Both southbound lanes of the Pennsylvania Turnpike Northeast Extension were closed from 155 p.m. EDT through 420 p.m. EDT after two tractor-trailers and a car collided in Lower Milford Township. Many flights at the Lehigh Valley International Airport were delayed, a couple were cancelled. Commuter buses bringing workers home from New York City had long delays. In Northampton County, a tractor-trailer jack-knifed on the eastbound lanes of Interstate 78 about 4 p.m. EDT near the Pennsylvania State Route 33 junction. All lanes were not reopened until 545 p.m. EDT. In Moore Township, a school bus collided head-on with a car. Both drivers, but no children were injured. In Lower Mount Bethel Township, a Jeep Cherokee slid into a home on Pennsylvania State Route 611 and injured the driver. A Ford Explorer driver was injured after the vehicle struck a tree in Wind Gap. Problems on Lehigh Valley roadways continued long after the snow and sleet ended. A driver was injured on U.S. Route 22 in Whitehall Township (Lehigh County) when ice chunks from a tractor-trailer hit their vehicle. In Upper Macungie Township (Lehigh County), ice chunks that flew off a Wal-Mart tractor-trailer cracked the windshield and dented the hood of a vehicle on Interstate 78 near the Pennsylvania State Route 100 exit. In the Poconos, many malls closed early. In Monroe County, a flipped over truck snarled traffic on U.S. Route 209 and Pennsylvania State Route 33. A jack-knifed tractor-trailer on eastbound Interstate 80 near Stroudsburg snarled the evening commute to a crawl.|Snow and sleet totals included 18.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County) and Effort (Monroe County), 15.4 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 14.5 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 14.2 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 10.0 inches in Slatington (Lehigh County), 8.5 inches in Reading (Berks County) and at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 6.5 inches in Birdsboro (Berks County) and Springtown (Bucks County), 6.1 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County), 6.0 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), 5.7 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County), 5.5 inches in Elkins Park and King of Prussia (both Montgomery County), 5.3 inches in Marshalls Creek (Northampton County), 5.0 inches in Broomall (Delaware County) and Bethlehem (Northampton County), 4.8 inches in Roxborough (Philadelphia County), 4.0 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County) and Neshaminy Falls (Bucks County), 3.8 inches in West Chester (Chester County), 3.1 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County)and 3.0 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.  |The winter storm was caused by a nor'easter low pressure system that developed on a cold front that moved through the area on the 15th. Prior to that, unseasonably mild air helped push high temperatures as high as the 70s. A strong high pressure system moved across nearby Canada and supplied a fresh supply of cold air into the region. Meanwhile, the low pressure system formed over South Carolina and Georgia on the morning of the 16th and moved northeast. At 2 p.m. EDT on the 16th, it was near Myrtle Beach, South Carolina; at 8 p.m. EDT that evening, it was just east of Virginia Beach; at 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, it was about 100 miles east of Atlantic City, New Jersey and was about 100 miles south of Cape Cod, Massachusetts at 8 a.m. EDT on the 17th. As central pressures go, this was not particularly a powerful system; it was only 996 millibars the morning of the 17th. What contributed to the event, was the strong high pressure system (about 1040 millibars the morning of the 16th). It supplied the fresh cold air needed to change the precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain and increased the pressure gradient (and consequently the wind) between itself and the developing nor'easter low pressure system.

An intense area of low pressure on the 1st moved into the Upper Midwest with a cold front stretched southward across the Ohio Valley all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. Meanwhile, another cold front moved through the Northeast and settled into the Mid Atlantic region. This boundary remained stationary for a time during the overnight hours of the 1st, as the strong low pressure system moved into the western Great Lakes. Gradually an occluded front moved through the area during the morning hours of the 2nd, as a weak area of low pressure developed over eastern Pennsylvania. Along and ahead of this boundary and weak area of low pressure, an abundance of moisture was brought northward across our area. This lead to 1 to 3 inches of rain late on the 1st into the 2nd, and when combined with melting piles of snow in some areas, mainly minor flooding of streams, creeks and some roads resulted across eastern Pennsylvania. Strong southerly winds, coupled with the runoff, produced tidal flooding along the Delaware River from Bucks County on southward.|The rain began between 7:45 PM and 9:00 PM EST on the 1st and became heavy at times, then tapered off generally between 9:00 AM and 10:00 AM EST on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the 2nd, the tidal gauge along the Delaware River at Philadelphia Pier 12 went above its flood stage of 8.2 feet at 11:41 AM EST, crested at 8.74 feet at 12:36 PM EST, and then dropped below flood stage at 1:38 PM EST. This level above stage arrived about an hour earlier along the portion of Delaware County that borders the Delaware River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure tracked north along the Mid-Atlantic Coast on March 16th and 17th. The low pressure moved along the New England Coast and then into Canada by the 18th. The northerly winds across the area caused by this low pressure allowed for a combination of lake enhanced and lake effect snow to develop across Northwest Pennsylvania during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the morning hours of the 16th, and continued through the afternoon. By evening, between 4.0 and 6.0 inches of snow fell across the area. The snow continued overnight, and ended on the morning of the 17th. By the end of the event, Waterford had picked up 11.0 inches, and Corry received 10.3 inches. Colt Station reported 14.0 inches.

The snow continued overnight, and ended during the morning hours of the 17th. Snowfall totals included 11.0 inches in Northeast, 8.0 inches in Millcreek Township, and 7.8 inches in Lake City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level disturbance and associated cold pool aloft combined with strong early May sunshine to produce a favorable environment for diurnal convection given considerable low and mid level instability. Storm initiation occurred on the ridges, then moved east across portions of central and southeastern Pennsylvania. Gusty winds, torrential downpours and pea sized hail occurred in several locations with one report of thunderstorm wind damage - trees and power lines down in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure tracked north along the Mid-Atlantic Coast on March 16th and 17th. The low pressure moved along the New England Coast and then into Canada by the 18th. The northerly winds across the area caused by this low pressure allowed for a combination of lake enhanced and lake effect snow to develop across Northwest Pennsylvania during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the morning hours of the 16th, and continued through the afternoon. By evening, between 3.0 and 5.0 inches of snow fell across the area. The snow continued overnight and ended the next morning. Snowfall totals ranged between 6.0 and 12.0 inches. Storm totals included 7.0 inches in Titusville, and 10.0 inches in Centerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool pool aloft associated with a large upper level low pressure system located off of the New England coast combined with strong mid-June sunshine to create a favorable environment for thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours on June 12, 2007. A compact upper air disturbance aided in providing large scale ascent as it rotated around the periphery of the upper level vortex . The storm motion was from the northeast to the southwest, which is unusual for summertime convection in Central PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported trees down in Benton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured a 61 mph wind gust in Bellwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much like on 12th, increasing low level instability below a relative minimum in temperatures aloft created a rapid destabilization of the atmosphere during the afternoon and evening hours on June 13, 2007. The storm motion was northeast to southwest, which is unusual for summertime convection in Central PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported trees down in Everett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced severe weather across portions of Western Pennsylvania, Southeast Ohio, and Northern West Virginia. As low pressure slowly moved east of the region, heavy rains with some snow melt then caused flooding of some streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads closed due to flooding across much of county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the leftover onshore flow from the departing nor'easter, fresh water run-off from the heavy rain and the astronomical spring tides caused by the new moon caused tidal flooding along the Delaware River and tidal sections of its tributaries from the afternoon of the 18th into the morning of the 21st. Moderate tidal flooding occurred with the high tide during the early morning of the 19th and minor tidal flooding occurred with the high tides during the afternoons of the 18th and 19th and the early morning of the 20th and 21st. The highest tide in Philadelphia was 9.48 feet above mean lower low water at 336 a.m. EDT on the 19th. Moderate tidal flooding begins at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water.

Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain and isolated flash flooding over southern and southeastern portions of Central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of June 1, 2007. Flash flooding transitioned into areal flooding over York County during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from a slow moving thunderstorm caused flash flooding near the town of Berlin. The heavy rain caused the closure of State Route 219, due to water flowing over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain and isolated flash flooding over southern and southeastern portions of Central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of June 1, 2007. Flash flooding transitioned into areal flooding over York County during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from slow moving thunderstorms caused flash flooding in York County. Especially hard hit were areas southeast of East Prospect, and areas northwest of Spring Grove. In Lower Windsor Township, southeast of East Prospect, several basements were flooded and several road intersections were closed. In Jackson Township, numerous roads were under water southwest of Spring Grove. Multiple cars were under water, and a water rescue was performed on Nashville Boulevard. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain and isolated flash flooding over southern and southeastern portions of Central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of June 1, 2007. Flash flooding transitioned into areal flooding over York County during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced by slow moving thunderstorms caused flash flooding in Dauphin County in Derry Township, mainly south of Hershey. There were 7 residential structures that were affected. Two homes sustained major damage, while another five sustained minor damage. Five additional reports of flooded basements were received. In addition, seven vehicle rescues were performed. A state of emergency was declared in Derry Township by local emergency management officials.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain and isolated flash flooding over southern and southeastern portions of Central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of June 1, 2007. Flash flooding transitioned into areal flooding over York County during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding near Waynesboro. A number of reports of flooded intersections and flooded basements were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the eastern Great Lakes Wednesday evening May 9th tracked east into upstate New York by Thursday morning the 10th. The low pressure system dragged a cold front through the region on the 10th. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon ahead of the cold front which swept through the region by late evening the 10th. The thunderstorms produced mainly large hail with a couple reports of wind damage and a small tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado rated an EF0 touched down near Elk Lake Pennsylvania southwest of Montrose. The tornado tracked northeast at 20 mph and lifted 3 miles north northeast of Elk Lake close to Montrose. Small trees were snapped and uprooted along a narrow path, estimated up to 75 yards wide at its widest. The path length was about 3 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure organized over the southeastern United States during the evening of Saturday April 14th, and tracked northeast along the East Coast Sunday the 15th, to Long Island on Monday the 16th. The low pressure system grew into a major late season winter storm by the time it reached the New Jersey coast Sunday evening the 15th. The central pressure of this storm dropped to 963 mb just south of Long Island on the 16th. The storm stalled in the vicinity of Long Island and the southern New England coast from the 16th until Tuesday the 17th when it finally drifted to well off the east coast.  Most of the precipitation with this storm fell as moderate to heavy rain through the day on the 15th before changing over to a mix of snow and sleet after midnight on the 16th. During that day on the 16th, the higher elevations of northeast Pennsylvania received heavy snow, while the valleys saw a mix of snow and sleet that primarily melted after hitting the ground.|The snow ended by the early morning hours on Tuesday the 17th. Total storm accumulations ranged from a slushy inch or two in the Wyoming, Susquehanna and Delaware River valleys to between 10 and 20 inches over the higher terrain including the Poconos. The snow was heavy and wet bringing down many trees and power lines causing scattered power outages over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 17 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation along the leading edge of a low pressure system that went into the Great Lakes fell as a wintry mix across the higher terrain of the Poconos from around 9 p.m. EDT on the 11th until around 9 a.m. EDT on the 12th. Precipitation started as rain, changed quickly to snow and then over to freezing rain around 2 a.m. EDT on the 12th. The freezing rain coated trees, signs and vehicles in the higher terrain of the Poconos. Even though the precipitation fell at night, the ground remained too warm for ice to accrue on roadways. Ice accretions were less than one quarter of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in about a week, an unseasonably chilly air mass from Canada moved into the region. This dropped low temperatures to around freezing in the Pocono Plateau and (besides Philadelphia), as low as the mid to upper 30s with pockets of frost elsewhere in Eastern Pennsylvania.  Low temperatures included 32 degrees in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 34 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 36 degrees in Sellersville (Bucks County) and West Chester (Chester County), 37 degrees in Easton (Northampton County) and 38 degrees in Reading (Berks County). Low temperatures in the 30s were not as widespread as they were the previous week.

An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to Eastern Pennsylvania on the 15th and strong to high winds as well as some snow to the region on the 16th. Numerous streams and rivers flooded, but the flooding along the Delaware River was minimal. Many roadways were closed due to flooding. Many basements were also flooded. There were a small number of mainly voluntary evacuations. About 25 major roadways around the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area were closed due to flooding.|Rain began falling throughout the area during the late evening on the 14th. It started briefly as snow over some of the higher terrain of the Poconos. The rain fell at its heaviest across the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th into the evening. Precipitation changed to snow over the higher terrain shortly after midnight EDT on the 16th while a mixture of rain and snow (during heavier bursts) continued in the valleys through the morning. Precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening. Elsewhere across Eastern Pennsylvania, the rain fell at its heaviest from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th to about 3 a.m. EDT on the 16th. As the upper air disturbance moved across southeast Pennsylvania, the rain did change to snow for a couple of hours around sunrise on the 16th and accumulated about an inch or two on grassy areas. The steady precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening on the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 6 inches. Event snowfall totals were an inch or two in most areas, except for 3 to 6 inches in the higher terrain of the Poconos. |The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. The combination of the heavy rain, even some snow and the winds helped knock down numerous trees and power lines. PECO Energy reported about 80,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power. More than 50 major roads were closed in southeastern Pennsylvania because of downed trees. also in Southeastern Pennsylvani, five SEPTA Regional Rail Service Lines had service interruptions on the 16th. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 77,000 of its customers lost power in Eastern Pennsylvania and about 65,000 homes and businesses lost power in the Lehigh Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph.|The combination of the snow, wind (power outages) and flooding (more so in the local Philadelphia area) caused many school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings on the 16th. The nor'easter was so nasty that even the Internal Revenue Service extended the tax deadline for 48 hours.  |The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along low lying areas and roadways near the Schuylkill River as well as mainly in the Perkiomen,Pennypack and Wissahickon Basins. About a dozen major roadways were closed due to flooding. Creek flooding also closed some smaller roadways. The Schuylkill River forced the closure of roadways in Conshohocken, Lower Merion, West Norriton and Norristown. Creek flooding closed roadways in Lower Moreland, Skippack, Upper Frederick, Upper Merion, Upper Moreland and Whitemarsh Townships.   |The Schuylkill River in Philadelphia was above it 11 foot flood stage from 703 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 430 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 11.34 feet at 1030 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The Manatawny Creek at Pottstown was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1252 p.m. EDT through 957 p.m. EDT on the 15th. it crested at 6.67 feet at 345 p.m. EDT. Additional heavy rain overnight forced the Manatawny Creek over its banks again. The Manatawny Creek at Pottstown was again over its 6 foot flood stage from 603 a.m. EDT through 852 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 6.11 feet at 7 a.m. EDT. The East Branch of the Perkiomen at Schwenksville was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1006 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 141 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 9.3 feet at 500 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The main stem of the Perkiomen Creek at East Greenville was above its 4 foot flood stage from 103 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 648 p.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 4.21 feet at 430 p.m. EDT on the 15th. Additional heavy rain caused the Perkiomen to flood the following morning. The Perkiomen Creek at East Greenville was above its 4 foot flood stage from 521 a.m. EDT on the 16th through 831 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 4.26 feet at 700 a.m. EDT on the 16th. Farther downstream, the Perkiomen Creek at Graterford was above its 11 foot flood stage from 142 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 1220 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 12.43 feet at 630 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The Wissahickon Creek at Fort Washington was above its 9 foot flood stage from 847 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 134 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 11.17 feet at 515p.m. EDT on the 15th. Additional heavy rain caused the Wissahickon to flood the following morning. The Wissahickon Creek at Fort Washington was above its 9 foot flood stage from 424 a.m. EDT on the 16th through 1001 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 10.75 feet at 615 a.m. EDT on the 16th.|Precipitation totals included 5.63 inches in Norristown, 4.99 inches in Souderton, 4.69 inches in Wynnewood, 4.67 inches in Eagleville, 4.44 inches in Hatboro, and 4.15 inches in Pottstown. Snowfall totals included 1.5 inches in Rosemont and Wynnewood.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in about a week, an unseasonably chilly air mass from Canada moved into the region. This dropped low temperatures to around freezing in the Pocono Plateau and (besides Philadelphia), as low as the mid to upper 30s with pockets of frost elsewhere in Eastern Pennsylvania.  Low temperatures included 32 degrees in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 34 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 36 degrees in Sellersville (Bucks County) and West Chester (Chester County), 37 degrees in Easton (Northampton County) and 38 degrees in Reading (Berks County). Low temperatures in the 30s were not as widespread as they were the previous week.

Widespread severe weather occurred on this day. Thunderstorms initiated in a warm and humid airmass ahead of a strong cold front moving eastward through the Ohio Valley. One storm produced and EF-0 tornado between Muncy and Hughesville in Lycoming county. In addition, a 24-year-old male was struck by lightning and killed in his driveway in York county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel sized hail in Mifflintown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to Eastern Pennsylvania on the 15th and strong to high winds as well as some snow to the region on the 16th. Numerous streams and rivers flooded, but the flooding along the Delaware River was minimal. Many roadways were closed due to flooding. Many basements were also flooded. There were a small number of mainly voluntary evacuations. About 25 major roadways around the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area were closed due to flooding.|Rain began falling throughout the area during the late evening on the 14th. It started briefly as snow over some of the higher terrain of the Poconos. The rain fell at its heaviest across the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th into the evening. Precipitation changed to snow over the higher terrain shortly after midnight EDT on the 16th while a mixture of rain and snow (during heavier bursts) continued in the valleys through the morning. Precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening. Elsewhere across Eastern Pennsylvania, the rain fell at its heaviest from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th to about 3 a.m. EDT on the 16th. As the upper air disturbance moved across southeast Pennsylvania, the rain did change to snow for a couple of hours around sunrise on the 16th and accumulated about an inch or two on grassy areas. The steady precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening on the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 6 inches. Event snowfall totals were an inch or two in most areas, except for 3 to 6 inches in the higher terrain of the Poconos. |The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. The combination of the heavy rain, even some snow and the winds helped knock down numerous trees and power lines. PECO Energy reported about 80,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power. More than 50 major roads were closed in southeastern Pennsylvania because of downed trees. also in Southeastern Pennsylvani, five SEPTA Regional Rail Service Lines had service interruptions on the 16th. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 77,000 of its customers lost power in Eastern Pennsylvania and about 65,000 homes and businesses lost power in the Lehigh Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph.|The combination of the snow, wind (power outages) and flooding (more so in the local Philadelphia area) caused many school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings on the 16th. The nor'easter was so nasty that even the Internal Revenue Service extended the tax deadline for 48 hours.  |The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flooding in Chester County was centered around the Brandywine Creek at its tributaries. Along the main stem of the Brandywine, Pennsylvania State Routes 926 and 100 were closed in Pocopson Township, State Route 100 and Creek Road were closed in Birmingham Township and Brandywine Creek Road was closed in East Bradford Township. Flood waters from the Brandywine ripped a 50 foot stretch of Brandywine Creek Road in Newlin Township. Flooding problems also occurred around West Chester. Flooding caused a fire at a PECO Energy substation in West Whiteland Township.|  |The East Branch of the Brandywine River at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 911 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 1112 p.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 9.48 feet at 245 p.m. EDT on the 15th. A second shot of heavier rain caused additional flooding the next day.  The East Branch of the Brandywine River at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage again from 257 a.m. EDT on the 16th through 420 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 8.51 feet at 745 a.m. EDT on the 16th. The main stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford was above its 9 foot flood stage from 155 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 907 p.m. EDT on the 16th.  It crested at 10.46 feet at 1015 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The French Creek at Phoenixville was above its 8 foot flood stage from 1013 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 1200 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 8.60 feet at 345 p.m. EDT on the 15th. Additional heavy rain caused the creek to flood again the next morning. The French Creek at Phoenixville was above its 8 foot flood stage from 430 a.m. EDT on the 16th through 915 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 8.16 feet at 345 p.m. EDT on the 16th. The Valley Creek at Valley Forge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 901 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 806 p.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 8.08 feet at 315 p.m. EDT on the 15th.|Precipitation totals included 4.97 inches in West Chester, 4.41 inches in Honey Brook, 4.19 inches in Glenmoore, and 3.65 inches in Coatesville. Snow accumulations 2.0 inches in East Nantmeal Township and 0.5 inches in Coatesville.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A run of unseasonably cold weather produce below freezing minimum temperatures from the morning of the 5th through the morning of the 11th. The coldest morning for most places were the mornings of the 9th and 11th when the winds were the lightest. This caused minor damage to early blooming fruit trees such as apricots and peaches. The unseasonably cold weather also slowed the germination and planting of early crops. Gardening and nursery center business traffic was also lighter than usual.|Lowest temperatures included 22 degrees in West Chester (Chester County) and Springtown (Bucks County), 24 degrees in Sellersville and Perkasie (Bucks County), 26 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County), 27 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County), 28 degrees in Longwood Gardens (Chester County) and 30 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport.

In the wake of the departing nor���easter, the combination of strong winds, snow on tree limbs and heavy rain loosening the ground caused many tree limbs, trees and wires to be knocked down on the 16th. Over 160,000 homes and businesses across Eastern Pennsylvania lost power. Most power was restored by the night of the 17th. Many school districts cancelled classes because of the power outages and/or blocked roadways.|Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 77,100 of its eastern Pennsylvania customers lost power on the 16th.  Carbon and Monroe Counties were among the hardest hit counties. In Carbon County, the downed trees caused most of the east side of Jim Thorpe to not have power for most of the daylight hours on the 16th. The docket for the county courthouse was cancelled for the day. In Mahoning Township, part of the metal flashing on the roof of the Times News newspaper was torn away. A fifteen foot piece of the metal was tossed to the ground. In Monroe County, an additional 3,000 Metropolitan Edison customers also lost power in Monroe County. |About 6,000 Metropolitan Edison customers lost power mainly in the eastern half of Berks County as well as Montgomery County. One home was badly damaged by a downed tree in Mount Penn Borough (Berks County). The family was displaced. In the Lehigh Valley, about 65,000 homes and businesses lost power. In Northampton County, the most concentrated outages were in the northern part of the county in Upper Mount Bethel, Bushkill and Moore Townships. Downed tree limbs closed a few roads in Plainfield Township.   |Across southeastern Pennsylvania including Philadelphia, about 80,000 PECO Energy customers lost power. In Chester County, a 100-foot tree crushed a King Road home in East Whiteland Township rendering it uninhabitable. Twelve roads across the county were closed because of downed trees, utility poles and wires. PECO Energy reported that 30,000 of its 80,000 outages in southeastern Pennsylvania occurred in Chester County. The R5 SEPTA Commuter Rail Line suspended its Paoli service because of lost power. The worst utility damage occurred in the northwest part of the county as many Pennsylvania Power and Light utility poles were knocked down along Pennsylvania State Route 10 in Honey Brook. The road was closed for several hours. In Delaware County, about 24,000 homes and businesses lost power, with the most outages in Radnor Township. One home was damaged by downed trees in Radnor Township. Downed trees closed roads and caused power outages in Chadds Ford, Colwyn, Concord, Folcroft, Haverford, Lansdowne, Marple, Newtown Square, Radnor, Sharon Hill and Springfield.  The Pennsylvania Route 101 trolley was replaced by bus service as a downed tree blocked the route. Power interruptions also affected the R3 SEPTA Regional Rail Media Line. In Montgomery County, the worst reported tree damage was in Lower Merion Township where about two dozen trees were knocked down. One house was damaged by the fallen trees. Power interruptions suspended service on the Cynwyd R6 SEPTA Regional Rail Line on the 16th. In Philadelphia, the strong winds caused delays that averaged 100 minutes at Philadelphia International Airport. U.S. Air cancelled about 35 flights outright.  About 50 trees were knocked down within Philadelphia. In Bucks County, about 6,000 homes and businesses lost power. |Peak wind gusts included 55 mph in Slatington (Lehigh County), 52 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 51 mph in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 49 mph in Doylestown (Bucks County) and London Grove (Chester County), 47 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 46 mph in Reading (Berks County) and the Lehigh Valley International Airport and the 45 mph at the Willow Grove NAS was 45 mph.

The road was closed for several hours. In Delaware County, about 24,000 homes and businesses lost power, with the most outages in Radnor Township. One home was damaged by downed trees in Radnor Township. Downed trees closed roads and caused power outages in Chadds Ford, Colwyn, Concord, Folcroft, Haverford, Lansdowne, Marple, Newtown Square, Radnor, Sharon Hill and Springfield.  The Pennsylvania Route 101 trolley was replaced by bus service as a downed tree blocked the route. Power interruptions also affected the R3 SEPTA Regional Rail Media Line. In Montgomery County, the worst reported tree damage was in Lower Merion Township where about two dozen trees were knocked down. One house was damaged by the fallen trees. Power interruptions suspended service on the Cynwyd R6 SEPTA Regional Rail Line on the 16th. In Philadelphia, the strong winds caused delays that averaged 100 minutes at Philadelphia International Airport. U.S. Air cancelled about 35 flights outright.  About 50 trees were knocked down within Philadelphia. In Bucks County, about 6,000 homes and businesses lost power. |Peak wind gusts included 55 mph in Slatington (Lehigh County), 52 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 51 mph in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 49 mph in Doylestown (Bucks County) and London Grove (Chester County), 47 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport, 46 mph in Reading (Berks County) and the Lehigh Valley International Airport and the 45 mph at the Willow Grove NAS was 45 mph.

Widespread severe weather occurred on this day. Thunderstorms initiated in a warm and humid airmass ahead of a strong cold front moving eastward through the Ohio Valley. One storm produced and EF-0 tornado between Muncy and Hughesville in Lycoming county. In addition, a 24-year-old male was struck by lightning and killed in his driveway in York county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into northwestern Pennsylvania causing widespread showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon hours.  Many of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm downburst caused damage from near Conneaut Lake to the south end of Tamarack Lake.  Hundreds of trees were downed along this damage path.  Several structures were damaged by the strong winds or downed trees.  One house along State Route 19 southwest of Meadville lost a large section of roof.  A hanger at Port Meadville Airport was also damaged by the strong winds.  The thunderstorm responsible for this downburst later produced a tornado near Tamarack Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into northwestern Pennsylvania causing widespread showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon hours.  Many of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down southeast of Meadville during the evening hours of May 1st. The track of the tornado began on the west side of Tamarack Lake near the intersection of Tamarack Drive and Phelps Road.  The tornado continued southeast for around two miles and lifted near the intersection of Tamarack Drive and Freyermuth Road.  The damage path was intermittent and 25 to 30 yards wide.  Ten structures were damaged by this tornado and several hundred trees were knocked down.  The parent thunderstorm responsible for this tornado also produced a downburst that caused damage from near Conneaut Lake to the south end of Tamarack Lake.

Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 10,000 of its customers in the Lehigh Valley and the northern Philadelphia suburbs in Montgomery and Bucks Counties lost power. All power was restored by 3 p.m. EDT on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Tredyffrin Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Lower Merion Township.

An upper air disturbance and a surface trof preceding a cold front produced severe thunderstorm across Eastern Pennsylvania. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 10,000 of its customers in the Lehigh Valley and the northern Philadelphia suburbs in Montgomery and Bucks Counties lost power. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in West Bradford Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather occurred on this day. Thunderstorms initiated in a warm and humid airmass ahead of a strong cold front moving eastward through the Ohio Valley. One storm produced and EF-0 tornado between Muncy and Hughesville in Lycoming county. In addition, a 24-year-old male was struck by lightning and killed in his driveway in York county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reported wind damage to business signs at a bowling alley and West Milton Bank in Mifflinburg. A roof was also removed from a home near JCT of Route 45 and Forest Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense area of low pressure on the 1st moved into the Upper Midwest with a cold front stretched southward across the Ohio Valley all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. Meanwhile, a cold front moved through the Northeast and settled into the Mid Atlantic region. This boundary remained stationary for awhile during the overnight hours of the 1st, as the strong low pressure system moved into the western Great Lakes. As cold air at the surface lingered to the north of the aforementioned boundary, precipitation moved in and caused a period of freezing rain. Gradually an occluded front moved through the area during the morning hours of the 2nd, as a weak area of low pressure developed over eastern Pennsylvania. This allowed warmer air to move in and the precipitation to taper off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold air locked in at the surface and as precipitation started at 9:00 PM EST on the 1st, freezing rain occurred. The icing was mostly confined to the higher elevations where temperatures remain at or just below freezing for several hours. Ice accumulated up to a quarter of an inch before the freezing rain changed to plain rain around 6:00 AM EST on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front/surface trough combined with moderate instability to produce scattered to numerous thunderstorms across portions of Central Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours on May 10th. Hail reports ranged from penny (0.75) to quarter (1.00) size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotter reported penny sized hail in Mill Hall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from strong thunderstorms produced flash flooding across portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley. Rainfall rates over one inch per hour were observed with as much as 2-3 inches falling in 1-2 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported flooded intersections and closed roads due to  rapid rises on area creeks and streams just north of Carlisle. County Emergency Manager reported a water rescue in Silver Spring Township around 8 pm. Rainfall reports of as much as 3.5 inches of rain were received from 5 pm to 8 pm in Carlisle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from strong thunderstorms produced flash flooding across portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley. Rainfall rates over one inch per hour were observed with as much as 2-3 inches falling in 1-2 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Emergency Manager reported several closed roads in eastern Franklin County south of Shippensburg due to flash flooding. Rising flood waters near the intersection of Route 696 and Interstate 81 trapped a vehicle and a water rescue was performed in Silver Spring Township. The northbound section of I-81 was closed, along with several secondary roads including Olde Scotland Road, Mount Rock Road and Woods Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving southeast from the Great lakes produced widespread severe weather across Southeast Ohio, Western Pennsylvania, and Northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down 6SM W Waynesburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain and isolated flash flooding over southern and southeastern portions of Central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of June 1, 2007. Flash flooding transitioned into areal flooding over York County during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from slow moving thunderstorms caused flash flooding in York County. Especially hard hit were areas southeast of East Prospect, and areas northwest of Spring Grove. In Lower Windsor Township, southeast of East Prospect, several basements were flooded and several road intersections were closed. In Jackson Township, numerous roads were under water southwest of Spring Grove. Multiple cars were under water, and a water rescue was performed on Nashville Boulevard. No injuries were reported. Flash flooding transitioned into a flood event, which lasted through the overnight hours into the early morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper low pressure system dropped light snow across Eastern Pennsylvania during the day on the 7th. Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches. The recent unseasonably cold weather made it easier for the snow to accumulate on roadways and bridges and overpasses during the early morning and also during heavier bursts of snow. Consequently untreated roadways were slippery and many accidents were reported, especially during the morning commute. Snow began falling between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST on the 7th across Eastern Pennsylvania and ended between 3 p.m. and 5 p.m. EST later that day.  |One of the worst reported accidents with injuries occurred in Towamensing Township (Carbon County) on Pennsylvania State Route 209. A pickup truck slid sideways into oncoming traffic where it crashed with another vehicle and then slammed into a pole. The oncoming car was then struck from behind by another vehicle which then skidded off the road into a tree. Three people were injured and hospitalized. Schools in Easton (Northampton County) and Catasauqua (Lehigh County) never opened. Several Bucks County school districts cancelled afternoon and evening activities.  |Actual accumulations included 2.5 inches in Reading (Berks County) and Fricks (Bucks County), 2.3 inches in Boothwyn (Delaware County), 2.0 inches in Downingtown (Chester County), 1.8 inches in Trappe (Montgomery County), Boyertown (Berks County) and Levittown (Bucks County), 1.7 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 1.5 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), Roxborough (Philadelphia County) and Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 1.4 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.0 inch in Effort (Monroe County) and 0.8 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County).    | |The Alberta Clipper as it name implies moved rapidly southeast from southeastern Montana at 7 a.m. EST on March 6th, to near Saint Louis, Missouri at 7 a.m. EST on March 7th, to the Virginia/West Virginia border at 1 p.m. EST on March 7th, to about 150 miles east of Virginia Beach, Virginia at 7 p.m. EST on March 7th. The rapid movement of the low pressure system prevented heavier snow from falling.

Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a strong cold front as it approached central New York and Northeast Pennsylvania. Widespread severe thunderstorms developed, with many reports of large hail and damaging winds. One thunderstorm produced a brief tornado in the eastern Finger Lakes region.

Hot and humid weather developed as a Bermuda high pressure system built into the Northeast United States. A cold front moved into the Great Lakes during the day on the 27th, with thunderstorms developing in the favorable environment across the area. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 27th, with some of the storms producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex ahead of a cold front over Lake Erie produced an area of thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts across Beaver county reached more than 4 inches in 2 hours with reports of 2 to 3 inches in 2 hours common in other counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued across Shaler and Ross townships with standing water across roads slowly receding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to Eastern Pennsylvania on the 15th and strong to high winds as well as some snow to the region on the 16th. Numerous streams and rivers flooded, but the flooding along the Delaware River was minimal. Many roadways were closed due to flooding. Many basements were also flooded. There were a small number of mainly voluntary evacuations. About 25 major roadways around the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area were closed due to flooding.|Rain began falling throughout the area during the late evening on the 14th. It started briefly as snow over some of the higher terrain of the Poconos. The rain fell at its heaviest across the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th into the evening. Precipitation changed to snow over the higher terrain shortly after midnight EDT on the 16th while a mixture of rain and snow (during heavier bursts) continued in the valleys through the morning. Precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening. Elsewhere across Eastern Pennsylvania, the rain fell at its heaviest from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th to about 3 a.m. EDT on the 16th. As the upper air disturbance moved across southeast Pennsylvania, the rain did change to snow for a couple of hours around sunrise on the 16th and accumulated about an inch or two on grassy areas. The steady precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening on the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 6 inches. Event snowfall totals were an inch or two in most areas, except for 3 to 6 inches in the higher terrain of the Poconos. |The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. The combination of the heavy rain, even some snow and the winds helped knock down numerous trees and power lines. PECO Energy reported about 80,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power. More than 50 major roads were closed in southeastern Pennsylvania because of downed trees. also in Southeastern Pennsylvani, five SEPTA Regional Rail Service Lines had service interruptions on the 16th. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 77,000 of its customers lost power in Eastern Pennsylvania and about 65,000 homes and businesses lost power in the Lehigh Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph.|The combination of the snow, wind (power outages) and flooding (more so in the local Philadelphia area) caused many school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings on the 16th. The nor'easter was so nasty that even the Internal Revenue Service extended the tax deadline for 48 hours.  |The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused flooding across Bucks County with the worst reported flooding along the Neshaminy Creek. Ground floor apartments, roadways and basements flooded in floods prone areas along the creek in Bensalem, Bristol, Middletown, Hulmeville and Northamptom. In Middletown Township, the creek flooded two roadways with six feet of water and vehicles that were not moved along Bridle Drive were totally flooded. |Flooding also occurred along the Delaware River, but mainly roadway and low-lying area as none of the gaged sites along the river reached flood stage. Voluntary evacuations occurred in Falls Township (Wellington Woods Development) and in New Hope (Waterview Development). Road closures and basement flooding occurred along the Delaware River in Bridgeton, Tinicum, New Hope, Upper Makefield, Yardley and Lower Makefield. In Yardley, the Delaware River flooded roadways in the Rivermawr section. |Flooding also severely affected Bristol Township, but was not related to either the Neshaminy Creek or Delaware River. About 50 homes and apartments suffered extensive damage. Twenty homes were inaccessible and family had to be rescued by boat. The Croydon section of the township was hit hard on Spencer, Lincoln, Swan and Dixon Avenues as well as Penn and Franklin Streets. The Lakeview Manor Apartments were evacuated near Silver Lake Park. |Elsewhere in the county, Martins Creek overeflowed in Tullytown and the Tohickon Creek flooded in Bedminster. Roadways were also closed in Buckingham, Doylestown, Perkasie and Upper Southampton.|The Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne was above its 9 foot flood stage from 1129 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 711 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 14.68 feet at 145 a.m. EDT on the 16th. This was the highest crest since June 17th, 2001.|Precipitation totals included 6.12 inches in Neshaminy Falls, 5.60 inches in Morrisville, 5.51 inches in Doylestown, 5.47 inches in Furlong, 5.26 at Doylestown Airport, 4.96 inches in Perkasie, 4.87 inches in Southampton, 4.70 inches in Springtown, and 3.99 inches in Sellersville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the fresh water run-off and the onshore flow associated with the nor'easter caused minor tidal flooding at the time of high tide along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries overnight on the 15th. This also slowed the run-off of streams, creeks and rivers that empty into the Delaware River. The high tide at the Delaware River in Philadelphia reached 8.72 feet above mean lower low water at 1230 a.m. EDT on the 16th. Minor tidal flooding begins at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.

The combination of the fresh water run-off and the onshore flow associated with the nor'easter caused minor tidal flooding at the time of high tide along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries overnight on the 15th. The high tide at the Delaware River in Philadelphia reached 8.72 feet above mean lower low water at 1230 a.m. EDT on the 16th. Minor tidal flooding begins at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.

A series of thunderstorms that moved across the same region (called training) around and just after Midnight EDT on the 27th caused flash flooding of streams and poor drainage flooding in Chester, Montgomery and Bucks Counties.The run-off from those thunderstorms and other thunderstorms later that morning caused flooding along some of the slower responding and/or larger streams. Doppler radar storm total estimates averaged between 1 and 4 inches across the three county area with the highest amounts in the central part of each county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain continued to repeat over the same areas in a narrow band in central Bucks County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3 to 4 inches in those areas. This axis of heavy rain extended from New Britain Township east through Doylestown Township, Buckingham Township, Plumstead Township and Solebury Township. | |The heavy rain from the thunderstorms caused about 18 roadways to be closed in Bucks County with many basements flooded and some motorists rescued. Even after the flood waters receded, debris had to be cleared from several roadways. Both New Britain and Solebury Townships declared states of emergency. In New Britain Township flooding occurred along the headwaters of the Neshaminy Creek. In Solebury Township, water reached up to six feet in some homes (basements). The Cuttalossa, Primrose and Paunacussing Creeks all flooded. A large chunk of Cuttalossa Road (30 feet by 30 feet) was washed away by the creek. The footbridge over the falls at the Cuttalossa Inn was washed away. River Road (Pennsylvania State Route 32) was undermined by the rushing waters of the Cuttalossa Creek.  |The Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne had moderate flooding and was above its 9 foot flood stage from 1044 a.m. EDT through 859 p.m. EDT on the 27th. It crested at 11.96 feet at 615 p.m. EDT.|Storm totals included 4.90 inches in Fricks, 4.17 inches in Doylestown, 3.02 inches in Perkasie, 2.30 inches in Furlong and 1.96 inches in Sellersville.

The first heat wave of the season (loosely defined as three consecutive days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher) occurred across most of southeastern Pennsylvania from the 26th through the 28th. The hottest day with respect to both temperature and humidity occurred on the 27th as many high temperatures reached into the mid 90s and heat indices were as high as around 100F. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and humid conditions were in place across much of Northeast Pennsylvania as a weak upper level disturbance crossed the area during the afternoon. Scattered thunderstorms developed in the moderately unstable airmass, and a few of the storms reached severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell on wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked northeast across the Great Lakes into Hudson Bay. A strong southwest flow developed ahead of the associated cold front and brought a warm and humid airmass across New York and Pennsylvania in advance of the front. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front...producing hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blown down county-wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first heat wave of the season (loosely defined as three consecutive days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher) occurred across most of southeastern Pennsylvania from the 26th through the 28th. The hottest day with respect to both temperature and humidity occurred on the 27th as many high temperatures reached into the mid 90s and heat indices were as high as around 100F. A cold front that cleared the region on the 28th brought cooler weather into the area for the end of the month.||Highest temperatures included 95 degrees in Philadelphia, 94 degrees in Reading (Berks County) and West Chester (Chester County), 93 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Doylestown (Bucks County) and 92 degrees in Easton (Northampton County).

The combination of a weak onshore flow circulating around a high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes, the spring tides associated with the new moon and some of the highest astronomical tides of the year helped cause minor tidal flooding during the times of high tide along the Delaware River and tidal sections of its tributaries on the night of the 13th. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.7 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding begins at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.

Isolated thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening in a very warm and moist environment across Northeast Pennsylvania.

A cold front encountered a very warm, humid and unstable airmass during the mid to late afternoon and early evening hours...igniting numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across Central Pennsylvania.  Severe thunderstorms produced widespread wind damage and as well as a few reports of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported penny sized hail near Shiremanstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather occurred on this day. Thunderstorms initiated in a warm and humid airmass ahead of a strong cold front moving eastward through the Ohio Valley. One storm produced and EF-0 tornado between Muncy and Hughesville in Lycoming county. In addition, a 24-year-old male was struck by lightning and killed in his driveway in York county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported penny sized hail in Lewistown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass helped trigger thunderstorms with torrential downpours. This caused isolated flash flooding in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos with the worst reported flooding in eastern Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller streams in southwestern Berks County, particularly in the the Cacoosing Creek river basin. Several water rescues occurred along State Hill Road along the Lower Heidelberg and Spring Townships. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid day for early June helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorms that did become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped nickel size hail in Kutztown. So much hail fell that it briefly covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance that passed through the region on an unseasonably hot and humid day helped produce strong to severe thunderstorms across Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. PECO Energy reported that about 40,000 homes and businesses lost power in their southeastern Pennsylvania service area because of the severe thunderstorms and lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage (downed trees and wires) along the Lower Merion Township and Philadelphia Main Line border. In the Gladwyne section of the township, one downed tree damaged a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to Eastern Pennsylvania on the 15th and strong to high winds as well as some snow to the region on the 16th. Numerous streams and rivers flooded, but the flooding along the Delaware River was minimal. Many roadways were closed due to flooding. Many basements were also flooded. There were a small number of mainly voluntary evacuations. About 25 major roadways around the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area were closed due to flooding.|Rain began falling throughout the area during the late evening on the 14th. It started briefly as snow over some of the higher terrain of the Poconos. The rain fell at its heaviest across the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th into the evening. Precipitation changed to snow over the higher terrain shortly after midnight EDT on the 16th while a mixture of rain and snow (during heavier bursts) continued in the valleys through the morning. Precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening. Elsewhere across Eastern Pennsylvania, the rain fell at its heaviest from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th to about 3 a.m. EDT on the 16th. As the upper air disturbance moved across southeast Pennsylvania, the rain did change to snow for a couple of hours around sunrise on the 16th and accumulated about an inch or two on grassy areas. The steady precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening on the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 6 inches. Event snowfall totals were an inch or two in most areas, except for 3 to 6 inches in the higher terrain of the Poconos. |The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. The combination of the heavy rain, even some snow and the winds helped knock down numerous trees and power lines. PECO Energy reported about 80,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power. More than 50 major roads were closed in southeastern Pennsylvania because of downed trees. also in Southeastern Pennsylvani, five SEPTA Regional Rail Service Lines had service interruptions on the 16th. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 77,000 of its customers lost power in Eastern Pennsylvania and about 65,000 homes and businesses lost power in the Lehigh Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph.|The combination of the snow, wind (power outages) and flooding (more so in the local Philadelphia area) caused many school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings on the 16th. The nor'easter was so nasty that even the Internal Revenue Service extended the tax deadline for 48 hours.  |The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused roadway and basement flooding in Delaware County on the 15th. Flooding approached several homes near Crum Creek in Marple Township. Flooding from Chester Creek inundated four roads within Chester. Two roads were closed because of Brandywine Creek flooding in Chadds Ford. The Darby Creek flooded parking lots off of Mill Street and Walnut Street in Darby Borough and Hook Road in Darby Township. Pennsylvania State Route 291 was closed between Eddystone and Ridley Township and the ramps to Interstate 95 were flooded in Yeadon Borough. Flooding was also reported along Cobbs Creek along the border with Philadelphia. Trolley tracks were under water on the Sharon Hill-102 line between Collingdale and Sharon Hill. |The Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford was above its 9 foot flood stage from 155 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 907 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 10.46 feet at 1015 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The Chester Creek at Chester was above its 8 foot flood state from 934 a.m. EDT through 1034 p.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 10.42 feet at 800 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The Crum Creek at Newtown Square was above its 6 foot flood stage from 932 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 651 p.m. EDT on the 15th.  It crested at 7.23 feet at 300 p.m. EDT on the 15th.|Precipitation totals included 6.04 inches in Media, 6.00 inches in Boothwyn, and 5.60 inches in Drexel Hill. Snowfall totals included 1.5 inches in both Drexel Hill and Havertown.

Snowfall accumulations included 1.4 inches in Slatington and a trace at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.
