EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused poor drainage and stream flooding. Flooding along the larger rivers was mainly confined to low lying areas and this included the Lehigh and Delaware Rivers. Roads were closed in Forks Township, Easton, West Easton, Bethelehem and Bushkill. The Lehigh River overflowed low lying areas of West Easton. The Delaware River overflowed low points in Forks Township. All roads in the county were re-opened by the evening of the 16th. Precipitation totals included 5.91 inches in Glendon and 4.44 inches in Martin���s Creek. Snowfall accumulations included 1.8 inches in Glendon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms that moved across the same region (called training) around and just after Midnight EDT on the 27th caused flash flooding of streams and poor drainage flooding in Chester, Montgomery and Bucks Counties.The run-off from those thunderstorms and other thunderstorms later that morning caused flooding along some of the slower responding and/or larger streams. Doppler radar storm total estimates averaged between 1 and 4 inches across the three county area with the highest amounts in the central part of each county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Run-off from the heavier rain earlier in the day in Chester County caused flooding along the main stem of the Brandywine Creek during the afternoon of the 27th. The main stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford was above its 9 foot flood stage from 1155 a.m. EDT through 524 p.m. EDT on the 27th. It crested at 9.66 feet at 3 p.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved through the Central Great Lakes on the 4th and 5th. Strong northerly winds behind this low pressure caused lake effect snow to develop as this low pressure moved northeast into eastern Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow began during the evening hours of the 4th, and continued through the 5th. The lake effect snow finally tapered off on the 6th as the low pressure moved well away from the region and the strong northerly winds subsided. The snow was heaviest during the overnight hours into the 5th. Edinboro picked up 6.0 inches by the morning of the 5th, and Corry measured 9.0 inches. Amity Township picked up 7.5 inches of snow for this event.

Flooding forced the closure of Clover Mill Road in East Pikeland Township. Repairs to Brandywine Creek Road in Coatesville were washed away. In Downingtown, the municipal lots were flooded.|The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown experienced moderate flooding. It crested at 9.67 feet at 4 a.m. EDT.   |Storm totals included 3.92 inches in Honey Brook, 3.30 inches in Glenmoore and 2.63 inches in East Nantmeal Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the fresh water run-off and the onshore flow associated with the nor'easter caused minor tidal flooding at the time of high tide along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries overnight on the 15th. This also slowed the run-off of streams, creeks and rivers that empty into the Delaware River. The high tide at the Delaware River in Philadelphia reached 8.72 feet above mean lower low water at 1230 a.m. EDT on the 16th. Minor tidal flooding begins at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.

An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to Eastern Pennsylvania on the 15th and strong to high winds as well as some snow to the region on the 16th. Numerous streams and rivers flooded, but the flooding along the Delaware River was minimal. Many roadways were closed due to flooding. Many basements were also flooded. There were a small number of mainly voluntary evacuations. About 25 major roadways around the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area were closed due to flooding.|Rain began falling throughout the area during the late evening on the 14th. It started briefly as snow over some of the higher terrain of the Poconos. The rain fell at its heaviest across the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th into the evening. Precipitation changed to snow over the higher terrain shortly after midnight EDT on the 16th while a mixture of rain and snow (during heavier bursts) continued in the valleys through the morning. Precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening. Elsewhere across Eastern Pennsylvania, the rain fell at its heaviest from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th to about 3 a.m. EDT on the 16th. As the upper air disturbance moved across southeast Pennsylvania, the rain did change to snow for a couple of hours around sunrise on the 16th and accumulated about an inch or two on grassy areas. The steady precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening on the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 6 inches. Event snowfall totals were an inch or two in most areas, except for 3 to 6 inches in the higher terrain of the Poconos. |The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. The combination of the heavy rain, even some snow and the winds helped knock down numerous trees and power lines. PECO Energy reported about 80,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power. More than 50 major roads were closed in southeastern Pennsylvania because of downed trees. also in Southeastern Pennsylvani, five SEPTA Regional Rail Service Lines had service interruptions on the 16th. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 77,000 of its customers lost power in Eastern Pennsylvania and about 65,000 homes and businesses lost power in the Lehigh Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph.|The combination of the snow, wind (power outages) and flooding (more so in the local Philadelphia area) caused many school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings on the 16th. The nor'easter was so nasty that even the Internal Revenue Service extended the tax deadline for 48 hours.  |The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain led to flooding along the Schuylkill River and smaller creeks within Philadelphia. Kelly and Lincoln Drives near the Schuylkill River were flooded and closed. Flooding was also reported along Roosevelt Boulevard and Flat Rock Road. The heavy rain caused the rest of a retaining wall behind a Lyceum Avenue to collapse. Basements were flooded along the Schuylkill River in the Roxborough section of the city. Flooding was reported along Cobbs Creek, especially at 70th Street and Woodland Avenue. The heavy rain caused many flight delays at the Philadelphia International Airport and about 80 cancelled flights.  |The Schuylkill River at Philadelphia was above it 11 foot flood stage from 703 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 430 p.m. EDT on the 16th.  It crested at 11.34 feet at 1030 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 903 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 1112 a.m. EDT on the 16th.  It crested at 10.34 feet at 800 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth was above its 5 foot flood stage from 951 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 424 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 7.32 feet at 745 p.m. EDT on the 15th. Precipitation totals included 6.22 inches in Roxborough, 5.90 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 5.09 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.|

These included Pennsylvania State Route 715 in Jackson Township, 314 in Pocono Township and 534 in Polk Township. The heavy rain caused a mudslide and road closure along Pennsylvania State Route 191 in Stroud Township. Flooding along the Delaware River was confined to low lying areas near the river as none of the gaged sites reached flood stage. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper air disturbance in the northwest flow behind the cold front still had sufficient moisture to develop strong to severe thunderstorms across Pennsylvania and New Jersey during the afternoon and the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees and wires in the central part of the county. A funnel cloud and rotating wall cloud was seen around Lehighton and Jim Thorpe. Several trees and wires blocked roadways. Downed tree limbs and trees were reported in both Jim Thorpe and Lehighton where a Skywarn spotter estimated wind gusts to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms formed ahead of a cold front moving Southeast across the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the hottest and most humid air masses of the summer affected the southeastern Pennsylvania on the 7th and most of eastern Pennsylvania on the 8th. Highest temperatures were well into the 90s in most areas. The 8th was the hottest day. In addition, dew point temperatures, especially on the 8th were as high as the mid to upper 70s. This produced afternoon heat indices as high as around 105F on the 8th.  ||Highest temperatures included 97 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and New Hope (Bucks County), 96 degrees in Reading (Berks County) and Doylestown and Perkasie (Bucks County), 95 degrees in Easton (Northampton County) and Willow Grove (Montgomery County) and 94 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Pottstown (Montgomery County) and West Chester (Chester County).||A cold front moved through the state on the night of the 8th. While there were some high temperatures in the lower 90s around Philadelphia on the 9th humidity levels were much lower. It was feared that if the excessive heat lasted one day longer, heat related deaths would have started in urbanized areas.

An area of low pressure moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the morning of August 25th. A cold front associated with this low pressure system was located in the Ohio Valley. A hot and especially humid airmass was found ahead of this front across the Middle Atlantic region on August 25th. As the front approached, showers and thunderstorms developed and several of the storms became severe. The actual cold front however did not clear the region until the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm rolled through Marion Township at about 8:18 PM EDT. Strong winds associated with this severe storm knocked down trees in the township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper air disturbance in the northwest flow behind the cold front still had sufficient moisture to develop strong to severe thunderstorms across Pennsylvania and New Jersey during the afternoon and the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The third severe thunderstorm of the day for Northampton County dropped a swath of hail as large as hen eggs (2 inches in diameter) across the central and southern parts of the county. The hen egg size hail fell over the higher terrain of Williams Township. Half dollar size hail fell in Bethlehem and penny size hail fell in Nazareth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms formed ahead of a cold front moving Southeast across the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees reported down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms formed ahead of a cold front moving Southeast across the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio trained spotter reported 0.88 inch hail in Harmarville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure was located in southeastern Canada during the early morning hours of August 8th. A cold front associated with this area of low pressure was moving across the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley regions during the morning. Well out ahead of this cold front was a surface trough which moved through the Mid Atlantic region during the very early morning hours on August 8th. A very warm and humid air mass was in place, and with some upper-level energy, a round of showers and thunderstorms moved through parts of eastern Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey. Some of the thunderstorms turned locally severe between 2:20 AM and 5:40 AM EDT with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pair of severe thunderstorms caused wind damage across Northampton County from Moore Township east through Forks Township. About 8,000 homes and businesses lost power. Seven hundred still did not have power as of Noon EDT on the 8th. Wind damage occurred in Wind Gap, Moore, Plainfield, Nazareth, Washington and Forks Townships. Pennsylvania State Route 191 was closed in Plainfield Township because of downed trees. Community Drive in Moore Township was also closed because of a downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper air disturbance in the northwest flow behind the cold front still had sufficient moisture to develop strong to severe thunderstorms across Pennsylvania and New Jersey during the afternoon and the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moth ball to tennis ball (2.5 inch diameter) hail fell across central and southeast Carbon County and caused considerable damage. In Nesquehoning, golf ball size hail covered the parking lot and damaged the vehicles of emergency responders at the 911 center. Hail as large as tennis balls fell in Franklin Township, as large as hen eggs around Palmerton, as large as quarters in Lehighton and as large as moth balls fell in Jim Thorpe. Hail also fell in Weatherly. Hail damage included broken home and vehicle windows, dented siding and broken skylights.

As the front approached, showers and thunderstorms developed and several of the storms became severe. The actual cold front however did not clear the region until the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms roared through Northampton County during the evening of the 25th. Intense winds produced by the severe storms downed trees and power lines across a good part of the county. The damage began around 9:40 PM EDT in Bath with additional damage in Nazareth, East Bangor and Wind Gap, where tree limbs and wires were down or caught in other wires with even whole trees downed. The most widespread wind damage was reported to be in Bushkill Township. The severe storms knocked out power to some 5,000 customers across Northampton County, which included 10 municipalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse severe thunderstorms developed in a warm and humid airmass over Central Pennsylvania...producing penny to quarter sized hail and wind damage. An apparent funnel cloud was also sited near the Letterkenny Army Depot in Franklin county...which sustained partial roof damage to a building on the eastern section of the Depot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several public reports of a funnel cloud were received by the Franklin county 911 center. Images of the apparent funnel cloud were captured by the Franklin county Fire Company. According to eyewitnesses, the funnel cloud did not touch the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper air disturbance in the northwest flow behind the cold front still had sufficient moisture to develop strong to severe thunderstorms across Pennsylvania and New Jersey during the afternoon and the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second severe thunderstorm of the day tore down additional trees, tree limbs and power lines from Lehigh Township southeast through Nazareth Township. Scattered power outages in the county lasted through the morning of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air aloft coupled with daytime heating helped develop strong to locally severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm also dropped penny size hail in Morgantown (Caernarvon Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex /MCS/ produced isolated wind damage and torrential rainfall across western and central Pennsylvania during the late evening and early overnight hours. An individual cell embedded within the convective system produced an EF1 tornado in eastern Centre county. This event continued into the early morning hours on August 8, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clinton county law enforcement reported trees down along Route 120 near Shintown or approximately 3 miles southwest of Renovo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex /MCS/ produced isolated wind damage and torrential rainfall across western and central Pennsylvania during the late evening and early overnight hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air aloft coupled with daytime heating helped develop strong to locally severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires across lower Bucks County across Middletown, Langhorne and Lower Southampton Townships. In Lower Southampton Township, one tree crashed through the roof and into the master bedroom of a home. A grandmother pulled her grandson off the bed just as the tree came crashing through. The grandson just suffered a cut on his hand. In Middletown Township, healthy trees were snapped and struck three moving vehicles on Woodburne Road (Pennsylvania State Route 213). No injuries were reported. A skylight and several windows were broken at nearby Oxford Valley Mall. Several roads in the township were closed because of downed trees. Several mature trees were also snapped in Langhorne Township. Penny to nickel size hail also fell from the severe thunderstorm in Middletown and Langhorne. About 12,000 homes and businesses lost power in Bucks County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass helped trigger thunderstorms with torrential downpours. This caused isolated flash flooding in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos with the worst reported flooding in eastern Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding of creeks in the Saucon Creek Basin in Upper Saucon Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3 inches. Flooding also occurred along Pennsylvania State Route 309.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the hottest and most humid air masses of the summer affected the southeastern Pennsylvania on the 7th and most of eastern Pennsylvania on the 8th. Highest temperatures were well into the 90s in most areas. The 8th was the hottest day. In addition, dew point temperatures, especially on the 8th were as high as the mid to upper 70s. This produced afternoon heat indices as high as around 105F on the 8th.  ||Highest temperatures included 97 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and New Hope (Bucks County), 96 degrees in Reading (Berks County) and Doylestown and Perkasie (Bucks County), 95 degrees in Easton (Northampton County) and Willow Grove (Montgomery County) and 94 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Pottstown (Montgomery County) and West Chester (Chester County).||A cold front moved through the state on the night of the 8th. While there were some high temperatures in the lower 90s around Philadelphia on the 9th humidity levels were much lower. It was feared that if the excessive heat lasted one day longer, heat related deaths would have started in urbanized areas.

One downed tree pulled down wires down onto a vehicle, but no injuries were reported. The three severe thunderstorms did cause scattered power outages in eastern Monroe County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind shear, abnormally warm temperatures, and high humidity for late September combined to produce severe thunderstorms - and one confirmed EF1 tornado near Tidioute, PA in Warren County.||Numerous reports of funnel clouds were received in real time as the event was on-going.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey confirmed EF1 tornado just northeast of Tidioute. Peak winds estimated at 90-100 MPH. ||The tornado touched down two miles northeast of Tidioute in southern Warren County...then tracked northeast along the Allegheny River for an additional three miles. Several hunting cabins and uninhabited dwellings were damaged. Hundreds of trees were toppled or damaged. Several eyewitnesses saw the descending funnel cloud in the vicinity of Tidioute and one reported seeing the tornado on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms initiated in an unseasonably warm and humid airmass ahead of a cold front during the afternoon and evening hours. Severe weather was confined only to Lycoming County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lycoming County Law enforcement reported trees down along Route 44 near Jersey Shore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably dry June and then July in the local area led to the declaration of a drought watch in East Stroudsburg on July 3rd. This drought watch was upgraded to a drought warning (imposed watering restrictions) on July 26th.

A cold front encountered a very warm, humid and unstable airmass during the mid to late afternoon and early evening hours...igniting numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across Central Pennsylvania.  Severe thunderstorms produced widespread wind damage and as well as a few reports of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lancaster county emergency management reported trees and wires down across portions of Rapho and East Donegal Townships. ||Multiple downbursts produced straight line wind damage (wind gusts of 60-80mph) to farm buildings and at least two homes just east of Route 772 in Rapho Township.||Two silos on Longenecker and Pinkerton roads were damaged. The domed roof of a silo on Pinkerton Road was ripped off during the storm, while a silo on Longenecker Road lost 20 to 30 feet from its upper half. Several outbuildings were also damaged along with corn fields. ||Also on Pinkerton Road, a tree fell onto the roof of a home, causing exterior and interior damage.||No injuries with the storms. More than 7,000 power outages (largely due to lightning strikes) were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind shear, abnormally warm temperatures, and high humidity for late September combined to produce severe thunderstorms - and one confirmed EF1 tornado near Tidioute, PA in Warren County.||Numerous reports of funnel clouds were received in real time as the event was on-going.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf-ball sized hail reported in Tidioute by Warren County Emergency Management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate mesoscale convective systems moved across Southeast Ohio, Western Pennsylvania, Northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF0 tornado occurred during the event. The tornado touched down in the West End of Pittsburgh. Significant flash flooding occurred in Millvale in Allegheny county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Survey found and EF0 tornado touched down in the West End area of Pittsburgh affecting areas along Toledo, Municipal, and Greenway Streets. Numerous trees were snapped and power lines were down. There was some structural damage to a few homes and buildings. Maximum estimated winds were 85 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate mesoscale convective systems moved across Southeast Ohio, Western Pennsylvania, Northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF0 tornado occurred during the event. The tornado touched down in the West End of Pittsburgh. Significant flash flooding occurred in Millvale in Allegheny county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Millvale area and much of the North Hills area of Pittsburgh as Girtys Run came out of it's banks. Over 900 homes were affected by the flooding as well as over 200 businesses. Infrasturcture owned by local municipalities sustained over 4 million dollars in damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down. A tree was also down in Herrick Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some structural damage occurred to a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system dived southeast across Upstate New York and Northeast Pennsylvania, bringing showers and thunderstorms to the area during the afternoon. Scattered thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down county-wide with power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front encountered a warm...moist and unstable airmass over east-central Pennsylvania during the early afternoon. Thunderstorms developed rapidly and produced large hail ranging from nickel to baseball size and damaging winds. Large hail covered the ground in several locations. In addition, hail ripped siding off of homes, put holes in slate roofs, dented hundreds of cars and broke numerous windshields.  Lycoming county emergency management estimated between $300,000 to $400,000 dollars in hail damage. Although some storms exhibited strong rotational signatures and funnel clouds were sighted, no tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported golf ball to baseball sized hail in Muncy (report relayed through trained storm spotter).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front encountered a very warm, humid and unstable airmass during the mid to late afternoon and early evening hours...igniting numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across Central Pennsylvania.  Severe thunderstorms produced widespread wind damage and as well as a few reports of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported numerous trees and power lines down in Bradford. McKean county emergency management reported the storm packed winds upwards of 50 to 70 mph...toppling more than 100 trees and damaging close to 60 homes. Two trees fell onto parked vehicles. Pennsylvania Electric representatives said that there were 5,000 residents without power during the peak of the storm with nearly 200 customers without power for over 24 hours. A large tank inside the American Refining Group's Refinery was buckled due to strong winds and a roof was torn off a boiler house. ||County officials said that no major injuries occurred as a result of the storms. |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed in weakly forced and highly unstable environment during the mid to late afternoon hours. Storms produced pea to nickel sized hail, downed trees and dumped torrential rainfall which lead to some localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CO-OP observer reported pea to nickel sized hail almost covering ground. The hail lasted from 535 to 550 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass remained in place across Northeast Pennsylvania.  Scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms developed, and a few of these became severe.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of trees down in the metro area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down. One tree fell on wires and caused a fire and power loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid weather developed as a Bermuda high pressure system built into the Northeast United States. A cold front moved into the Great Lakes during the day on the 27th, with thunderstorms developing in the favorable environment across the area. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 27th, with some of the storms producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down county-wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved through eastern Canada and dragged a cold front through the northeast. Scattered convection developed along the front, producing large hail and damaging winds across Northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air aloft coupled with daytime heating helped develop strong to locally severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 12th.
Cold air aloft coupled with daytime heating helped develop strong to locally severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down around three dozen trees within the city and injured four people. A downed tree on West Oak Lane fell onto a vehicle and injured three people. A 55-year-old man was injured when his vehicle was crushed by a tree on Limekiln Pike. Downed wires in the Northern Liberty section of the city set a vehicle on fire. Bad weather in Philadelphia and other cities attempting to fly to and from the city caused hundreds of cancellations. About 750 people were forced to spend the night at the airport. PECO Energy reported about 30,000 homes and businesses in its southeastern Pennsylvania service area lost power because of the thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass helped trigger thunderstorms with torrential downpours. This caused isolated flash flooding in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos with the worst reported flooding in eastern Monroe County.

Several roads were closed in Stroudsburg, East Stroudsburg, Stroud and Smithfield Townships. There were a few water rescues along Pennsylvania State Route 611. A mudslide was also reported in Smithfield Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3 to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first heat wave of the season (loosely defined as three consecutive days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher) occurred across most of southeastern Pennsylvania from the 26th through the 28th. The hottest day with respect to both temperature and humidity occurred on the 27th as many high temperatures reached into the mid 90s and heat indices were as high as around 100F. A cold front that cleared the region on the 28th brought cooler weather into the area for the end of the month.||Highest temperatures included 95 degrees in Philadelphia, 94 degrees in Reading (Berks County) and West Chester (Chester County), 93 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Doylestown (Bucks County) and 92 degrees in Easton (Northampton County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mobile home trailer on an elevated platform at the top of hill in a field was demolished by a 60 mph wind gust. Debris from trailer was scattered across the field and blocked by nearby rows of corn. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense straight-line thunderstorm winds estimated between 95 and 100 mph produced significant tree damage. The damage was mainly uprooted and snapped healthy red oak trees 5 to 10 inches in diameter. Many of these trees fell on power lines and homes in the Paupack and Hawley areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three people were struck by lightning in Hamlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 96-year-old man died from the heat in Philadelphia. His body was discovered by police officers on the afternoon of the 15th. The air conditioner the gentleman had broke down during the heat wave of the previous week. Officers noted that his apartment felt hotter than it was outdoors. The high temperature that afternoon in Philadelphia was 92 degrees. This was one of the only two (and the last one) heat related deaths in the city of Philadelphia during the summer of 2007.

Thunderstorms that formed and continued to reform along a warm frontal boundary produced torrential downpours in the Lehigh Valley shortly after Midnight EDT on the 5th. This caused flooding of roadways and small streams in central parts of Lehigh County and southern parts of Northampton County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 4 to 5 inches from South Whitehall Township (Lehigh County) east through Allentown, Bethlehem and Lower Saucon Township (Northampton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of roadways and small streams occurred in central Lehigh County. Roadways were closed due to flooding in South Whitehall and Hanover Townships. The storm total at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was 2.30 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that formed and continued to reform along a warm frontal boundary produced torrential downpours in the Lehigh Valley shortly after Midnight EDT on the 5th. This caused flooding of roadways and small streams in central parts of Lehigh County and southern parts of Northampton County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 4 to 5 inches from South Whitehall Township (Lehigh County) east through Allentown, Bethlehem and Lower Saucon Township (Northampton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused flooding of roadways and small streams in southern parts of Northampton County and affected Bethlehem and Lower Saucon Township the most. In Bethlehem, flooding along the Monocacy Creek inundated historical buildings in the Colonial Industrial Quarter. The flooding was exacerbated by debris that accumulated under a nearby bridge which acted like a dam. Four feet of water and mud accumulated in the 1776 Tannery and 1762 Waterworks. Old wooden pieces of Luckenbach Mill were also damaged. The flooding overturned tannery benches and furniture and literally knocked a few doors of their hinges. The water damaged mortar and plaster walls. Chunks of blacktop were also ripped from the parking lot. The event total at Lehigh Valley International Airport was 2.30 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in a warm and humid airmass as an upper level trough passed through the region. Cooling temperatures aloft caused isolated large hail.

A weak upper air disturbance combined with an unseasonably hot and humid air mass to trigger pulse type thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 10th in eastern Pennsylvania. In addition to being prolific cloud-to-ground lightning producers, a few thunderstorms also became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes across Northampton County caused damage to a few structures. In Bethlehem Township, a house burst into flames after lightning struck a chimney and traveled downward to the gas fireplace. Four fire companies battled the blaze. A firefighter was unhurt after he fell through a ceiling. In Wilson Borough, lightning struck and obliterated a chimney. The debris damaged a minivan parked on Lehigh Street. In Palmer Township, a lightning strike damaged a shed. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger a severe thunderstorm in Berks County during the evening of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm damaged the second and third floors as well as the roof of a toothbrush manufacturing plant in Kutztown. The roof and part of the third story wall of the plant were partially knocked down. The main stairwell in the plant was also destroyed. Elsewhere a Chinese Restaurant in the borough lost its display sign. Several trees and lines were knocked down on and around Main Street. Tree debris covered one playground set. About 5,000 homes and businesses in the area lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper air disturbance combined with an unseasonably hot and humid air mass to trigger pulse type severe thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 10th in eastern Pennsylvania. In addition to the severe weather, the thunderstorms were prolific cloud-to-ground lightning producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 59 mph was measured at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny size hail in Scottdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the onshore circulation around a high pressure system over New England and the Canadian Maritimes, fresh water runoff from heavy rain inland and spring tides associated with the full moon caused widespread minor tidal flooding during the afternoon high tide on the 27th  on the Delaware River and tidal sections of its tributaries. The high tide In Philadelphia reached 8.73 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water. An offshore flow developed later in the day on the 27th and prevented further tidal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Northeast United States, bringing abundant sunshine and unusually warm temperatures. High temperature records were broken for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperature records were broken for several days at the airport in Avoca. On October 4th, the high of 85 degrees broke the old record of 83 set in 1959. On the 6th, the high of 86 broke the record high of 84 also set in 1959. The high of 87 on October 8th broke the previous record of 78 set in 1990. Finally, the high temperature of 84 degrees on the 9th broke the old record of 80 set in 1997.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an unseasonably dry September, The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection added all of the Eastern Pennsylvania Counties (and kept Monroe County) to the drought watch on October 5th. A drought watch calls for a 5 percent voluntary reduction in non-essential water usage. Groundwater levels throughout the state were showing stress. Surface water and reservoir supplies were also in decline. There were some record low stream flows. The Department of Environmental Protection recommended residents to check for leaks, install low flow plumbing, take showers instead of baths, replace older appliances with more efficient new ones, run the dishwasher and washing machine at full loads, keep cold water in the refrigerator, water plants either early in the day or late at night and to use a broom instead of water to clean driveways, steps and sidewalks.||Locally a drought emergency was in effect for East Stroudsburg (Monroe County) as of October 2nd. Water levels dropped a foot and a half at the borough's reservoir within a week. Residents were now required to cut back on all non-essential water use including lawn watering, car washing and irrigating plants. Sellersville Borough (Bucks County) declared a drought warning on October 8th and asked residents to voluntarily reduce water usage by 10 percent. There was also a ban on washing cars, watering yards and serving water (without being asked first) in restaurants. Buckingham Township (Bucks County) asked Furlong residents to reduce the amount of water usage as well replacement was not exceeding usage. A couple of homeowners' wells ran dry in the Poconos. A burning ban is in effect in Franconia Township (Montgomery County).  ||The Jordan Creek in South Whitehall Township (Lehigh County) ran dry.  As of October 5th, seventy-six percent of the farming topsoil in the state was considered short or very short of moisture. The dryness overall arrived late enough to not be a problem for most Pennsylvania farms. Dryness was preferred for the pumpkin crop which was expected to be excellent.||October 2007 was the first month since April 2007 that brought widespread above normal precipitation to the area.

A slow moving weather pattern gave eastern Pennsylvania four days of rainy weather which culminated with periods of heavy rain from the afternoon of the 26th into the morning of the 27th. Four day storm totals averaged 2.5 to 5 inches across most the area with the highest amounts across the Philadelphia northwest suburbs. This caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some of the smaller creeks in and around the local Philadelphia area. Specific storm totals included 5.74 inches in Norristown (Montgomery County), 4.76 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County), 4.11 inches in West Chester (Chester County), 3.73 inches in Chadds Ford  (Delaware County), 3.33 inches in Walnutport (Northampton County), 3.20 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.00 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 2.97 inches in Reading (Berks County), 2.93 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 2.86 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.   ||The heavy rain was caused by a long and continuous fetch of tropical moisture between a slow moving upper level low from the south central part of the United States and a blocking ridge over the western Atlantic. The closed system lifted into the Great Lakes on Saturday the 27th and permitted a cold front to move through the region that day and shut off the moisture connection from the tropics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth with the Schuylkill River was above its 5 foot flood stage from 1115 a.m. EDT through 908 p.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 5.63 feet at 645 p.m. EDT. Storm totals within Philadelphia since precipitation started falling on Wednesday the 24th included 3.06 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 2.83 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an unseasonably dry September, The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection added all of the Eastern Pennsylvania Counties (and kept Monroe County) to the drought watch on October 5th. A drought watch calls for a 5 percent voluntary reduction in non-essential water usage. Groundwater levels throughout the state were showing stress. Surface water and reservoir supplies were also in decline. There were some record low stream flows. The Department of Environmental Protection recommended residents to check for leaks, install low flow plumbing, take showers instead of baths, replace older appliances with more efficient new ones, run the dishwasher and washing machine at full loads, keep cold water in the refrigerator, water plants either early in the day or late at night and to use a broom instead of water to clean driveways, steps and sidewalks.||Locally a drought emergency was in effect for East Stroudsburg (Monroe County) as of October 2nd. Water levels dropped a foot and a half at the borough's reservoir within a week. Residents were now required to cut back on all non-essential water use including lawn watering, car washing and irrigating plants. Sellersville Borough (Bucks County) declared a drought warning on October 8th and asked residents to voluntarily reduce water usage by 10 percent. There was also a ban on washing cars, watering yards and serving water (without being asked first) in restaurants. Buckingham Township (Bucks County) asked Furlong residents to reduce the amount of water usage as well replacement was not exceeding usage. A couple of homeowners' wells ran dry in the Poconos. A burning ban is in effect in Franconia Township (Montgomery County).  ||The Jordan Creek in South Whitehall Township (Lehigh County) ran dry.  As of October 5th, seventy-six percent of the farming topsoil in the state was considered short or very short of moisture. The dryness overall arrived late enough to not be a problem for most Pennsylvania farms. Dryness was preferred for the pumpkin crop which was expected to be excellent.||October 2007 was the first month since April 2007 that brought widespread above normal precipitation to the area.

Scattered severe thunderstorms developed over Western Pennsylvania and Northern West Virginia ahead of a strong cold front moving east from the Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and associated cold air aloft combined with marginal surface based instability to produce scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strike destroyed chimney on a 2 story single family dwelling. Report received by PEMA via the York county 911 center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather occurred on this day. Thunderstorms initiated in a warm and humid airmass ahead of a strong cold front moving eastward through the Ohio Valley. One storm produced and EF-0 tornado between Muncy and Hughesville in Lycoming county. In addition, a 24-year-old male was struck by lightning and killed in his driveway in York county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey Team confirmed EF-0 Tornado embedded in larger area of downburst wind damage between Muncy and Hughesville. Dozens of trees were knocked down along with minor damage to structures. Average path width was 50 to 70 yards with an approximate length of 1 mile. Peak winds were estimated to be between 65 to 70 mph. There were no injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex /MCS/ produced isolated wind damage and torrential rainfall across western and central Pennsylvania during the late evening and early overnight hours. An individual cell embedded within the convective system produced an EF1 tornado in eastern Centre county. This event continued into the early morning hours on August 8, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF1 tornado confirmed near Woodward in eastern Centre county. Peak winds estimated between 85-95mph. Two homes and one cabin sustained minor damage. Approximately 200 trees were either uprooted or significantly damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave brought unseasonably hot weather to most of Eastern Pennsylvania on July 8th through the 10th. The highest temperatures were on the 9th and 10th and were mostly in the mid to upper 90s. The combination of the heat and humidity produced afternoon heat indices of around 100F both afternoons. Cloudiness that preceded a cold front on the 11th prevented most places from reaching 90F again. There was one heat caused death in Philadelphia: a 71-year-old man was found dead in a vehicle in North Philadelphia. The issuance of an excessive heat warning activated special summer heat programs within the city of Philadelphia. The Philadelphia Corporation for Aging was operating a heatline service for seniors.  ||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County), 97 degrees at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 96 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, Easton (Northampton County) and West Chester (Chester County), 95 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International, Sellersville (Bucks County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 94 degrees in Reading and Hamburg (Berks County), Willow Grove (Montgomery County), 93 degrees in Southampton (Bucks County) and 92 degrees in Perkasie (Bucks County).

A weak upper air disturbance combined with an unseasonably hot and humid air mass to trigger pulse type severe thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 10th in eastern Pennsylvania. In addition to the severe weather, the thunderstorms were prolific cloud-to-ground lightning producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused widespread tree damage across east central Northampton County and affected the city of Easton, West Easton Borough, Wilson Borough and Forks and Palmer Townships the hardest. Dozens of roadways were closed because of downed trees. About 22,500 homes and businesses lost power in the county and it took until the evening of the 12th for power to be fully restored. ||Wilson Borough declared a borough disaster because about three-quarters of the borough lost power. Several homes and vehicles were struck by downed trees. Many trees were knocked down on Washington Boulevard. One roadway remained closed until the 13th. In Easton, the College Hill section of the city was hit the hardest. A large tree crashed onto a home on Cattell Street. Another home had a tree limb go through its roof. At least two vehicles were damaged by downed trees. One street light was damaged by a downed tree. Two large trees (at least 80 feet tall) were knocked down in Hugh Moore Park. In Forks Township, many properties suffered some tree damage. The worst reported damage occurred on Old Mill Road where the carport of one home was uprooted, thrown across power lines and split, then landed onto and badly damaged an above ground pool. Power lines were also bent on Old Mill Road. On Richmond Avenue, a chimney and a garage roof were damaged by the winds. One tree also fell onto a home. Another fell onto a vehicle. Damage in West Easton and Palmer Township was mainly just to the trees. The clean-up was expected to take weeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate mesoscale convective systems moved across Southeast Ohio, Western Pennsylvania, Northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF0 tornado occurred during the event. The tornado touched down in the West End of Pittsburgh. Significant flash flooding occurred in Millvale in Allegheny county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flash flooding along Route 51 between the Aliquippa bridge and Monaca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure was located in southeastern Canada during the early morning hours of August 8th. A cold front associated with this area of low pressure was moving across the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley regions during the morning. Well out ahead of this cold front was a surface trough which moved through the Mid Atlantic region during the very early morning hours on August 8th. A very warm and humid air mass was in place, and with some upper-level energy, a round of showers and thunderstorms moved through parts of eastern Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey and produced wind and lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and the ensuing fire destroyed a mobile home in Moore Township. The homeowner was seriously injured. The home was located on Jay Lane within the Manufactured Homes Retirement Community on Pennsylvania State Route 512. Damage to the home was estimated at $55,000.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave brought unseasonably hot weather to most of Eastern Pennsylvania on July 8th through the 10th. The highest temperatures were on the 9th and 10th and were mostly in the mid to upper 90s. The combination of the heat and humidity produced afternoon heat indices of around 100F both afternoons. Cloudiness that preceded a cold front on the 11th prevented most places from reaching 90F again. There was one heat caused death in Philadelphia: a 71-year-old man was found dead in a vehicle in North Philadelphia. The issuance of an excessive heat warning activated special summer heat programs within the city of Philadelphia. The Philadelphia Corporation for Aging was operating a heatline service for seniors.  ||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County), 97 degrees at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 96 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, Easton (Northampton County) and West Chester (Chester County), 95 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International, Sellersville (Bucks County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 94 degrees in Reading and Hamburg (Berks County), Willow Grove (Montgomery County), 93 degrees in Southampton (Bucks County) and 92 degrees in Perkasie (Bucks County).

A cold front that was approaching northern Pennsylvania from the Saint Lawrence River Valley initiated severe thunderstorms northwest of the state. These storms maintained their severity as they moved through the Poconos during the evening of the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell near the Pocono Raceway in Tunkhannock Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the morning of August 25th. A cold front associated with this low pressure system was located in the Ohio Valley. A hot and especially humid airmass was found ahead of this front across the Middle Atlantic region on August 25th. As the front approached, showers and thunderstorms developed and several of the storms became severe. The actual cold front however did not clear the region until the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm rumbled through Mahoning Township, which produced strong winds that downed trees and power lines at 7:55 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection issued a drought watch for fifty-eight Pennsylvania counties on August 6th. This included Carbon and Monroe Counties in the Poconos. Precipitation deficits continued through July and early August and groundwater levels were falling. A drought watch calls for a 5 percent voluntary reduction in non-essential water usage. Locally a drought warning remained in effect for East Stroudsburg (Monroe County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave brought unseasonably hot weather to most of Eastern Pennsylvania on July 8th through the 10th. The highest temperatures were on the 9th and 10th and were mostly in the mid to upper 90s. The combination of the heat and humidity produced afternoon heat indices of around 100F both afternoons. Cloudiness that preceded a cold front on the 11th prevented most places from reaching 90F again. There was one heat caused death in Philadelphia: a 71-year-old man was found dead in a vehicle in North Philadelphia. The issuance of an excessive heat warning activated special summer heat programs within the city of Philadelphia. The Philadelphia Corporation for Aging was operating a heatline service for seniors.  ||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County), 97 degrees at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 96 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, Easton (Northampton County) and West Chester (Chester County), 95 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International, Sellersville (Bucks County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 94 degrees in Reading and Hamburg (Berks County), Willow Grove (Montgomery County), 93 degrees in Southampton (Bucks County) and 92 degrees in Perkasie (Bucks County).

An area of low pressure moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the morning of August 25th. A cold front associated with this low pressure system was located in the Ohio Valley. A hot and especially humid airmass was found ahead of this front across the Middle Atlantic region on August 25th. As the front approached, showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across the area. The actual cold front however did not clear the region until the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent cloud to ground lightning associated with a small cluster of thunderstorms rolled across Northampton County during the evening of the 25th. Lightning struck the steeple of the Great Shepard Christian Fellowship Church in Plainfield Township at 9:50 PM EDT. This resulted in a fire, which caused an estimated $250,000 in damage. In addition, lightning struck a saw mill in Upper Mount Bethel Township, sparking a fire at about 10:15 PM EDT. The saw mill was destroyed, which resulted in $1 million dollars in loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stretched from west to east across the region during the early morning hours of the 7th. This frontal boundary was associated with an area of low pressure centered over the plains states. Through the day on the 7th, this boundary moved north of the area allowing a warm and humid airmass to set up over the region. Thunderstorms were able to develop across the area during the late morning hours of the 7th, and again once the warmer and more humid airmass set up across the area during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down.

Showers and thunderstorms developed across upstate New York and northeast Pennsylvania ahead of a slow moving cold front that extended from southern Ontario to lower Michigan. The airmass ahead of this front was warm and unstable, and with the aid of an upper level disturbance, severe thunderstorms developed over the area. Some of the storms produced very large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees were blown down throughout Wayne County including in the Hawley area.

The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection removed Carbon County from the drought watch on September 5th as groundwater levels improved. The drought watch continued for Monroe County. September overall was a dry month across eastern Pennsylvania. County weighted monthly rainfall averages ranged from 0.4 inches in Chester and Montgomery Counties to 1.8 inches in Carbon County. Normal is around 4.0 inches. The greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area had less than 75 percent of its normal rainfall. At the Philadelphia International Airport, it was the sixth driest September on record. The rainfall total was 0.58 inches. Normal is 3.88 inches. At the Lehigh Valley International Airport, it was the third driest September on record. The monthly rainfall total was 0.57 inches. Normal is 4.37 inches. Other monthly precipitation totals included 0.98 inches in Reading (normal is 4.36 inches) and 2.31 inches in Mount Pocono (normal is 4.98 inches). | |A drought watch calls for a 5 percent voluntary reduction in non-essential water usage. Locally a drought warning remained in effect for East Stroudsburg (Monroe County). The one crop that was not expected to be affected by the drought was pumpkins.

A cold front moved through the area during the afternoon and evening hours of the 8th. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the cold front, and some of the thunderstorms became severe. Flooding conditions also occurred as the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall flooded an underpass in less than ten minutes. The underpass was closed as floodwaters rose to a depth of five feet. A creek nearby left its banks and helped flood the underpass so quickly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Poconos were on the northwest fringe of the precipitation shield associated with a coastal low pressure system during the night of the 26th. They were also along the southern fringe of precipitation associated with a low pressure system that was approaching from the west. The higher terrain locations failed to rise above freezing as shots of light precipitation moved through. While it was warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain, it froze on contact across some of the higher terrain locations in the Poconos. Since precipitation amounts were light, ice accretions were mostly just a trace. But, untreated walkways and roadways were slippery.  This same system produced sleet during the evening of the 26th across Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Philadelphia northwest suburbs. Temperatures in those areas were above freezing.

A cold front approaching from the West interacted with a warm and humid air mass provoking the development of showers and thunderstorms across the region.  Several storms became severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported by a public observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense area of low pressure on the 1st moved into the Upper Midwest with a cold front stretched southward across the Ohio Valley all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. Meanwhile, another cold front moved through the Northeast and settled into the Mid Atlantic region. This boundary remained stationary for a time during the overnight hours of the 1st, as the strong low pressure system moved into the western Great Lakes. Gradually an occluded front moved through the area during the morning hours of the 2nd, as a weak area of low pressure developed over eastern Pennsylvania. Along and ahead of this boundary and weak area of low pressure, an abundance of moisture was brought northward across our area. This lead to 1 to 3 inches of rain late on the 1st into the 2nd, and when combined with melting piles of snow in some areas, mainly minor flooding of streams, creeks and some roads resulted across eastern Pennsylvania. Some of the flooding closed several roads in the region.|The rain began between 7:45 PM and 9:00 PM EST on the 1st and became heavy at times, then tapered off generally between 9:00 AM and 10:00 AM EST on the 2nd. Some rainfall totals include, 3.65 inches in New Garden Township (Chester County); 2.50 inches in Fricks (Bucks County); 2.25 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County); 2.21 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County); 2.20 inches in Exton, Franklin and Honey Brook (Chester County); 2.13 inches in Pine Valley (Philadelphia County); 2.10 inches in Southampton (Bucks County); 2.00 inches on Martins Creek (Northampton County); 1.89 inches in Chadds Ford (Delaware County); 1.82 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport (Philadelphia County); 1.73 inches at Blue Marsh Lake (Berks County); 1.70 inches in Springfield (Delaware County); 1.67 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County); 1.62 inches in Palm (Montgomery County); 1.52 inches in Hamburg and at the Reading Airport (Berks County); 1.50 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport near Allenton (Lehigh County); 1.46 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County); 1.45 inches in Blakeslee (Monroe County); 1.43 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport (Philadelphia County); 1.40 inches at the Mount Pocono Airport (Monroe County); 1.40 inches in Beltzville Lake (Carbon County); 1.38 inches in Green Lane (Montgomery County); and 0.93 inches in Stoddartsville (Monroe County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rising water levels on the 2nd in Bushkill Township pushed water, which threatened the signs at Bushkill Park. In addition, a creek overflowed and flooded the intersection of Sullivan Trail and Newlins Road West in Forks Township, making the road all but impassable. A vehicle was floating in the flood waters, however no injuries were reported. Also in Forks Township, water covered Kesslersville Road near Hunter Street following heavy rain late on the 1st and into the 2nd. Easton Avenue at Willow Park Road in Bethlehem Township, Van Buren Road near Nazareth Road in Palmer Township and Route 512 between Hanoverville Road and Jacksonville Road in East Allen Township were some of the most trouble spots. At some points, cars were flooded out as waters rose 2 feet high. In Bethlehem, the Monocacy and Saucon Creeks overflowed and swamped some streets and city parks. The Saucon Park in south Bethlehem was covered by water anywhere from a foot and a half to 3 feet deep. The Monacacy also spilled over into the Colonial Industrial Quarter near Main Street, and rougher waters upstream may have damaged pavilions at Monocacy Park near Illick���s Mill. A few Bethlehem side streets also flooded in the usual, low-lying areas. Elizabeth Avenue was the only major road affected. Flooding along the Monocacy closed one lane of the road at the Paint Mill Bridge. A car was submerged on Route 512 just north of Locust Road in East Allen Township early on the 2nd. Rescuers later learned that the female motorist escaped the car.|In Bethlehem Township, the portion of Green Pond Road between Farmersville and Country Club roads remained closed on the 6th due to lingering flooding. The Monocacy Creek at Bethlehem went above the flood of 4.5 feet at 6:43 AM EST, crested at 5.69 feet at 2:45 PM EST and dropped below flood stage at 6:54 PM EST (all on the 2nd).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather occurred on this day. Thunderstorms initiated in a warm and humid airmass ahead of a strong cold front moving eastward through the Ohio Valley. One storm produced and EF-0 tornado between Muncy and Hughesville in Lycoming county. In addition, a 24-year-old male was struck by lightning and killed in his driveway in York county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clearfield county 911 center reported multiple trees down along Route 879 from Lecontes Mills to Karthaus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid airmass was over the Mid Atlantic region during the 19th. A strong area of low pressure moved into southeastern Canada during the afternoon, and a strong cold front associated with it approached and moved through the Mid Atlantic and Northeast in the evening. As a result, numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms developed. There was plenty of wind energy above the surface, therefore some of the isolated thunderstorms and even heavier showers produced strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several large tree limbs in northern Berks  County in and around Albany and Windsor Townships.
