Significant flash flooding occurred in Millvale in Allegheny county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported Sandt Creek out of its banks flooding portions of Rodi road. Numerous basements with up to three feet of water in them from poor drainage flooding around 3rd street in Penn Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual moisture behind a passing cold front provided enough instability across southern New York and northeast Pennsylvania for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual moisture behind a passing cold front provided enough instability across southern New York and northeast Pennsylvania for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Silver Lake Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much like on 12th, increasing low level instability below a relative minimum in temperatures aloft created a rapid destabilization of the atmosphere during the afternoon and evening hours on June 13, 2007. The storm motion was northeast to southwest, which is unusual for summertime convection in Central PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported numerous large trees down approximately 2 miles east-northeast of Loysville in Elliotsburg Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much like on 12th, increasing low level instability below a relative minimum in temperatures aloft created a rapid destabilization of the atmosphere during the afternoon and evening hours on June 13, 2007. The storm motion was northeast to southwest, which is unusual for summertime convection in Central PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Schuylkill county 911 center reported numerous wires and large trees down in Deer Lake and Penn Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 8th. Numerous severe thunderstorms developed ahead and along this cold front. Severe weather occurred throughout northern Ohio, and the activity continued into northwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down from Fallowfield to Vernon, just south of Conneaut Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching warm front helped produce severe thunderstorms and also caused some lightning damage across Eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few large tree limbs and wires in and around Pocopson Township in Chester County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front caused showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region. Many storms became severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down in the area by law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense squall line produced widespread wind damage across Central Pennsylvania during the evening hours on June 8, 2007. Numerous trees and power lines were downed causing several power outages and other sporadic damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported several trees and wires down in Altoona, Duncansville and Hollidaysburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense squall line produced widespread wind damage across Central Pennsylvania during the evening hours on June 8, 2007. Numerous trees and power lines were downed causing several power outages and other sporadic damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Juniata county Emergency Manager reported numerous wires and trees down in McAlisterville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense squall line produced widespread wind damage across Central Pennsylvania during the evening hours on June 8, 2007. Numerous trees and power lines were downed causing several power outages and other sporadic damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported several trees and wires down in Loysville and Ickesburg Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving southeast from the Great lakes produced widespread severe weather across Southeast Ohio, Western Pennsylvania, and Northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and power lines down in New Bedford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked northeast across the Great Lakes into Hudson Bay. A strong southwest flow developed ahead of the associated cold front and brought a warm and humid airmass across New York and Pennsylvania in advance of the front. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front...producing hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were blown down throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed along a slow moving cold front across Western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced severe weather across portions of Western Pennsylvania, Southeast Ohio, and Northern West Virginia. As low pressure slowly moved east of the region, heavy rains with some snow melt then caused flooding of some streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams and creeks flooded and roads were closed due to flooding across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to Eastern Pennsylvania on the 15th and strong to high winds as well as some snow to the region on the 16th. Numerous streams and rivers flooded, but the flooding along the Delaware River was minimal. Many roadways were closed due to flooding. Many basements were also flooded. There were a small number of mainly voluntary evacuations. About 25 major roadways around the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area were closed due to flooding.|Rain began falling throughout the area during the late evening on the 14th. It started briefly as snow over some of the higher terrain of the Poconos. The rain fell at its heaviest across the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th into the evening. Precipitation changed to snow over the higher terrain shortly after midnight EDT on the 16th while a mixture of rain and snow (during heavier bursts) continued in the valleys through the morning. Precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening. Elsewhere across Eastern Pennsylvania, the rain fell at its heaviest from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th to about 3 a.m. EDT on the 16th. As the upper air disturbance moved across southeast Pennsylvania, the rain did change to snow for a couple of hours around sunrise on the 16th and accumulated about an inch or two on grassy areas. The steady precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening on the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 6 inches. Event snowfall totals were an inch or two in most areas, except for 3 to 6 inches in the higher terrain of the Poconos. |The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. The combination of the heavy rain, even some snow and the winds helped knock down numerous trees and power lines. PECO Energy reported about 80,000 of its southeastern Pennsylvania customers lost power. More than 50 major roads were closed in southeastern Pennsylvania because of downed trees. also in Southeastern Pennsylvani, five SEPTA Regional Rail Service Lines had service interruptions on the 16th. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 77,000 of its customers lost power in Eastern Pennsylvania and about 65,000 homes and businesses lost power in the Lehigh Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph.|The combination of the snow, wind (power outages) and flooding (more so in the local Philadelphia area) caused many school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings on the 16th. The nor'easter was so nasty that even the Internal Revenue Service extended the tax deadline for 48 hours.  |The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding twice along the Manatawny Creek on the 15th and 16th. The Manatawny Creek at Spangville was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1102 am EDT on the 15th through 902 pm EDT on the 15th.  It crested at 6.39 feet at 215 pm EDT on the 15th.  Additional heavy rain caused the creek to flood the following morning.  The Manatawny Creek at Spangville again was above its 6 foot flood stage from 540 am EDT on the 16th through 130 pm EDT on the 16th.  It crested at 6.14 feet at 745 am EDT on the 16th. Precipitation totals included 4.58 inches in Hamburg, 3.70 inches in Scarlet���s Mill, 3.03 inches in Fleetwood and 2.69 inches at the Reading Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool pool aloft associated with a large upper level low pressure system located off of the New England coast combined with strong mid-June sunshine to create a favorable environment for thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours on June 12, 2007. A compact upper air disturbance aided in providing large scale ascent as it rotated around the periphery of the upper level vortex . The storm motion was from the northeast to the southwest, which is unusual for summertime convection in Central PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported trees down on wires 4 miles south of Herndon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense area of low pressure on the 1st moved into the Upper Midwest with a cold front stretched southward across the Ohio Valley all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. Meanwhile, another cold front moved through the Northeast and settled into the Mid Atlantic region. This boundary remained stationary for a time during the overnight hours of the 1st, as the strong low pressure system moved into the western Great Lakes. Gradually an occluded front moved through the area during the morning hours of the 2nd, as a weak area of low pressure developed over eastern Pennsylvania. Along and ahead of this boundary and weak area of low pressure, an abundance of moisture was brought northward across our area. This lead to 1 to 3 inches of rain late on the 1st into the 2nd, and when combined with melting piles of snow in some areas, mainly minor flooding of streams, creeks and some roads resulted across eastern Pennsylvania. Some of the flooding closed several roads in the region.|The rain began between 7:45 PM and 9:00 PM EST on the 1st and became heavy at times, then tapered off generally between 9:00 AM and 10:00 AM EST on the 2nd. Some rainfall totals include, 3.65 inches in New Garden Township (Chester County); 2.50 inches in Fricks (Bucks County); 2.25 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County); 2.21 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County); 2.20 inches in Exton, Franklin and Honey Brook (Chester County); 2.13 inches in Pine Valley (Philadelphia County); 2.10 inches in Southampton (Bucks County); 2.00 inches on Martins Creek (Northampton County); 1.89 inches in Chadds Ford (Delaware County); 1.82 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport (Philadelphia County); 1.73 inches at Blue Marsh Lake (Berks County); 1.70 inches in Springfield (Delaware County); 1.67 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County); 1.62 inches in Palm (Montgomery County); 1.52 inches in Hamburg and at the Reading Airport (Berks County); 1.50 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport near Allenton (Lehigh County); 1.46 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County); 1.45 inches in Blakeslee (Monroe County); 1.43 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport (Philadelphia County); 1.40 inches at the Mount Pocono Airport (Monroe County); 1.40 inches in Beltzville Lake (Carbon County); 1.38 inches in Green Lane (Montgomery County); and 0.93 inches in Stoddartsville (Monroe County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Perkiomen Creek, which closed at least nine roads early on the 2nd. The Perkiomen Creek at East Greenville rose above its flood stage of 4.0 feet at 5:19 AM EST, crested at 5.48 feet at 9:45 AM EST and then dropped below its flood stage at 2:10 PM EST. The Wissahickon Creek at Fort Washington surpassed its flood stage of 9.0 feet at 7:25 AM EST, crested at 9.50 feet at 9:00 AM EST and then dropped below flood stage at 10:12 AM EST. The Manatawny Creek at Pottstown rose above its flood stage of 6.0 feet at 6:25 AM EST, crested at 7.48 feet at 2:15 PM EST and then dropped below flood stage at 7:12 PM EST. The East Branch Perkiomen at Schwenksville rose above its flood stage of 7.0 feet at 7:13 AM EST, crested at 7.82 feet at 11:30 AM EST and then dropped below flood stage at 2:18 PM EST. (all times on the 2nd).
